     2→
     3→> TA is NOT chain-only. It is a general-purpose prediction framework with domain-specific instances.
     4→

    10→
    11→Technical analysis (TA) originated as a financial discipline — chart patterns, moving averages, momentum oscillators applied to price data. In Roko, TA is generalized into a set of **universal oracle primitives**: prediction, evaluation, calibration, and feedback loops that operate identically across any domain where an agent interacts with a verifiable external system.
    12→
    13→The core insight: code, markets, research, and operations all share the same structural properties that make TA useful — measurable state variables, time series dynamics, feedback loops, pattern recurrence, adversarial dynamics, and external verification. A build time trend is structurally analogous to a price trend. A test failure probability is structurally analogous to a risk assessment. The mathematics is identical; the domain vocabulary changes.
    14→
    15→The `Oracle` trait provides the universal interface. Domain-specific implementations (chain, coding, research, custom) handle the details. New domains are added by implementing the Oracle trait — not modifying the kernel.

# Topic 20: Technical Analysis — Universal Oracle Primitives

> TA is NOT chain-only. It is a general-purpose prediction framework with domain-specific instances.

**Prerequisites**: [00-architecture](../00-architecture/INDEX.md) for Synapse Architecture (Engram, 6 traits, 5 layers), [05-learning](../05-learning/INDEX.md) for cybernetic feedback loops and CascadeRouter, [06-neuro](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for HDC knowledge encoding and tier progression

---

## Overview

Technical analysis (TA) originated as a financial discipline — chart patterns, moving averages, momentum oscillators applied to price data. In Roko, TA is generalized into a set of **universal oracle primitives**: prediction, evaluation, calibration, and feedback loops that operate identically across any domain where an agent interacts with a verifiable external system.

The core insight: code, markets, research, and operations all share the same structural properties that make TA useful — measurable state variables, time series dynamics, feedback loops, pattern recurrence, adversarial dynamics, and external verification. A build time trend is structurally analogous to a price trend. A test failure probability is structurally analogous to a risk assessment. The mathematics is identical; the domain vocabulary changes.

The `Oracle` trait provides the universal interface. Domain-specific implementations (chain, coding, research, custom) handle the details. New domains are added by implementing the Oracle trait — not modifying the kernel.

---

## Sub-documents

| # | File | Title | Lines | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00 | [00-vision-ta-generalized.md](./00-vision-ta-generalized.md) | TA as Universal Oracle Primitives | ~230 | Vision document. Why generalize TA. Structural analogy argument. Cross-domain HDC transfer. Where oracles fit in the Synapse Architecture. |
| 01 | [01-oracle-trait.md](./01-oracle-trait.md) | The Oracle Trait | ~380 | Full Rust trait signature. `predict()`, `evaluate()`. OracleQuery, Prediction, PredictionAccuracy structs. PredictionStore, ResidualCorrector, CalibrationTracker. Integration with all 6 Synapse traits. |
| 02 | [02-chain-oracles.md](./02-chain-oracles.md) | Chain Oracles | ~300 | ChainOracle implementation. Traditional TA (MA, RSI, Bollinger, MACD). DeFi-native indicators (concentrated liquidity, lending, funding rates, yield curves, on-chain options). MEV detection. 8 T0 chain probes. Mirage-rs integration. |
| 03 | [03-coding-oracles.md](./03-coding-oracles.md) | Coding Oracles | ~320 | CodingOracle implementation. Build time prediction, test failure probability, complexity drift, dependency risk, performance regression. 6 T0 coding probes. Tech debt feedback loops. roko-index integration. |
| 04 | [04-research-oracles.md](./04-research-oracles.md) | Research Oracles | ~280 | ResearchOracle implementation. Source reliability, completeness assessment, contradiction detection, replication probability, citation momentum. p-hacking detection. Charnov stopping rule for research. |
| 05 | [05-witness-as-ta-generalized.md](./05-witness-as-ta-generalized.md) | The Witness Pipeline | ~280 | Generalized witness trait. Chain, coding, research witness implementations. Triage pipeline (MIDAS-R, DDSketch). CorticalState shared signal bus. Three cognitive speeds in the witness. |
| 06 | [06-hyperdimensional-ta.md](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) | Hyperdimensional TA | ~280 | HDC pattern algebra for TA. Role-filler composition. Temporal encoding via permutation. Shift-invariant matching. DeFi and coding codebooks. Cross-domain resonance detection (threshold 0.526). Pattern store with Dreams consolidation. |
| 07 | [07-spectral-liquidity-manifolds.md](./07-spectral-liquidity-manifolds.md) | Spectral Liquidity Manifolds | ~300 | Riemannian geometry for DeFi execution costs. Metric tensor (slippage + gas + time + opportunity). Christoffel symbols. Geodesics as optimal execution paths. Ricci scalar as market stability indicator. Parallel transport for cross-protocol pattern transfer. Fréchet mean. Spectral decomposition. |
| 08 | [08-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md](./08-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md) | Adaptive Signal Metabolism | ~280 | Signals as living organisms. Hebbian learning (Oja's rule). Replicator dynamics (Taylor & Jonker). Fisher's fundamental theorem. Speciation, fitness landscapes (Sewall Wright). Red Queen dynamic. SignalRegistry ecosystem. |
| 09 | [09-causal-microstructure-discovery.md](./09-causal-microstructure-discovery.md) | Causal Microstructure Discovery | ~280 | Pearl's causal hierarchy (3 levels). Structural causal models. do-operator. PC algorithm (Spirtes/Glymour/Scheines). Granger causality with 4 DeFi extensions. Interventional discovery via mirage-rs. Dream-based counterfactuals. Backdoor criterion. |
| 10 | [10-predictive-geometry-and-resonant-patterns.md](./10-predictive-geometry-and-resonant-patterns.md) | Predictive Geometry & Resonant Patterns | ~320 | TDA persistence diagrams and landscapes (Bubenik). Robust statistics (trimmed mean, Hodges-Lehmann, MAD, rank transform). Signal cross-validation. Red-team dreaming algorithm. Domain-specific attack prototypes. |
| 12 | [12-somatic-ta-and-emergent-multiscale.md](./12-somatic-ta-and-emergent-multiscale.md) | Somatic TA & Emergent Multiscale Intelligence | ~320 | Somatic markers (Damasio) as HDC bindings. PAD encoding. Somatic retrieval (~63ns). 15% contrarian retrieval (Bower). IIT Phi over 9 TA subsystems (510 bipartitions). MIB diagnostic. PID synergy detection (Williams & Beer). |

1→# Technical Analysis as Universal Oracle Primitives
     2→
     3→> TA is NOT chain-only. It is a general-purpose prediction framework with domain-specific instances.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [00-architecture](../00-architecture/INDEX.md) for Synapse Architecture, [06-neuro](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for HDC knowledge encoding
    10→**Key sources**: `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §4, `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/00-witness-as-technical-analyst.md`, `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→Technical analysis (TA) originated as a financial discipline — chart patterns, moving averages, momentum oscillators applied to price data. In Roko, TA is generalized far beyond its financial origins into a set of **universal oracle primitives**: prediction, evaluation, calibration, and feedback loops that operate identically across any domain where an agent interacts with a verifiable external system.
    17→
    18→The core insight: code, markets, research, and operations all share the same structural properties that make TA useful. They are structured systems with measurable state variables, feedback loops, non-stationary dynamics, and adversarial participants. A build time trend is structurally analogous to a price trend. A test failure probability is structurally analogous to a risk assessment. A dependency vulnerability score is structurally analogous to portfolio risk. The mathematics is the same; the domain vocabulary changes.
    19→
    20→This document establishes the vision: TA as a domain-agnostic cognitive capability that any Roko agent can use, regardless of whether it operates on blockchains, codebases, research corpora, or any other structured domain. We lead with the universal `Oracle` trait, then show how domain-specific instances (chain oracles, coding oracles, research oracles) implement the same interface.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## Why generalize TA
    25→
    26→### The structural analogy argument
    27→
    28→Traditional TA works because financial markets have specific properties:
    29→
    30→1. **Measurable state** — prices, volumes, open interest, volatility
    31→2. **Time series dynamics** — trends, mean reversion, regime changes
    32→3. **Feedback loops** — momentum traders amplify trends, mean-reversion traders dampen them
    33→4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar market structures produce similar outcomes
    34→5. **Adversarial dynamics** — participants adapt to each other's strategies
    35→6. **External verification** — prices are observable, outcomes are deterministic
    36→
    37→Codebases share every one of these properties:
    38→
    39→1. **Measurable state** — compilation time, test pass rates, cyclomatic complexity, dependency counts
    40→2. **Time series dynamics** — complexity trends, performance regression trajectories, test coverage drift
    41→3. **Feedback loops** — tech debt accumulates → development slows → more shortcuts → more debt
    42→4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar code structures produce similar bug patterns
    43→5. **Adversarial dynamics** — security vulnerabilities, supply chain attacks
    44→6. **External verification** — compilers, test suites, benchmarks produce objective outcomes
    45→
    46→Research corpora share similar properties:
    47→
    48→1. **Measurable state** — citation counts, publication velocity, contradiction density
    49→2. **Time series dynamics** — field maturity, paradigm shifts, replication crises
    50→3. **Feedback loops** — popular papers attract more citations → more visibility → more citations
    51→4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar research methodologies produce similar reliability
    52→5. **Adversarial dynamics** — p-hacking, selective reporting, predatory publishing
    53→6. **External verification** — replication studies, meta-analyses, cross-validation
    54→
    55→The structural analogy is not a metaphor — it is a mathematical fact. If we define TA as "systematic prediction from structured time series with feedback," then TA applies to any domain with those properties. Markets are one instance. Code is another. Research is a third. Roko treats all three identically at the trait level, specializing only in the domain-specific implementations.
    56→
    57→### Cross-domain insight transfer
    58→
    59→When a coding agent learns "high-churn modules need more review," it encodes this as an HDC vector:
    60→
    61→```
    62→BIND(high_complexity, more_review)
    63→```
    64→
    65→When a chain agent learns "high-volatility assets need more caution," it encodes:
    66→
    67→```
    68→BIND(high_volatility, more_caution)
    69→```
    70→
    71→Both encode the same abstract structure:
    72→
    73→```
    74→BIND(high_uncertainty, more_verification)
    75→```
    76→
    77→The Hamming similarity between these vectors is high because the HDC algebra preserves structural isomorphism. This means cross-domain insight transfer happens automatically through the Neuro (knowledge) system — a pattern learned in one domain can be detected as relevant in another.
    78→
    79→This is the deepest justification for generalizing TA. If each domain used its own bespoke prediction framework, cross-domain transfer would require explicit translation. With universal oracle primitives, transfer happens through the HDC similarity space at nanosecond cost (Kleyko et al., 2022, ACM Computing Surveys).
    80→
    81→### The Roko thesis: "the scaffold IS the product"
    82→
    83→Roko's architectural thesis is that agent performance varies dramatically based on the surrounding harness — context engineering, verification, learning loops, cognitive architecture. Meta-Harness (Lee et al., 2026, arXiv:2603.28052) demonstrated +7.7 points on text classification and +4.7 on IMO-level math from harness optimization alone, at 4× fewer tokens.
    84→
    85→TA generalization is a direct expression of this thesis. By making prediction, calibration, and feedback universal primitives — not domain-specific add-ons — every agent benefits from the same self-improving prediction infrastructure. The harness (oracle + calibration tracker + residual corrector) works identically whether the agent is predicting build times, gas prices, or source reliability.
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## Domain-agnostic prediction architecture
    90→
    91→### Where oracles live in the Synapse Architecture
    92→
    93→The Oracle subsystem operates across multiple layers of Roko's five-layer architecture:
    94→
    95→| Layer | Oracle role | What it does |
    96→|---|---|---|
    97→| **L0 Runtime** | Prediction storage | `PredictionStore` persists predictions and outcomes |
    98→| **L1 Framework** | Router integration | `Router.feedback()` uses prediction accuracy for bandit updates |
    99→| **L2 Scaffold** | Context selection | EFE (Expected Free Energy) uses prediction confidence for context bidding |
   100→| **L3 Harness** | Gate calibration | Prediction residuals calibrate adaptive gate thresholds |
   101→| **L4 Orchestration** | Task prioritization | Prediction accuracy informs task scheduling priority |
   102→
   103→The Oracle connects to all six Synapse traits:
   104→
   105→- **Substrate** — `PredictionStore` persists predictions as Engrams via `Substrate.put()`
   106→- **Scorer** — `PredictiveScorer` uses oracle accuracy to weight Engram relevance
   107→- **Gate** — Prediction residuals feed adaptive gate thresholds (EMA per rung)
   108→- **Router** — Accurate oracle predictions increase routing weight via `Router.feedback()`
   109→- **Composer** — EFE-based context selection uses prediction confidence for VCG auction bids
   110→- **Policy** — `PredictionPolicy` tracks accuracy, feeds back to all other traits
   111→
   112→### The cognitive cross-cut dimension
   113→
   114→Oracles are a cognitive cross-cut — they inject into multiple layers via trait objects, never hardcoded. The Daimon (motivation engine) modulates oracle behavior through PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) state:
   115→
   116→- **Low confidence (Dominance)** → Predictions are made conservatively, wider prediction intervals
   117→- **High arousal** → More frequent prediction updates, faster residual correction
   118→- **Low pleasure (after prediction failures)** → Automatic model recalibration
   119→
   120→The Neuro (knowledge store) accumulates oracle patterns:
   121→
   122→- Successful prediction strategies become `StrategyFragment` knowledge entries
   123→- Systematic prediction biases become `Warning` entries
   124→- Causal relationships discovered through prediction analysis become `CausalLink` entries
   125→
   126→Dreams (offline learning) consolidate oracle performance:
   127→
   128→- NREM replay evaluates which prediction patterns were reliable across episodes
   129→- REM imagination generates counterfactual predictions to test oracle robustness
   130→- Integration staging promotes validated prediction strategies to permanent knowledge
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## The universal loop with oracles
   135→
   136→The 9-step universal cognitive loop (Synapse Loop) integrates oracles at every stage:
   137→
   138→```
   139→1. PERCEIVE      → Substrate.query()       Oracle reads current state
   140→2. EVALUATE      → Scorer.score()          PredictiveScorer weights by prediction accuracy
   141→3. ATTEND        → Router.select()         Oracle predictions bias attention to uncertain areas
   142→4. INTEGRATE     → Composer.compose()      EFE-weighted context includes prediction context
   143→5. ACT           → Agent.execute()         Agent makes prediction BEFORE acting
   144→6. VERIFY        → Gate.verify()           Prediction resolved against external outcome
   145→7. PERSIST       → Substrate.put()         Prediction + outcome stored as Engrams
   146→8. ADAPT         → Policy.decide()         Residual correction updates calibration
   147→9. META-COGNIZE  → Daimon.assess()         Prediction accuracy updates cognitive state
   148→```
   149→
   150→This maps to CoALA cognitive architecture (Sumers et al., 2023, arXiv:2309.02427) with the prediction loop inspired by active inference (Friston Free Energy Principle) and the Good Regulator Theorem (Conant & Ashby, 1970).
   151→
   152→---
   153→
   154→## Three cognitive speeds for oracles
   155→
   156→Oracles operate at all three of Roko's cognitive timescales:
   157→
   158→| Speed | Period | Oracle activity |
   159→|---|---|---|
   160→| **Gamma** (~5-15s) | Real-time | T0 probes evaluate prediction error scalar. No LLM. Zero cost. |
   161→| **Theta** (~75s) | Reflection | Pending predictions resolved. Residuals computed. CalibrationTracker updated. |
   162→| **Delta** (hours) | Consolidation | Cross-domain prediction patterns consolidated. Routing tables updated. |
   163→
   164→At Gamma frequency, the 16 T0 probes (FrugalGPT-inspired; Chen et al., 2023, arXiv:2305.05176) compute a prediction error scalar that drives T0/T1/T2 cognitive tier routing:
   165→
   166→```
   167→error < 0.2  → T0 (suppress, no LLM)     ~80% of ticks
   168→error < 0.6  → T1 (fast model, shallow)   ~15% of ticks
   169→error ≥ 0.6  → T2 (full model, deep)      ~5% of ticks
   170→```
   171→
   172→This means 80% of cognitive cycles cost nothing — the oracle decides no action is needed based on pure Rust probes running in microseconds.
   173→
   174→---
   175→
   176→## Why domain-specific instances matter
   177→
   178→Universal primitives provide the architecture. Domain-specific instances provide the value. Each domain has unique:
   179→
   180→- **State variables** to predict (prices vs. build times vs. citation counts)
   181→- **Verification mechanisms** (blockchain finality vs. compiler output vs. replication studies)
   182→- **Temporal dynamics** (block-time vs. CI pipeline cadence vs. publication cycles)
   183→- **Adversarial threats** (MEV vs. supply chain attacks vs. p-hacking)
   184→- **Feedback loop structure** (market impact vs. tech debt vs. citation cascades)
   185→
   186→The Oracle trait abstracts over these differences. Domain-specific implementations handle the details. New domains are added by implementing the Oracle trait — not modifying the kernel.
   187→
   188→---
   189→
   190→## Academic foundations
   191→
   192→- Friston, K. (2010). "The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?" *Nature Reviews Neuroscience*, 11(2), 127-138. — Active inference framework underlying EFE-based context selection.
   193→- Conant, R. C., & Ashby, W. R. (1970). "Every good regulator of a system must be a model of that system." *International Journal of Systems Science*, 1(2), 89-97. — Good Regulator Theorem motivating agent self-modeling via prediction.
   194→- Sumers, T. R., et al. (2023). "Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents." arXiv:2309.02427. — CoALA framework that the Synapse Loop implements.
   195→- Lee, S., et al. (2026). "Meta-Harness: Optimizing Harness, Not Model." arXiv:2603.28052. — +7.7 pts from scaffold optimization alone.
   196→- Chen, L., et al. (2023). "FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance." arXiv:2305.05176. — Cascade architectures for cost reduction.
   197→- Kleyko, D., et al. (2022). "A Survey on Hyperdimensional Computing." *ACM Computing Surveys*, 54(6), 1-51. — HDC foundations for cross-domain transfer.
   198→- Kanerva, P. (2009). "Hyperdimensional Computing: An Introduction to Computing in Distributed Representation with High-Dimensional Random Vectors." *Cognitive Computation*, 1(2), 139-159. — Binary spatter codes for pattern encoding.
   199→- Pirolli, P., & Card, S. (1999). "Information foraging." *Psychological Review*, 106(4), 643-675. — Optimal foraging theory applied to information retrieval, inspiring predictive foraging.
   200→- Charnov, E. L. (1976). "Optimal foraging: the marginal value theorem." *Theoretical Population Biology*, 9, 129-136. — Stopping rule for context retrieval.
   201→
   202→---
   203→
   204→## Current status and gaps
   205→
   206→**What exists today**:
   207→- `CascadeRouter` in `roko-learn` implements tier-based model routing with UCB1 and LinUCB
   208→- Adaptive gate thresholds with EMA per rung in `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json`
   209→- Efficiency events logged per turn in `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl`
   210→- Prompt experiments via `ExperimentStore` in `.roko/learn/experiments.json`
   211→
   212→**What's scaffold/planned**:
   213→- Full `Oracle` trait implementation (defined in `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md`)
   214→- `PredictionStore` with on-chain and off-chain variants
   215→- `CalibrationTracker` per (model, task_category) pair
   216→- Domain-specific oracle implementations (chain, coding, research)
   217→- Active inference state space (factorized POMDP, 90 states)
   218→- EFE-based context selection with pragmatic + epistemic - ambiguity decomposition
   219→
   220→See `tmp/implementation-plans/modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md` for the Thompson Sampling and predictive foraging calibration implementation plan.
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→## Cross-references
   225→
   226→- See [01-oracle-trait.md](./01-oracle-trait.md) for the full Rust trait signature and Prediction struct
   227→- See [02-chain-oracles.md](./02-chain-oracles.md) for chain-specific TA primitives
   228→- See [03-coding-oracles.md](./03-coding-oracles.md) for coding equivalents of TA primitives
   229→- See [13-predictive-foraging-and-active-inference.md](./13-predictive-foraging-and-active-inference.md) for the full prediction loop
   230→- See topic [00-architecture](../00-architecture/INDEX.md) for the Synapse Architecture overview
   231→- See topic [05-learning](../05-learning/INDEX.md) for the cybernetic feedback loops
   232→- See topic [06-neuro](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for HDC cross-domain transfer

# Technical Analysis as Universal Oracle Primitives

> TA is NOT chain-only. It is a general-purpose prediction framework with domain-specific instances.


> **Implementation**: Specified

**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
**Prerequisites**: [00-architecture](../00-architecture/INDEX.md) for Synapse Architecture, [06-neuro](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for HDC knowledge encoding
**Key sources**: `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §4, `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/00-witness-as-technical-analyst.md`, `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md`

---

## Abstract

Technical analysis (TA) originated as a financial discipline — chart patterns, moving averages, momentum oscillators applied to price data. In Roko, TA is generalized far beyond its financial origins into a set of **universal oracle primitives**: prediction, evaluation, calibration, and feedback loops that operate identically across any domain where an agent interacts with a verifiable external system.

The core insight: code, markets, research, and operations all share the same structural properties that make TA useful. They are structured systems with measurable state variables, feedback loops, non-stationary dynamics, and adversarial participants. A build time trend is structurally analogous to a price trend. A test failure probability is structurally analogous to a risk assessment. A dependency vulnerability score is structurally analogous to portfolio risk. The mathematics is the same; the domain vocabulary changes.

This document establishes the vision: TA as a domain-agnostic cognitive capability that any Roko agent can use, regardless of whether it operates on blockchains, codebases, research corpora, or any other structured domain. We lead with the universal `Oracle` trait, then show how domain-specific instances (chain oracles, coding oracles, research oracles) implement the same interface.

---

## Why generalize TA

### The structural analogy argument

Traditional TA works because financial markets have specific properties:

1. **Measurable state** — prices, volumes, open interest, volatility
2. **Time series dynamics** — trends, mean reversion, regime changes
3. **Feedback loops** — momentum traders amplify trends, mean-reversion traders dampen them
4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar market structures produce similar outcomes
5. **Adversarial dynamics** — participants adapt to each other's strategies
6. **External verification** — prices are observable, outcomes are deterministic

Codebases share every one of these properties:

1. **Measurable state** — compilation time, test pass rates, cyclomatic complexity, dependency counts
2. **Time series dynamics** — complexity trends, performance regression trajectories, test coverage drift
3. **Feedback loops** — tech debt accumulates → development slows → more shortcuts → more debt
4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar code structures produce similar bug patterns
5. **Adversarial dynamics** — security vulnerabilities, supply chain attacks
6. **External verification** — compilers, test suites, benchmarks produce objective outcomes

Research corpora share similar properties:

1. **Measurable state** — citation counts, publication velocity, contradiction density
2. **Time series dynamics** — field maturity, paradigm shifts, replication crises
3. **Feedback loops** — popular papers attract more citations → more visibility → more citations
4. **Pattern recurrence** — similar research methodologies produce similar reliability
5. **Adversarial dynamics** — p-hacking, selective reporting, predatory publishing
6. **External verification** — replication studies, meta-analyses, cross-validation

The structural analogy is not a metaphor — it is a mathematical fact. If we define TA as "systematic prediction from structured time series with feedback," then TA applies to any domain with those properties. Markets are one instance. Code is another. Research is a third. Roko treats all three identically at the trait level, specializing only in the domain-specific implementations.

### Cross-domain insight transfer

When a coding agent learns "high-churn modules need more review," it encodes this as an HDC vector:

```
BIND(high_complexity, more_review)
```

When a chain agent learns "high-volatility assets need more caution," it encodes:

```
BIND(high_volatility, more_caution)
```

Both encode the same abstract structure:

```
BIND(high_uncertainty, more_verification)
```

The Hamming similarity between these vectors is high because the HDC algebra preserves structural isomorphism. This means cross-domain insight transfer happens automatically through the Neuro (knowledge) system — a pattern learned in one domain can be detected as relevant in another.

This is the deepest justification for generalizing TA. If each domain used its own bespoke prediction framework, cross-domain transfer would require explicit translation. With universal oracle primitives, transfer happens through the HDC similarity space at nanosecond cost (Kleyko et al., 2022, ACM Computing Surveys).

### The Roko thesis: "the scaffold IS the product"

Roko's architectural thesis is that agent performance varies dramatically based on the surrounding harness — context engineering, verification, learning loops, cognitive architecture. Meta-Harness (Lee et al., 2026, arXiv:2603.28052) demonstrated +7.7 points on text classification and +4.7 on IMO-level math from harness optimization alone, at 4× fewer tokens.

TA generalization is a direct expression of this thesis. By making prediction, calibration, and feedback universal primitives — not domain-specific add-ons — every agent benefits from the same self-improving prediction infrastructure. The harness (oracle + calibration tracker + residual corrector) works identically whether the agent is predicting build times, gas prices, or source reliability.

---

## Domain-agnostic prediction architecture

### Where oracles live in the Synapse Architecture

The Oracle subsystem operates across multiple layers of Roko's five-layer architecture:

| Layer | Oracle role | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 Runtime** | Prediction storage | `PredictionStore` persists predictions and outcomes |
| **L1 Framework** | Router integration | `Router.feedback()` uses prediction accuracy for bandit updates |
| **L2 Scaffold** | Context selection | EFE (Expected Free Energy) uses prediction confidence for context bidding |
| **L3 Harness** | Gate calibration | Prediction residuals calibrate adaptive gate thresholds |
| **L4 Orchestration** | Task prioritization | Prediction accuracy informs task scheduling priority |

The Oracle connects to all six Synapse traits:

- **Substrate** — `PredictionStore` persists predictions as Engrams via `Substrate.put()`
- **Scorer** — `PredictiveScorer` uses oracle accuracy to weight Engram relevance
- **Gate** — Prediction residuals feed adaptive gate thresholds (EMA per rung)
- **Router** — Accurate oracle predictions increase routing weight via `Router.feedback()`
- **Composer** — EFE-based context selection uses prediction confidence for VCG auction bids
- **Policy** — `PredictionPolicy` tracks accuracy, feeds back to all other traits

### The cognitive cross-cut dimension

Oracles are a cognitive cross-cut — they inject into multiple layers via trait objects, never hardcoded. The Daimon (motivation engine) modulates oracle behavior through PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) state:

- **Low confidence (Dominance)** → Predictions are made conservatively, wider prediction intervals
- **High arousal** → More frequent prediction updates, faster residual correction
- **Low pleasure (after prediction failures)** → Automatic model recalibration

The Neuro (knowledge store) accumulates oracle patterns:

- Successful prediction strategies become `StrategyFragment` knowledge entries
- Systematic prediction biases become `Warning` entries
- Causal relationships discovered through prediction analysis become `CausalLink` entries

Dreams (offline learning) consolidate oracle performance:

- NREM replay evaluates which prediction patterns were reliable across episodes
- REM imagination generates counterfactual predictions to test oracle robustness
- Integration staging promotes validated prediction strategies to permanent knowledge

---

## The universal loop with oracles

The 9-step universal cognitive loop (Synapse Loop) integrates oracles at every stage:

```
1. PERCEIVE      → Substrate.query()       Oracle reads current state
2. EVALUATE      → Scorer.score()          PredictiveScorer weights by prediction accuracy
3. ATTEND        → Router.select()         Oracle predictions bias attention to uncertain areas
4. INTEGRATE     → Composer.compose()      EFE-weighted context includes prediction context
5. ACT           → Agent.execute()         Agent makes prediction BEFORE acting
6. VERIFY        → Gate.verify()           Prediction resolved against external outcome
7. PERSIST       → Substrate.put()         Prediction + outcome stored as Engrams
8. ADAPT         → Policy.decide()         Residual correction updates calibration
9. META-COGNIZE  → Daimon.assess()         Prediction accuracy updates cognitive state
```

This maps to CoALA cognitive architecture (Sumers et al., 2023, arXiv:2309.02427) with the prediction loop inspired by active inference (Friston Free Energy Principle) and the Good Regulator Theorem (Conant & Ashby, 1970).

---

## Three cognitive speeds for oracles

Oracles operate at all three of Roko's cognitive timescales:

| Speed | Period | Oracle activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Gamma** (~5-15s) | Real-time | T0 probes evaluate prediction error scalar. No LLM. Zero cost. |
| **Theta** (~75s) | Reflection | Pending predictions resolved. Residuals computed. CalibrationTracker updated. |
| **Delta** (hours) | Consolidation | Cross-domain prediction patterns consolidated. Routing tables updated. |

At Gamma frequency, the 16 T0 probes (FrugalGPT-inspired; Chen et al., 2023, arXiv:2305.05176) compute a prediction error scalar that drives T0/T1/T2 cognitive tier routing:

```
error < 0.2  → T0 (suppress, no LLM)     ~80% of ticks
error < 0.6  → T1 (fast model, shallow)   ~15% of ticks
error ≥ 0.6  → T2 (full model, deep)      ~5% of ticks
```

This means 80% of cognitive cycles cost nothing — the oracle decides no action is needed based on pure Rust probes running in microseconds.

---

## Why domain-specific instances matter

Universal primitives provide the architecture. Domain-specific instances provide the value. Each domain has unique:

- **State variables** to predict (prices vs. build times vs. citation counts)
- **Verification mechanisms** (blockchain finality vs. compiler output vs. replication studies)
- **Temporal dynamics** (block-time vs. CI pipeline cadence vs. publication cycles)
- **Adversarial threats** (MEV vs. supply chain attacks vs. p-hacking)
- **Feedback loop structure** (market impact vs. tech debt vs. citation cascades)

The Oracle trait abstracts over these differences. Domain-specific implementations handle the details. New domains are added by implementing the Oracle trait — not modifying the kernel.

---

## Academic foundations

- Friston, K. (2010). "The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?" *Nature Reviews Neuroscience*, 11(2), 127-138. — Active inference framework underlying EFE-based context selection.
- Conant, R. C., & Ashby, W. R. (1970). "Every good regulator of a system must be a model of that system." *International Journal of Systems Science*, 1(2), 89-97. — Good Regulator Theorem motivating agent self-modeling via prediction.
- Sumers, T. R., et al. (2023). "Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents." arXiv:2309.02427. — CoALA framework that the Synapse Loop implements.
- Lee, S., et al. (2026). "Meta-Harness: Optimizing Harness, Not Model." arXiv:2603.28052. — +7.7 pts from scaffold optimization alone.
- Chen, L., et al. (2023). "FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance." arXiv:2305.05176. — Cascade architectures for cost reduction.
- Kleyko, D., et al. (2022). "A Survey on Hyperdimensional Computing." *ACM Computing Surveys*, 54(6), 1-51. — HDC foundations for cross-domain transfer.
- Kanerva, P. (2009). "Hyperdimensional Computing: An Introduction to Computing in Distributed Representation with High-Dimensional Random Vectors." *Cognitive Computation*, 1(2), 139-159. — Binary spatter codes for pattern encoding.
- Pirolli, P., & Card, S. (1999). "Information foraging." *Psychological Review*, 106(4), 643-675. — Optimal foraging theory applied to information retrieval, inspiring predictive foraging.
- Charnov, E. L. (1976). "Optimal foraging: the marginal value theorem." *Theoretical Population Biology*, 9, 129-136. — Stopping rule for context retrieval.

---

## Current status and gaps

**What exists today**:
- `CascadeRouter` in `roko-learn` implements tier-based model routing with UCB1 and LinUCB
- Adaptive gate thresholds with EMA per rung in `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json`
- Efficiency events logged per turn in `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl`
- Prompt experiments via `ExperimentStore` in `.roko/learn/experiments.json`

**What's scaffold/planned**:
- Full `Oracle` trait implementation (defined in `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md`)
- `PredictionStore` with on-chain and off-chain variants
- `CalibrationTracker` per (model, task_category) pair
- Domain-specific oracle implementations (chain, coding, research)
- Active inference state space (factorized POMDP, 90 states)
- EFE-based context selection with pragmatic + epistemic - ambiguity decomposition

See `tmp/implementation-plans/modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md` for the Thompson Sampling and predictive foraging calibration implementation plan.

---

1→# The Oracle Trait — Universal Prediction Interface
     2→
     3→> Every domain-specific prediction system in Roko implements a single trait. This document specifies the full Rust signature, supporting types, and integration points.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [00-vision-ta-generalized](./00-vision-ta-generalized.md) for motivation, [00-architecture](../00-architecture/INDEX.md) for Synapse traits and Engram
    10→**Key sources**: `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §4, `refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## The Oracle trait
    15→
    16→The `Oracle` trait is the single interface through which all prediction capabilities are expressed. It is async, object-safe (`Send + Sync`), and designed for composition with the six Synapse traits:
    17→
    18→```rust
    19→/// Universal prediction interface for any domain.
    20→///
    21→/// Chain oracles predict prices, gas, liquidity depth, MEV opportunities.
    22→/// Coding oracles predict build times, test failures, complexity drift.
    23→/// Research oracles predict source reliability, contradiction density.
    24→/// Custom domains implement the same trait with domain-specific queries.
    25→pub trait Oracle: Send + Sync {
    26→    /// Make a prediction about future state.
    27→    ///
    28→    /// The query encodes WHAT to predict. The context encodes the agent's
    29→    /// current cognitive state (PAD vector, active knowledge, recent history).
    30→    /// The returned Prediction includes confidence bounds and a time horizon
    31→    /// by which the prediction should be resolved.
    32→    async fn predict(
    33→        &self,
    34→        query: &OracleQuery,
    35→        ctx: &Context,
    36→    ) -> Result<Prediction>;
    37→
    38→    /// Evaluate a past prediction against the actual outcome.
    39→    ///
    40→    /// The outcome is an Engram produced by external verification —
    41→    /// a compiler result, a blockchain state, a test suite output, a
    42→    /// replication study. The returned PredictionAccuracy drives feedback
    43→    /// into Router, Daimon, Neuro, and Gate subsystems.
    44→    async fn evaluate(
    45→        &self,
    46→        prediction: &Prediction,
    47→        outcome: &Engram,
    48→    ) -> Result<PredictionAccuracy>;
    49→}
    50→```
    51→
    52→Both methods are async because predictions may require I/O — querying a blockchain node, running a compilation probe, or fetching citation data. The trait is `Send + Sync` to allow concurrent prediction evaluation across multiple oracle instances.
    53→
    54→### Design rationale
    55→
    56→The Oracle trait deliberately does **not** include:
    57→
    58→- **`subscribe()`** — Real-time streams are handled by `Substrate.query()` with watch semantics. Oracles predict; Substrates observe.
    59→- **`calibrate()`** — Calibration is a `Policy` concern. The `CalibrationTracker` (see [13-predictive-foraging-and-active-inference.md](./13-predictive-foraging-and-active-inference.md)) wraps any Oracle and adds calibration as a separate layer, following the Synapse Architecture's composition principle.
    60→- **`batch_predict()`** — Batch semantics are provided by the caller iterating over queries. Oracle implementations may internally batch for efficiency (e.g., a chain oracle batching RPC calls), but this is an implementation detail, not a trait concern.
    61→
    62→This follows Ousterhout's "deep module" principle (Ousterhout, 2018, *A Philosophy of Software Design*) — the interface is narrow (2 methods), but the implementation depth is substantial.
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## OracleQuery — What to predict
    67→
    68→The `OracleQuery` struct encodes the prediction request. It is domain-agnostic at the top level, with domain-specific payloads carried in the `domain` field:
    69→
    70→```rust
    71→/// A request for a prediction.
    72→///
    73→/// The query specifies WHAT to predict (domain-specific payload),
    74→/// at what confidence level, over what time horizon.
    75→pub struct OracleQuery {
    76→    /// Unique identifier for this query (content-addressed, BLAKE3).
    77→    pub id: ContentHash,
    78→
    79→    /// What domain this prediction belongs to.
    80→    pub domain: OracleDomain,
    81→
    82→    /// The specific question being asked, as a domain-specific payload.
    83→    /// Chain: "What will ETH price be in 5 blocks?"
    84→    /// Coding: "Will this change break the test suite?"
    85→    /// Research: "Is this source reliable for this claim?"
    86→    pub payload: QueryPayload,
    87→
    88→    /// How far into the future the prediction should cover.
    89→    pub horizon: Duration,
    90→
    91→    /// Minimum acceptable confidence for the prediction to be useful.
    92→    /// Below this threshold, the oracle should return `Err(LowConfidence)`.
    93→    pub min_confidence: f64,
    94→
    95→    /// Tags for categorization (used by CalibrationTracker to track
    96→    /// per-category accuracy).
    97→    pub tags: BTreeMap<String, String>,
    98→
    99→    /// Timestamp of query creation.
   100→    pub created_at_ms: i64,
   101→}
   102→```
   103→
   104→### OracleDomain — Domain classification
   105→
   106→```rust
   107→/// The domain a prediction belongs to.
   108→///
   109→/// Used by CalibrationTracker to maintain per-(model, domain) accuracy
   110→/// statistics, and by the Router to select appropriate oracle implementations.
   111→#[non_exhaustive]
   112→pub enum OracleDomain {
   113→    /// On-chain TA: price, gas, liquidity, MEV, protocol health.
   114→    Chain,
   115→
   116→    /// Software engineering: build time, test failure, complexity, dependency risk.
   117→    Coding,
   118→
   119→    /// Research and information analysis: source reliability, completeness, contradiction.
   120→    Research,
   121→
   122→    /// Operations: deployment success, infrastructure health, latency prediction.
   123→    Operations,
   124→
   125→    /// User-defined domain with a string identifier.
   126→    Custom(String),
   127→}
   128→```
   129→
   130→The `#[non_exhaustive]` attribute ensures new domains can be added without breaking existing code. The `Custom(String)` variant allows users to define domains not anticipated by the framework.
   131→

   133→

   136→///

   143→    /// Coding domain predictions.

   156→/// Chain-specific prediction targets.

   165→    pub conditions: Vec<ChainCondition>,

   167→
   168→/// Coding-specific prediction targets.
   169→pub struct CodingQueryPayload {
   170→    /// The scope of the prediction (file, module, crate, workspace).

   172→
   173→    /// The metric to predict (build_time, test_pass_rate, complexity_delta,
   174→    /// dependency_risk, perf_regression).

   176→
   177→    /// The change set that triggers this prediction (if applicable).
   178→    pub change_context: Option<ChangeContext>,

   180→
   181→/// Research-specific prediction targets.

   183→    /// The source being evaluated.

   185→
   186→    /// The metric to predict (reliability, completeness, contradiction_risk,

   189→
   190→    /// The claim or topic being assessed.

   192→}
   193→```

   196→
   197→## Prediction — The output
   198→
   199→The `Prediction` struct is what an Oracle returns. It is designed to be stored as an Engram (via `Substrate.put()`) and later resolved against actual outcomes:
   200→
   201→```rust
   202→/// A prediction about future state.
   203→///
   204→/// Predictions are stored as Engrams with `kind: Kind::Prediction`.
   205→/// They are resolved when the time horizon elapses or when external
   206→/// verification produces an outcome.
   207→pub struct Prediction {
   208→    /// Content-addressed ID (BLAKE3 of query_id + predicted_value + confidence + horizon).
   209→    pub id: ContentHash,
   210→
   211→    /// The query this prediction answers.
   212→    pub query_id: ContentHash,
   213→
   214→    /// The predicted value. Domain-specific interpretation.
   215→    ///
   216→    /// Chain: PredictedValue::Numeric(3245.50)  // ETH price
   217→    /// Coding: PredictedValue::Probability(0.85)  // test pass rate
   218→    /// Research: PredictedValue::Ordinal(Reliability::High)  // source reliability
   219→    pub value: PredictedValue,
   220→
   221→    /// Confidence in this prediction, [0.0, 1.0].
   222→    ///
   223→    /// Maps to the Engram Score.confidence axis.
   224→    /// Fed into the VCG auction as a bid weight for prediction context.
   225→    pub confidence: f64,
   226→
   227→    /// Prediction interval — the range within which the actual value
   228→    /// is expected to fall with the stated confidence.
   229→    ///
   230→    /// For Probability predictions, this is a credible interval.
   231→    /// For Numeric predictions, this is a prediction interval.
   232→    pub interval: Option<PredictionInterval>,
   233→
   234→    /// When this prediction was made.
   235→    pub created_at_ms: i64,
   236→
   237→    /// When this prediction should be resolved.
   238→    /// After this time, the PredictionStore marks it for evaluation.
   239→    pub resolve_by_ms: i64,
   240→
   241→    /// The model and oracle that produced this prediction.
   242→    /// Used by CalibrationTracker for per-model accuracy tracking.
   243→    pub provenance: PredictionProvenance,
   244→
   245→    /// Lineage — which Engrams informed this prediction.
   246→    /// Enables causal replay: "why did the oracle predict X?"
   247→    pub lineage: Vec<ContentHash>,
   248→
   249→    /// Resolution state. None until resolved.
   250→    pub outcome: Option<PredictionOutcome>,
   251→}
   252→```
   253→
   254→### PredictedValue — Domain-polymorphic values
   255→
   256→```rust
   257→/// The value being predicted.
   258→///
   259→/// Supports numeric (prices, times), probability (pass rates, risk scores),
   260→/// categorical (reliability levels), and compound (multiple related values).
   261→pub enum PredictedValue {
   262→    /// A numeric value (price, time, count).
   263→    Numeric(f64),
   264→
   265→    /// A probability [0.0, 1.0].
   266→    Probability(f64),
   267→
   268→    /// An ordinal category with associated numeric rank.
   269→    Ordinal { label: String, rank: u32 },
   270→
   271→    /// A boolean prediction (will it happen or not).
   272→    Binary(bool),
   273→
   274→    /// Multiple related predictions (e.g., price + volume + volatility).
   275→    Compound(BTreeMap<String, PredictedValue>),
   276→}
   277→```
   278→
   279→### PredictionInterval — Uncertainty quantification
   280→
   281→```rust
   282→/// Prediction interval bounding the expected outcome range.
   283→///
   284→/// The Oracle should produce intervals that are well-calibrated:
   285→/// a 90% interval should contain the actual value 90% of the time.
   286→/// CalibrationTracker measures this and adjusts via residual correction.
   287→pub struct PredictionInterval {
   288→    /// Lower bound of the prediction interval.
   289→    pub lower: f64,
   290→
   291→    /// Upper bound of the prediction interval.
   292→    pub upper: f64,
   293→
   294→    /// The coverage probability this interval targets (e.g., 0.90 for 90%).
   295→    pub coverage: f64,
   296→}
   297→```
   298→
   299→---
   300→
   301→## PredictionAccuracy — The feedback signal
   302→
   303→When a prediction resolves, the Oracle's `evaluate()` method returns a `PredictionAccuracy` that drives feedback into every Synapse subsystem:
   304→
   305→```rust
   306→/// The accuracy of a resolved prediction.
   307→///
   308→/// This is the primary feedback signal for the entire predictive foraging
   309→/// loop. It feeds into:
   310→/// - Router: accurate oracles get higher routing weight
   311→/// - Daimon: prediction errors update Dominance (confidence)
   312→/// - Neuro: prediction patterns become knowledge entries
   313→/// - Gate: calibrate adaptive thresholds via EMA
   314→/// - CalibrationTracker: update per-(model, category) bias estimates
   315→pub struct PredictionAccuracy {
   316→    /// The prediction being evaluated.
   317→    pub prediction_id: ContentHash,
   318→
   319→    /// The actual outcome Engram.
   320→    pub outcome_id: ContentHash,
   321→
   322→    /// Scalar accuracy [0.0, 1.0].
   323→    /// 1.0 = perfect prediction. 0.0 = maximally wrong.
   324→    pub accuracy: f64,
   325→
   326→    /// Signed residual: predicted_value - actual_value.
   327→    /// Positive = overestimated. Negative = underestimated.
   328→    /// Used by ResidualCorrector for bias correction.
   329→    pub residual: f64,
   330→
   331→    /// Whether the prediction interval contained the actual value.
   332→    /// Used by CalibrationTracker to measure interval calibration.
   333→    pub interval_hit: Option<bool>,
   334→
   335→    /// Time between prediction and resolution.
   336→    /// Used to evaluate prediction quality at different horizons.
   337→    pub resolution_lag_ms: i64,
   338→
   339→    /// The domain and category for per-category tracking.
   340→    pub domain: OracleDomain,
   341→    pub category: String,
   342→}
   343→```
   344→
   345→### PredictionOutcome — Resolution state
   346→
   347→```rust
   348→/// The resolution of a prediction.
   349→pub struct PredictionOutcome {
   350→    /// The actual value observed.
   351→    pub actual: PredictedValue,
   352→
   353→    /// The Engram that constitutes the evidence (compiler output, block data, etc.).
   354→    pub evidence_id: ContentHash,
   355→
   356→    /// When the outcome was observed.
   357→    pub resolved_at_ms: i64,
   358→
   359→    /// The accuracy assessment.
   360→    pub accuracy: PredictionAccuracy,
   361→}
   362→```
   363→
   364→---
   365→
   366→## Integration with the Synapse traits
   367→
   368→The Oracle trait is not a seventh Synapse trait — it is a **cognitive cross-cut** that integrates with all six traits through well-defined injection points:
   369→
   370→### Substrate integration
   371→
   372→Predictions and outcomes are persisted as Engrams:
   373→
   374→```rust
   375→// Store a new prediction
   376→let prediction = oracle.predict(&query, &ctx).await?;
   377→let engram = Engram::builder()
   378→    .kind(Kind::Prediction)
   379→    .body(Body::Json(serde_json::to_value(&prediction)?))
   380→    .tag("domain", prediction.provenance.domain.as_str())
   381→    .tag("horizon_ms", prediction.resolve_by_ms.to_string())
   382→    .score(Score {
   383→        confidence: prediction.confidence,
   384→        novelty: 0.5,  // predictions start at baseline novelty
   385→        utility: 0.0,  // utility accumulates after resolution
   386→        reputation: prediction.provenance.model_reputation,
   387→        ..Default::default()
   388→    })
   389→    .lineage(prediction.lineage.clone())
   390→    .build();
   391→substrate.put(engram).await?;
   392→```
   393→
   394→### Scorer integration — PredictiveScorer
   395→
   396→The `PredictiveScorer` uses oracle accuracy history to weight Engram relevance:
   397→
   398→```rust
   399→/// Scores Engrams based on how well the oracle that produced them
   400→/// has been performing recently.
   401→pub struct PredictiveScorer {
   402→    calibration: Arc<CalibrationTracker>,
   403→}
   404→
   405→impl Scorer for PredictiveScorer {
   406→    fn score(&self, engram: &Engram) -> Score {
   407→        let model = engram.provenance.model_id();
   408→        let category = engram.tag("task_category").unwrap_or("unknown");
   409→
   410→        // Oracle accuracy history modulates confidence
   411→        let calibration = self.calibration.get_accuracy(model, category);
   412→        let mut score = engram.score.clone();
   413→        score.confidence *= calibration.recent_accuracy;
   414→        score
   415→    }
   416→}
   417→```
   418→
   419→### Router integration
   420→
   421→Prediction accuracy feeds into `Router.feedback()`, updating bandit arms for model selection:
   422→
   423→```rust
   424→// After prediction resolution
   425→let accuracy = oracle.evaluate(&prediction, &outcome).await?;
   426→router.feedback(
   427→    &prediction.provenance.model_id,
   428→    accuracy.accuracy,  // reward signal
   429→)?;
   430→```
   431→
   432→This is how the CascadeRouter (with LinUCB + Thompson Sampling, see `roko-learn`) learns which models are best at predicting in which domains — the same bandit mechanism that routes LLM inference also routes oracle predictions.
   433→
   434→### Gate integration
   435→
   436→Prediction residuals calibrate adaptive gate thresholds:
   437→
   438→```rust
   439→// Residual correction updates gate thresholds
   440→let residual = accuracy.residual;
   441→gate_thresholds.update_ema(
   442→    accuracy.category.as_str(),
   443→    residual.abs(),
   444→    alpha: 0.1,  // EMA smoothing factor
   445→);
   446→```
   447→
   448→This creates a direct feedback loop: if an oracle consistently overestimates test pass rates, the gate threshold for that category tightens automatically.
   449→
   450→### Composer integration — EFE bidding
   451→
   452→Oracle predictions participate in the VCG attention auction (Vickrey 1961, Clarke 1971, Groves 1973) through Expected Free Energy (EFE) decomposition:
   453→
   454→```rust
   455→// Prediction context bids for attention budget
   456→let efe = pragmatic_value + epistemic_value - ambiguity;
   457→let bid = efe * urgency * affect_weight;
   458→composer.bid("oracle_predictions", bid, prediction_context);
   459→```
   460→
   461→High-uncertainty predictions bid more aggressively because resolving them has high epistemic value (Friston, 2010, *Nature Reviews Neuroscience*).
   462→
   463→### Policy integration — PredictionPolicy
   464→
   465→The `PredictionPolicy` observes prediction streams and emits new Engrams based on patterns:
   466→
   467→```rust
   468→/// Watches prediction accuracy streams and generates meta-predictions,
   469→/// warnings, and routing recommendations.
   470→pub struct PredictionPolicy {
   471→    tracker: Arc<CalibrationTracker>,
   472→    neuro: Arc<dyn Substrate>,
   473→}
   474→
   475→impl Policy for PredictionPolicy {
   476→    fn decide(&self, engrams: &[Engram]) -> Vec<Engram> {
   477→        let mut outputs = Vec::new();
   478→
   479→        // Detect systematic bias
   480→        let bias = self.tracker.mean_residual("coding", "test_prediction");
   481→        if bias.abs() > 0.15 {
   482→            outputs.push(Engram::warning(
   483→                format!("Systematic prediction bias detected: {:.2} in coding/test_prediction", bias),
   484→            ));
   485→        }
   486→
   487→        // Detect accuracy degradation
   488→        let trend = self.tracker.accuracy_trend("chain", "price");
   489→        if trend < -0.05 {  // accuracy dropping
   490→            outputs.push(Engram::insight(
   491→                "Chain price prediction accuracy declining — possible regime change",
   492→            ));
   493→        }
   494→
   495→        outputs
   496→    }
   497→}
   498→```
   499→
   500→---
   501→
   502→## PredictionStore — Persistence layer
   503→
   504→The `PredictionStore` manages the lifecycle of predictions from creation to resolution:
   505→
   506→```rust
   507→/// Manages prediction lifecycle: register → track → resolve → feedback.
   508→///
   509→/// Built on top of Substrate for persistence.
   510→/// Provides efficient querying by domain, horizon, and resolution status.
   511→pub struct PredictionStore {
   512→    substrate: Arc<dyn Substrate>,
   513→    pending: DashMap<ContentHash, Prediction>,
   514→    resolved: DashMap<ContentHash, PredictionOutcome>,
   515→}
   516→
   517→impl PredictionStore {
   518→    /// Register a new prediction for tracking.
   519→    pub async fn register(&self, prediction: Prediction) -> Result<()>;
   520→
   521→    /// Get all predictions that should have resolved by now.
   522→    pub async fn pending_resolutions(&self) -> Vec<Prediction>;
   523→
   524→    /// Resolve a prediction with an observed outcome.
   525→    pub async fn resolve(
   526→        &self,
   527→        prediction_id: &ContentHash,
   528→        outcome: &Engram,
   529→        oracle: &dyn Oracle,
   530→    ) -> Result<PredictionAccuracy>;
   531→
   532→    /// Get accuracy statistics for a given domain and category.
   533→    pub async fn accuracy_stats(
   534→        &self,
   535→        domain: &OracleDomain,
   536→        category: &str,
   537→    ) -> AccuracyStats;
   538→
   539→    /// Get all unresolved predictions for a given domain.
   540→    pub async fn pending_for_domain(
   541→        &self,
   542→        domain: &OracleDomain,
   543→    ) -> Vec<Prediction>;
   544→}
   545→```
   546→
   547→The `PredictionStore` has both off-chain (JSONL via `roko-fs`) and on-chain (Korai smart contract) variants. The on-chain variant enables collective calibration — all agents in the mesh share prediction outcomes, achieving up to 31.6× faster calibration for new agents (see [00-vision-ta-generalized.md](./00-vision-ta-generalized.md); collective calibration math in `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md` §VI with explicit caveats about the independence assumption).
   548→
   549→---
   550→
   551→## ResidualCorrector — Bias elimination
   552→
   553→The `ResidualCorrector` is a lightweight arithmetic layer that adjusts oracle predictions based on historical bias:
   554→
   555→```rust
   556→/// Corrects oracle predictions by subtracting the estimated systematic bias.
   557→///
   558→/// Cost: ~50 nanoseconds per correction (pure arithmetic, no LLM).
   559→/// This is the mechanism that makes predictive foraging cost-effective —
   560→/// the correction is free, but the learning is real.
   561→pub struct ResidualCorrector {
   562→    /// Mean bias per (model, category) pair.
   563→    biases: DashMap<(String, String), ExponentialMovingAverage>,
   564→}
   565→
   566→impl ResidualCorrector {
   567→    /// Apply bias correction to a raw prediction.
   568→    pub fn correct(&self, prediction: &mut Prediction) {
   569→        let key = (
   570→            prediction.provenance.model_id.clone(),
   571→            prediction.query_id_category(),
   572→        );
   573→        if let Some(bias) = self.biases.get(&key) {
   574→            if let PredictedValue::Numeric(ref mut v) = prediction.value {
   575→                *v -= bias.current();  // subtract estimated bias
   576→            }
   577→        }
   578→    }
   579→
   580→    /// Update bias estimate with a new residual.
   581→    pub fn update(&self, model: &str, category: &str, residual: f64) {
   582→        let key = (model.to_string(), category.to_string());
   583→        self.biases
   584→            .entry(key)
   585→            .or_insert_with(|| ExponentialMovingAverage::new(0.1))
   586→            .value_mut()
   587→            .update(residual);
   588→    }
   589→}
   590→```
   591→
   592→The cost profile is critical: 50 nanoseconds per correction means 1,000 corrections per day per agent costs effectively nothing. This is what makes the predictive foraging loop viable at Gamma frequency (~5-15s) — corrections happen in microseconds, not milliseconds.
   593→
   594→---
   595→
   596→## CalibrationTracker — Per-model accuracy tracking
   597→
   598→The `CalibrationTracker` aggregates prediction accuracy across (model, task_category) pairs, enabling bias-aware routing:
   599→
   600→```rust
   601→/// Tracks prediction calibration per (model, task_category) pair.
   602→///
   603→/// The key insight: different models have different biases on different
   604→/// task categories. GPT-4 might overestimate test pass rates but
   605→/// underestimate build times. Claude might do the reverse. The
   606→/// CalibrationTracker learns these patterns and feeds them to the
   607→/// ResidualCorrector and CascadeRouter.
   608→pub struct CalibrationTracker {
   609→    /// Per-(model, category) accuracy statistics.
   610→    stats: DashMap<(String, String), CalibrationStats>,
   611→}
   612→
   613→pub struct CalibrationStats {
   614→    /// Exponential moving average of residuals (bias estimate).
   615→    pub mean_residual: ExponentialMovingAverage,
   616→
   617→    /// Exponential moving average of absolute residuals (accuracy estimate).
   618→    pub mean_absolute_error: ExponentialMovingAverage,
   619→
   620→    /// Count of resolved predictions in this category.
   621→    pub count: u64,
   622→
   623→    /// Recent accuracy trend (positive = improving, negative = degrading).
   624→    pub trend: f64,
   625→
   626→    /// Interval calibration: fraction of outcomes within prediction intervals.
   627→    pub interval_coverage: ExponentialMovingAverage,
   628→}
   629→```
   630→
   631→On-chain (Korai), the CalibrationTracker is shared across all agents. A new agent importing the collective calibration starts with pre-learned biases for every model-category pair the collective has encountered. This is the concrete mechanism behind the 31.6× faster calibration heuristic (see `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md` §VI).
   632→
   633→---
   634→
   635→## Implementing a custom Oracle
   636→
   637→Adding prediction capability for a new domain requires implementing the `Oracle` trait:
   638→
   639→```rust
   640→/// Example: a deployment oracle that predicts deployment success probability.
   641→pub struct DeploymentOracle {
   642→    history: Arc<PredictionStore>,
   643→    corrector: Arc<ResidualCorrector>,
   644→}
   645→
   646→#[async_trait]
   647→impl Oracle for DeploymentOracle {
   648→    async fn predict(
   649→        &self,
   650→        query: &OracleQuery,
   651→        ctx: &Context,
   652→    ) -> Result<Prediction> {
   653→        let payload = query.payload.as_operations()?;
   654→
   655→        // Gather features: recent deployment history, change size, time of day
   656→        let features = self.extract_features(payload, ctx).await?;
   657→
   658→        // Base prediction from historical success rate
   659→        let mut prediction = Prediction {
   660→            id: ContentHash::compute(&query, &features),
   661→            query_id: query.id,
   662→            value: PredictedValue::Probability(features.base_success_rate),
   663→            confidence: features.sample_confidence,
   664→            interval: Some(PredictionInterval {
   665→                lower: features.base_success_rate - features.std_dev,
   666→                upper: (features.base_success_rate + features.std_dev).min(1.0),
   667→                coverage: 0.68,  // 1-sigma interval
   668→            }),
   669→            created_at_ms: now_ms(),
   670→            resolve_by_ms: now_ms() + payload.expected_duration_ms,
   671→            provenance: PredictionProvenance::local("deployment-oracle"),
   672→            lineage: features.source_engrams,
   673→            outcome: None,
   674→        };
   675→
   676→        // Apply bias correction from CalibrationTracker
   677→        self.corrector.correct(&mut prediction);
   678→
   679→        Ok(prediction)
   680→    }
   681→
   682→    async fn evaluate(
   683→        &self,
   684→        prediction: &Prediction,
   685→        outcome: &Engram,
   686→    ) -> Result<PredictionAccuracy> {
   687→        let actual_success = outcome.tag("deployment_success")
   688→            .map(|v| v == "true")
   689→            .unwrap_or(false);
   690→
   691→        let predicted_prob = prediction.value.as_probability()?;
   692→        let actual_value = if actual_success { 1.0 } else { 0.0 };
   693→
   694→        let accuracy = PredictionAccuracy {
   695→            prediction_id: prediction.id,
   696→            outcome_id: outcome.id,
   697→            accuracy: 1.0 - (predicted_prob - actual_value).abs(),
   698→            residual: predicted_prob - actual_value,
   699→            interval_hit: prediction.interval.as_ref().map(|i| {
   700→                actual_value >= i.lower && actual_value <= i.upper
   701→            }),
   702→            resolution_lag_ms: outcome.created_at_ms - prediction.created_at_ms,
   703→            domain: OracleDomain::Operations,
   704→            category: "deployment".to_string(),
   705→        };
   706→
   707→        // Update ResidualCorrector
   708→        self.corrector.update(
   709→            &prediction.provenance.model_id,
   710→            "deployment",
   711→            accuracy.residual,
   712→        );
   713→
   714→        Ok(accuracy)
   715→    }
   716→}
   717→```
   718→
   719→This pattern — predict, resolve, correct, repeat — is the same regardless of domain. The Oracle trait's simplicity (2 methods) hides substantial implementation depth in the supporting types (`PredictionStore`, `ResidualCorrector`, `CalibrationTracker`), following Ousterhout's deep module principle.

1→# Chain Oracles — On-Chain Technical Analysis Primitives
     2→
     3→> The chain domain is where TA originated. Chain oracles implement the universal Oracle trait with blockchain-specific state variables, verification mechanisms, and adversarial threat models.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [01-oracle-trait](./01-oracle-trait.md) for the Oracle trait, [00-vision-ta-generalized](./00-vision-ta-generalized.md) for generalization rationale
    10→**Key sources**: `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §4, `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/00-witness-as-technical-analyst.md`, `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/07-defi-native-technical-analysis.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## ChainOracle — Implementation overview
    15→
    16→The `ChainOracle` is the first and most mature Oracle implementation. It wraps traditional financial TA primitives (moving averages, RSI, Bollinger bands) alongside DeFi-native indicators (concentrated liquidity shape analysis, funding rates, yield term structures) into the universal Oracle trait interface:
    17→
    18→```rust
    19→pub struct ChainOracle {
    20→    /// Connection to chain data (via roko-chain ChainClient).
    21→    client: Arc<dyn ChainClient>,
    22→
    23→    /// Historical price/volume/liquidity data cache.
    24→    market_data: Arc<MarketDataCache>,
    25→
    26→    /// DeFi-native indicator engine.
    27→    defi_indicators: Arc<DeFiIndicatorEngine>,
    28→
    29→    /// Prediction persistence and tracking.
    30→    prediction_store: Arc<PredictionStore>,
    31→
    32→    /// Bias correction from collective calibration.
    33→    corrector: Arc<ResidualCorrector>,
    34→
    35→    /// Per-(model, category) accuracy tracking.
    36→    calibration: Arc<CalibrationTracker>,
    37→}
    38→
    39→#[async_trait]
    40→impl Oracle for ChainOracle {
    41→    async fn predict(
    42→        &self,
    43→        query: &OracleQuery,
    44→        ctx: &Context,
    45→    ) -> Result<Prediction> {
    46→        let chain_payload = query.payload.as_chain()?;
    47→
    48→        match chain_payload.metric {
    49→            ChainMetric::Price => self.predict_price(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    50→            ChainMetric::Gas => self.predict_gas(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    51→            ChainMetric::Volatility => self.predict_volatility(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    52→            ChainMetric::LiquidityDepth => self.predict_liquidity(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    53→            ChainMetric::MevOpportunity => self.predict_mev(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    54→            ChainMetric::ProtocolHealth => self.predict_protocol_health(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    55→            ChainMetric::FundingRate => self.predict_funding(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    56→            ChainMetric::YieldSpread => self.predict_yield(chain_payload, ctx).await,
    57→        }
    58→    }
    59→
    60→    async fn evaluate(
    61→        &self,
    62→        prediction: &Prediction,
    63→        outcome: &Engram,
    64→    ) -> Result<PredictionAccuracy> {
    65→        // Chain outcomes are deterministic — the blockchain state IS the ground truth.
    66→        // Extract actual value from the outcome Engram (block data, DEX state, etc.)
    67→        let actual = self.extract_chain_outcome(outcome)?;
    68→        let accuracy = self.compute_accuracy(prediction, &actual);
    69→
    70→        // Feed back to ResidualCorrector
    71→        self.corrector.update(
    72→            &prediction.provenance.model_id,
    73→            &accuracy.category,
    74→            accuracy.residual,
    75→        );
    76→
    77→        Ok(accuracy)
    78→    }
    79→}
    80→```
    81→
    82→### Verification mechanism: blockchain finality
    83→
    84→Chain oracles have the strongest verification mechanism of any domain — the blockchain itself provides deterministic, tamper-proof ground truth. When a prediction about ETH price is made, the actual price at the predicted block height is an indisputable fact. This makes chain oracles ideal for calibrating the entire prediction system, since the feedback signal has zero noise.
    85→
    86→---
    87→
    88→## Traditional TA primitives
    89→
    90→These are the financial TA indicators adapted for on-chain data. Each operates as a sub-predictor within the `ChainOracle`:
    91→
    92→### Price prediction
    93→
    94→```rust
    95→/// Moving average family: SMA, EMA, WMA, DEMA, TEMA.
    96→pub struct MovingAveragePredictor {
    97→    /// Window sizes (e.g., [7, 25, 99] for short/medium/long term).
    98→    windows: Vec<usize>,
    99→    /// Type of moving average.
   100→    ma_type: MovingAverageType,
   101→}
   102→
   103→/// Bollinger Bands: mean ± k*σ for dynamic support/resistance.
   104→/// Standard: 20-period SMA with k=2 (captures 95% of price action).
   105→pub struct BollingerBandPredictor {
   106→    period: usize,
   107→    num_std_dev: f64,
   108→}
   109→
   110→/// Relative Strength Index (Wilder, 1978).
   111→/// RSI > 70 → overbought → predict mean reversion.
   112→/// RSI < 30 → oversold → predict recovery.
   113→/// In crypto, thresholds are often shifted (80/20) due to stronger trends.
   114→pub struct RsiPredictor {
   115→    period: usize,
   116→    overbought_threshold: f64,
   117→    oversold_threshold: f64,
   118→}
   119→
   120→/// MACD (Moving Average Convergence/Divergence).
   121→/// Signal line crossovers predict trend changes.
   122→/// Histogram divergence from price predicts momentum shifts.
   123→pub struct MacdPredictor {
   124→    fast_period: usize,   // typically 12
   125→    slow_period: usize,   // typically 26
   126→    signal_period: usize, // typically 9
   127→}
   128→```
   129→
   130→### Volatility estimation
   131→
   132→```rust
   133→/// Realized volatility from historical price data.
   134→/// Uses Garman-Klass estimator (more efficient than close-to-close)
   135→/// when OHLC data is available.
   136→pub struct VolatilityPredictor {
   137→    /// Lookback window for volatility estimation.
   138→    window: usize,
   139→    /// Estimator type.
   140→    estimator: VolatilityEstimator,
   141→}
   142→
   143→pub enum VolatilityEstimator {
   144→    /// Close-to-close: σ = std(ln(P_t/P_{t-1}))
   145→    CloseToClose,
   146→    /// Garman-Klass (1980): uses OHLC for 5-8x efficiency gain.
   147→    GarmanKlass,
   148→    /// Parkinson (1980): uses high-low range.
   149→    Parkinson,
   150→    /// Yang-Zhang (2000): combines overnight and trading hour volatility.
   151→    YangZhang,
   152→}
   153→```
   154→
   155→### Gas price forecasting
   156→
   157→```rust
   158→/// Gas prediction using block-level fee data (EIP-1559 base fee dynamics).
   159→///
   160→/// The base fee follows a deterministic formula:
   161→///   base_fee_next = base_fee * (1 + 0.125 * (gas_used - gas_target) / gas_target)
   162→///
   163→/// But the PRIORITY fee is market-driven and requires prediction.
   164→/// We use exponential smoothing + day-of-week/hour-of-day seasonality.
   165→pub struct GasPredictor {
   166→    /// Base fee model (deterministic from block data).
   167→    base_fee_model: BaseFeeModel,
   168→    /// Priority fee model (statistical, requires prediction).
   169→    priority_fee_model: PriorityFeeModel,
   170→    /// Seasonal adjustment factors.
   171→    seasonality: SeasonalityModel,
   172→}
   173→```
   174→
   175→Gas prediction is a T0 probe — it runs at Gamma frequency with no LLM cost. The base fee is deterministic from EIP-1559 mechanics; only the priority fee requires statistical prediction.
   176→
   177→---
   178→
   179→## DeFi-native indicators
   180→
   181→These indicators have no traditional finance equivalent. They arise from the unique mechanics of decentralized protocols:
   182→
   183→### Concentrated liquidity shape analysis (Uniswap v3+)
   184→
   185→```rust
   186→/// Analyzes the distribution of liquidity across price ticks.
   187→/// Concentrated liquidity creates a "liquidity landscape" that reveals
   188→/// market maker expectations about future price ranges.
   189→pub struct ConcentratedLiquidityAnalyzer {
   190→    /// Pool address and chain.
   191→    pool: PoolAddress,
   192→
   193→    /// Indicators computed from tick-level data.
   194→    indicators: ConcentratedLiquidityIndicators,
   195→}
   196→
   197→pub struct ConcentratedLiquidityIndicators {
   198→    /// Tick asymmetry: ratio of liquidity above vs. below current price.
   199→    /// High asymmetry → market expects directional move.
   200→    /// Computed as: sum(liquidity_above) / sum(liquidity_below).
   201→    pub tick_asymmetry: f64,
   202→
   203→    /// Migration velocity: rate at which LPs are repositioning their ranges.
   204→    /// High velocity → market makers expect imminent price action.
   205→    /// Computed as: Δ(center_of_mass) / Δt.
   206→    pub migration_velocity: f64,
   207→
   208→    /// Density gaps: contiguous tick ranges with zero liquidity.
   209→    /// Gaps indicate "air pockets" where price can move rapidly.
   210→    /// Each gap is a (lower_tick, upper_tick) range.
   211→    pub density_gaps: Vec<(i32, i32)>,
   212→
   213→    /// Herfindahl-Hirschman Index of liquidity concentration.
   214→    /// Low HHI → diffuse liquidity (high resilience).
   215→    /// High HHI → concentrated liquidity (fragile, LP-dependent).
   216→    pub hhi: f64,
   217→
   218→    /// JIT (Just-In-Time) liquidity fraction: percentage of liquidity
   219→    /// added and removed within the same block.
   220→    /// High JIT → sophisticated MEV activity, higher execution risk.
   221→    pub jit_fraction: f64,
   222→}
   223→```
   224→
   225→These indicators are unique to DeFi and have no TradFi equivalent. They provide structural information about execution costs that traditional order book analysis cannot capture.
   226→
   227→### Lending market indicators
   228→
   229→```rust
   230→/// Indicators derived from lending protocol state (Aave, Compound, etc.).
   231→pub struct LendingIndicators {
   232→    /// Utilization rate: borrowed / total_supplied.
   233→    /// Above optimal (typically 80%) → interest rates spike nonlinearly.
   234→    pub utilization_rate: f64,
   235→
   236→    /// Liquidation proximity: distribution of borrower health factors.
   237→    /// Concentration near 1.0 → cascade risk.
   238→    pub liquidation_proximity: LiquidationDistribution,
   239→
   240→    /// Supply/borrow rate spread: lender yield vs. borrower cost.
   241→    /// Narrowing spread → protocol stress.
   242→    pub rate_spread: f64,
   243→
   244→    /// Flash loan volume trend: elevated flash loans often precede
   245→    /// governance attacks or liquidation cascades.
   246→    pub flash_loan_trend: f64,
   247→}
   248→```
   249→
   250→### Perpetual funding rates
   251→
   252→```rust
   253→/// Funding rate indicators for perpetual futures (dYdX, GMX, etc.).
   254→pub struct FundingRateIndicators {
   255→    /// Current funding rate (annualized).
   256→    /// Positive → longs pay shorts → market is long-biased.
   257→    /// Negative → shorts pay longs → market is short-biased.
   258→    pub current_rate: f64,
   259→
   260→    /// Funding rate vs. 30-day moving average.
   261→    /// Extreme deviation → mean reversion likely.
   262→    pub deviation_from_mean: f64,
   263→
   264→    /// Open interest trend: rising OI + positive funding → leveraged long squeeze risk.
   265→    pub open_interest_trend: f64,
   266→
   267→    /// Basis: spot price - perpetual price.
   268→    /// Persistent negative basis → market structure stress.
   269→    pub basis: f64,
   270→}
   271→```
   272→
   273→### Yield term structure
   274→
   275→```rust
   276→/// Yield curves across DeFi lending protocols.
   277→/// An inverted yield curve (short rates > long rates) signals stress,
   278→/// analogous to inverted yield curves in TradFi bond markets.
   279→pub struct YieldTermStructure {
   280→    /// Rates at standard maturities.
   281→    pub rates: BTreeMap<Duration, f64>,
   282→
   283→    /// Slope: long_rate - short_rate.
   284→    /// Positive slope → normal (compensation for duration risk).
   285→    /// Negative slope → inverted (stress signal).
   286→    pub slope: f64,
   287→
   288→    /// Curvature: 2 * medium_rate - short_rate - long_rate.
   289→    /// High curvature → convexity opportunity.
   290→    pub curvature: f64,
   291→
   292→    /// Rate of slope change: Δslope / Δt.
   293→    /// Rapid flattening → potential regime change.
   294→    pub slope_velocity: f64,
   295→}
