   171→    pub percentage_codebook: QuantizedCodebook,
   172→}
   173→```
   174→
   175→### Quantized numeric encoding
   176→
   177→Continuous values are quantized into discrete HDC vectors using thermometer encoding:
   178→
   179→```rust
   180→/// Quantized codebook for encoding continuous values as HDC vectors.
   181→///
   182→/// Uses thermometer encoding: for value in range [min, max] with N levels,
   183→/// the encoded vector is a blend of the level vectors weighted by proximity.
   184→///
   185→/// This preserves ordinal relationships: encode(3.0) is more similar to
   186→/// encode(4.0) than to encode(100.0).
   187→pub struct QuantizedCodebook {
   188→    /// Level vectors, one per quantization level.
   189→    levels: Vec<HdcVector>,
   190→    /// Value range.
   191→    min: f64,
   192→    max: f64,
   193→    /// Number of quantization levels.
   194→    n_levels: usize,
   195→}
   196→
   197→impl QuantizedCodebook {
   198→    /// Encode a continuous value as an HDC vector.
   199→    pub fn encode(&self, value: f64) -> HdcVector {
   200→        let normalized = (value - self.min) / (self.max - self.min);
   201→        let level = (normalized * self.n_levels as f64).clamp(0.0, (self.n_levels - 1) as f64);
   202→        let lower = level.floor() as usize;
   203→        let upper = (lower + 1).min(self.n_levels - 1);
   204→        let weight = level - lower as f64;
   205→
   206→        // Interpolate between adjacent level vectors
   207→        self.levels[lower].weighted_bundle(&self.levels[upper], 1.0 - weight, weight)
   208→    }
   209→}
   210→```
   211→
   212→### Pattern composition queries
   213→
   214→Complex TA patterns are composed from primitive encodings:
   215→
   216→```rust
   217→/// Example: encode "a large ETH swap on Uniswap with high slippage"
   218→///
   219→/// This creates a single 10,240-bit vector that captures the
   220→/// full semantic content of the pattern.
   221→pub fn encode_swap_pattern(
   222→    codebook: &DeFiCodebook,
   223→    token_pair: &str,
   224→    amount: f64,
   225→    slippage: f64,
   226→    protocol: &str,
   227→) -> HdcVector {
   228→    let type_binding = codebook.swap.clone();
   229→    let pair_binding = codebook.token_pair.xor(&codebook.encode_string(token_pair));
   230→    let amount_binding = codebook.amount.xor(&codebook.amount_codebook.encode(amount));
   231→    let slip_binding = codebook.slippage.xor(&codebook.percentage_codebook.encode(slippage));
   232→    let proto_binding = codebook.protocol.xor(&codebook.encode_string(protocol));
   233→
   234→    HdcVector::bundle(&[type_binding, pair_binding, amount_binding, slip_binding, proto_binding])
   235→}
   236→
   237→/// Query: "find all patterns similar to large swaps with high slippage"
   238→pub fn query_similar_patterns(
   239→    pattern: &HdcVector,
   240→    memory: &[HdcVector],
   241→    threshold: f64,  // typically 0.526 per refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md §XIII
   242→) -> Vec<(usize, f64)> {
   243→    memory.iter()
   244→        .enumerate()
   245→        .filter_map(|(i, m)| {
   246→            let sim = pattern.hamming_similarity(m);
   247→            if sim > threshold { Some((i, sim)) } else { None }
   248→        })
   249→        .collect()
   250→}
   251→```
   252→
   253→---
   254→
   255→## Cross-domain pattern matching
   256→
   257→The deepest value of HDC for TA is cross-domain insight resonance (see `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md` §XIII). When a coding oracle encodes "high churn in auth module" and a chain oracle encodes "high volatility in ETH/USDC," the HDC vectors are structurally similar because both encode `BIND(high_uncertainty, critical_subsystem)`:
   258→
   259→```rust
   260→/// Cross-domain insight resonance detection.
   261→///
   262→/// Continuously cross-correlate new Engrams against the HDC knowledge
   263→/// base across ALL domains. When similarity exceeds threshold (0.526),
   264→/// emit a cross-domain insight.
   265→pub fn detect_cross_domain_resonance(
   266→    new_engram: &Engram,
   267→    all_domain_knowledge: &[Engram],
   268→    threshold: f64,
   269→) -> Vec<CrossDomainInsight> {
   270→    let new_hv = new_engram.hdc_vector();
   271→    let new_domain = new_engram.domain();
   272→
   273→    all_domain_knowledge.iter()
   274→        .filter(|k| k.domain() != new_domain)  // cross-domain only
   275→        .filter_map(|k| {
   276→            let sim = new_hv.hamming_similarity(&k.hdc_vector());
   277→            if sim > threshold {
   278→                Some(CrossDomainInsight {
   279→                    source_engram: new_engram.id,
   280→                    target_engram: k.id,
   281→                    source_domain: new_domain.clone(),
   282→                    target_domain: k.domain().clone(),
   283→                    similarity: sim,
   284→                    description: format!(
   285→                        "Pattern in {} domain has structural similarity ({:.3}) to pattern in {} domain",
   286→                        new_domain, sim, k.domain()
   287→                    ),
   288→                })
   289→            } else {
   290→                None
   291→            }
   292→        })
   293→        .collect()
   294→}
   295→```
   296→
   297→The threshold of 0.526 comes from information-theoretic analysis: with 10,240-bit vectors, random vectors have expected Hamming similarity of 0.500 with standard deviation of ~0.005. A threshold of 0.526 (5σ above chance) ensures that detected similarities are statistically significant.
   298→

   340→---
   341→
   342→## HDC pattern memory — The pattern store
   343→
   344→```rust
   345→/// HDC pattern memory for TA.
   346→///
   347→/// Stores encoded TA patterns as a searchable vector space.
   348→/// Queries return the most similar patterns, enabling:
   349→/// - "Have I seen this pattern before?" (recall)
   350→/// - "What patterns are similar to this?" (analogy)
   351→/// - "What patterns from other domains match?" (transfer)
   352→pub struct PatternStore {
   353→    /// All stored patterns indexed by domain.
   354→    patterns: HashMap<OracleDomain, Vec<StoredPattern>>,
   355→
   356→    /// Cross-domain index for resonance detection.
   357→    cross_domain_index: Vec<(OracleDomain, HdcVector, ContentHash)>,
   358→}
   359→
   360→pub struct StoredPattern {
   361→    /// The encoded HDC vector.
   362→    pub vector: HdcVector,
   363→
   364→    /// The Engram this pattern was derived from.
   365→    pub source_engram: ContentHash,
   366→
   367→    /// The outcome when this pattern last occurred.
   368→    pub outcome: Option<PredictionOutcome>,
   369→
   370→    /// How often this pattern has been observed.
   371→    pub frequency: u64,
   372→
   373→    /// Reliability: how often did this pattern's predicted outcome match actual?
   374→    pub reliability: f64,
   375→}
   376→
   377→impl PatternStore {
   378→    /// Find the K most similar patterns to a query.
   379→    ///
   380→    /// Cost: O(N) with N = total patterns, ~13ns per comparison.
   381→    /// For 100K patterns: ~1.3ms. For 1M: ~13ms.
   382→    pub fn find_similar(
   383→        &self,
   384→        query: &HdcVector,
   385→        domain: Option<&OracleDomain>,
   386→        k: usize,
   387→        threshold: f64,
   388→    ) -> Vec<(f64, &StoredPattern)> {
   389→        let candidates = match domain {
   390→            Some(d) => self.patterns.get(d).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[]),
   391→            None => &self.cross_domain_index.iter()
   392→                .map(|(_, v, _)| v)
   393→                .collect::<Vec<_>>(),  // simplified
   394→        };
   395→
   396→        let mut results: Vec<_> = candidates.iter()
   397→            .map(|p| (query.hamming_similarity(&p.vector), p))
   398→            .filter(|(sim, _)| *sim > threshold)
   399→            .collect();
   400→
   401→        results.sort_by(|a, b| b.0.partial_cmp(&a.0).unwrap());
   402→        results.truncate(k);
   403→        results
   404→    }
   405→}
   406→```
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## Integration with Dreams — Pattern consolidation
   411→
   412→During Delta-frequency consolidation (Dreams), the HDC pattern store undergoes three operations:
   413→
   414→1. **NREM replay**: High-value patterns are replayed and their reliability scores updated based on accumulated outcomes.
   415→2. **REM recombination**: Novel pattern compositions are generated by bundling existing patterns with random perturbation (mutation via XOR with noise vector).
   416→3. **Pruning**: Patterns with reliability below 0.3 after 10+ observations are removed.
   417→
   418→```rust
   419→/// Dream consolidation for HDC pattern store.
   420→pub fn dream_consolidation(store: &mut PatternStore) {
   421→    // NREM: replay high-value patterns
   422→    for pattern in store.high_value_patterns() {
   423→        let updated_reliability = pattern.recompute_reliability();
   424→        pattern.reliability = updated_reliability;
   425→    }
   426→
   427→    // REM: generate novel compositions
   428→    let existing: Vec<&HdcVector> = store.all_vectors().collect();
   429→    for _ in 0..10 {
   430→        let a = existing.choose(&mut rng).unwrap();
   431→        let b = existing.choose(&mut rng).unwrap();
   432→        let novel = a.xor(b).permute(1);  // recombine + shift
   433→        store.add_hypothetical(novel, confidence: 0.2);
   434→    }
   435→
   436→    // Prune: remove unreliable patterns
   437→    store.prune(|p| p.frequency >= 10 && p.reliability < 0.3);
   438→}
   439→```
   440→
   441→---
   442→
   443→## Implementation details
   444→
   445→### Codebook generation algorithm
   446→
   447→Codebooks are generated deterministically from a domain-specific seed. This ensures that all agents sharing a seed share the same vector space, enabling direct cross-agent pattern comparison without alignment.
   448→
   449→```rust
   450→/// Generate a domain-specific HDC codebook deterministically.
   451→///
   452→/// The seed derives from the domain name via SHA-256. Each role vector
   453→/// is drawn from the resulting CSPRNG stream. Because the seed is
   454→/// deterministic, every agent in the same domain produces identical
   455→/// codebooks without coordination.
   456→pub struct CodebookGenerator {
   457→    /// Domain seed (SHA-256 of domain name).
   458→    seed: [u8; 32],
   459→    /// Dimensionality of generated vectors (default: 10_240).
   460→    dim: usize,
   461→}
   462→
   463→impl CodebookGenerator {
   464→    pub fn new(domain: &str, dim: usize) -> Self {
   465→        let seed = sha256(domain.as_bytes());
   466→        Self { seed, dim }
   467→    }
   468→
   469→    /// Generate a role vector at a given index.
   470→    ///
   471→    /// Uses ChaCha20 seeded from `self.seed ++ index.to_le_bytes()`.
   472→    /// Each bit is drawn with P(1) = 0.5 (dense binary).
   473→    pub fn generate_role(&self, index: u32) -> HdcVector {
   474→        let mut key = self.seed.to_vec();
   475→        key.extend_from_slice(&index.to_le_bytes());
   476→        let mut rng = ChaCha20Rng::from_seed(sha256(&key));
   477→        HdcVector::random(&mut rng, self.dim)
   478→    }
   479→
   480→    /// Generate a QuantizedCodebook for a value range.
   481→    ///
   482→    /// Level vectors use thermometer construction:
   483→    ///   level_0 = random base vector
   484→    ///   level_k = level_{k-1} with `flip_count` random bits flipped
   485→    ///
   486→    /// `flip_count = dim / (2 * n_levels)` ensures adjacent levels
   487→    /// have Hamming similarity ~= 1 - 1/(2*n_levels).
   488→    pub fn generate_quantized(
   489→        &self,
   490→        codebook_index: u32,
   491→        n_levels: usize,
   492→        min: f64,
   493→        max: f64,
   494→    ) -> QuantizedCodebook {
   495→        let flip_count = self.dim / (2 * n_levels);
   496→        let base = self.generate_role(codebook_index);
   497→        let mut levels = vec![base];
   498→
   499→        for k in 1..n_levels {
   500→            let prev = &levels[k - 1];
   501→            let mut rng = ChaCha20Rng::from_seed(
   502→                sha256(&[&self.seed[..], &(codebook_index + k as u32).to_le_bytes()].concat())
   503→            );
   504→            let flipped = prev.flip_random_bits(flip_count, &mut rng);
   505→            levels.push(flipped);
   506→        }
   507→
   508→        QuantizedCodebook { levels, min, max, n_levels }
   509→    }
   510→}
   511→```
   512→
   513→**Configuration parameters**:
   514→
   515→| Parameter | Default | Range | Notes |
   516→|---|---|---|---|
   517→| `dim` | 10,240 | 1,024 - 65,536 | Must be multiple of 64 for SIMD alignment. 10,240 = 160 u64 words. |
   518→| `n_levels` (QuantizedCodebook) | 64 | 8 - 256 | More levels = finer granularity, more memory. 64 gives ~1.5% resolution. |
   519→| `flip_count` | `dim / (2 * n_levels)` | derived | Controls similarity between adjacent levels. |
   520→
   521→### QuantizedCodebook::encode() interpolation
   522→
   523→The `encode()` method interpolates between adjacent level vectors via a weighted bundle. The procedure:
   524→
   525→1. Normalize the input value to `[0.0, 1.0]` within the codebook's range.
   526→2. Map to a fractional level index: `level_f = normalized * (n_levels - 1)`.
   527→3. Identify the two bracketing levels: `lower = floor(level_f)`, `upper = lower + 1`.
   528→4. Compute interpolation weight: `w = level_f - lower`.
   529→5. Return `weighted_bundle(levels[lower], levels[upper], 1.0 - w, w)`.
   530→
   531→The weighted bundle for two vectors uses probabilistic bit selection: for each bit position, select from `levels[upper]` with probability `w`, else from `levels[lower]`. This produces a vector whose Hamming similarity to each level is proportional to the interpolation weight.
   532→
   533→**Error handling**: Values outside `[min, max]` are clamped. If `n_levels` is 1, return the single level vector regardless of input. If the codebook is empty (zero levels), return a zero vector and log a warning.
   534→
   535→### Pattern store serialization (CBOR)
   536→
   537→The PatternStore serializes to CBOR (RFC 8949) for compact, schema-flexible persistence:
   538→
   539→```rust
   540→/// CBOR schema for PatternStore persistence.
   541→///
   542→/// Top-level: CBOR map {
   543→///   "version": u32,              // schema version (currently 1)
   544→///   "domains": map {             // keyed by OracleDomain string
   545→///     "<domain>": array [        // array of StoredPattern
   546→///       {
   547→///         "v": bytes(1280),      // HDC vector (10,240 bits = 1,280 bytes)
   548→///         "src": bytes(32),      // source engram ContentHash
   549→///         "out": ?i8,            // outcome: -1 (loss), 0 (neutral), 1 (profit), null
   550→///         "freq": u64,           // observation count
   551→///         "rel": f32,            // reliability [0.0, 1.0]
   552→///       },
   553→///       ...
   554→///     ]
   555→///   },
   556→///   "cross_index": array [       // cross-domain index entries
   557→///     { "d": string, "v": bytes(1280), "h": bytes(32) },
   558→///     ...
   559→///   ]
   560→/// }
   561→///
   562→/// File size estimate: 1,280 bytes per pattern + 45 bytes metadata.
   563→/// 100K patterns ~= 130 MB. 1M patterns ~= 1.3 GB.
   564→pub fn serialize_pattern_store(store: &PatternStore) -> Vec<u8> {
   565→    let mut encoder = CborEncoder::new();
   566→    encoder.map(3);
   567→    encoder.text("version").unsigned(1);
   568→    encoder.text("domains");
   569→    encoder.map(store.patterns.len());
   570→    for (domain, patterns) in &store.patterns {
   571→        encoder.text(&domain.to_string());
   572→        encoder.array(patterns.len());
   573→        for p in patterns {
   574→            encoder.map(5);
   575→            encoder.text("v").bytes(&p.vector.as_bytes());
   576→            encoder.text("src").bytes(&p.source_engram.as_bytes());
   577→            encoder.text("out").optional_i8(p.outcome.map(|o| o as i8));
   578→            encoder.text("freq").unsigned(p.frequency);
   579→            encoder.text("rel").float32(p.reliability as f32);
   580→        }
   581→    }
   582→    // ... cross_index similarly
   583→    encoder.finish()
   584→}
   585→```
   586→
   587→### Similarity threshold calibration
   588→
   589→The default threshold of 0.526 derives from the information-theoretic properties of 10,240-bit BSC vectors. Calibrate it in practice with this procedure:
   590→
   591→1. **Generate null distribution**: Create 10,000 random vector pairs. Compute their Hamming similarities. The distribution should be approximately Gaussian with mean 0.500 and stddev ~0.00494.
   592→2. **Choose significance level**: The default 0.526 corresponds to 5.26 sigma (p < 1e-7). For applications tolerating more false positives, use 0.515 (3 sigma, p < 0.0013).
   593→3. **Validate on held-out data**: Take known-similar pattern pairs from the domain. Compute their similarity distribution. The threshold should separate the null distribution from the true-positive distribution with <1% overlap.
   594→4. **Adjust per domain**: If a domain has noisier encodings (fewer role-filler pairs per pattern), increase the threshold. Rule of thumb: add 0.005 per missing role-filler pair below 5.
   595→
   596→```rust
   597→/// Calibrate similarity threshold for a given vector dimensionality.
   598→///
   599→/// Returns (mean, stddev, suggested_threshold) based on the null distribution.
   600→pub fn calibrate_threshold(dim: usize, sigma_level: f64, n_samples: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64) {
   601→    let mut rng = thread_rng();
   602→    let mut similarities = Vec::with_capacity(n_samples);
   603→    for _ in 0..n_samples {
   604→        let a = HdcVector::random(&mut rng, dim);
   605→        let b = HdcVector::random(&mut rng, dim);
   606→        similarities.push(a.hamming_similarity(&b));
   607→    }
   608→    let mean = similarities.iter().sum::<f64>() / n_samples as f64;
   609→    let variance = similarities.iter().map(|s| (s - mean).powi(2)).sum::<f64>() / n_samples as f64;
   610→    let stddev = variance.sqrt();
   611→    (mean, stddev, mean + sigma_level * stddev)
   612→}
   613→```
   614→
   615→### Cross-domain routing protocol
   616→
   617→When oracles from different domains want to exchange patterns, the routing protocol works as follows:
   618→
   619→```
   620→1. Source oracle encodes a pattern using its domain codebook.
   621→2. Source sends (domain_id, pattern_hv, metadata) to the PatternStore.
   622→3. PatternStore inserts the pattern into the cross_domain_index.
   623→4. Any oracle can query the cross_domain_index with a pattern vector.
   624→5. Matches above threshold are returned with their source domain.
   625→6. The querying oracle decides whether to incorporate the cross-domain match.
   626→```
   627→
   628→No codebook translation is needed because all codebooks share the same vector space (10,240-bit BSC with XOR bind and majority bundle). The cross-domain similarity is structural, not lexical.
   629→
   630→**Integration wiring**: `PatternStore::find_similar()` with `domain: None` searches the cross-domain index. The oracle calls this during its Theta-frequency analysis pass.
   631→
   632→### Pruning rules
   633→
   634→When the pattern count exceeds memory limits, prune according to these rules (applied in order):
   635→
   636→1. **Unreliable patterns**: Remove patterns where `reliability < 0.3` and `frequency >= 10`. These have enough observations to confirm they do not predict well.
   637→2. **Stale patterns**: Remove patterns not matched in the last `max_staleness` duration (default: 72 hours). These are no longer relevant to current market/code conditions.
   638→3. **Redundant patterns**: For patterns with Hamming similarity > 0.95 to each other, keep only the one with higher reliability. This deduplicates near-identical encodings.
   639→4. **LRU eviction**: If the store still exceeds `max_patterns`, remove the least-recently-matched patterns until within budget.
   640→
   641→```rust
   642→/// Configuration for pattern store pruning.
   643→pub struct PruneConfig {
   644→    /// Maximum patterns per domain before pruning triggers.
   645→    pub max_patterns_per_domain: usize,  // default: 100_000
   646→    /// Minimum reliability to survive pruning (with sufficient observations).
   647→    pub min_reliability: f64,            // default: 0.3
   648→    /// Minimum observations before reliability-based pruning applies.
   649→    pub min_frequency: u64,              // default: 10
   650→    /// Maximum time since last match before staleness pruning.
   651→    pub max_staleness: Duration,         // default: 72 hours
   652→    /// Similarity threshold for deduplication.
   653→    pub dedup_threshold: f64,            // default: 0.95
   654→}
   655→```
   656→
   657→### Connection to Dreams: reliability updates
   658→
   659→During Delta-frequency dream consolidation, the `StoredPattern.reliability` field is updated from dream outcomes:
   660→
   661→```
   662→1. Dreams NREM phase replays high-frequency patterns.
   663→2. For each replayed pattern, Dreams evaluates: "If this pattern
   664→   activated now, would the predicted outcome hold?"
   665→3. The evaluation runs the pattern through the current causal model
   666→   (from causal microstructure discovery) and compares the predicted
   667→   outcome to the pattern's stored outcome.
   668→4. If they agree: reliability += 0.05 (capped at 1.0).
   669→5. If they disagree: reliability -= 0.10 (floored at 0.0).
   670→6. During REM recombination, newly generated hypothetical patterns
   671→   start with reliability = 0.2.
   672→```
   673→
   674→The asymmetric update (slower increase, faster decrease) follows the principle that trust is hard to earn and easy to lose. A pattern must consistently agree with the causal model across multiple dream cycles to reach high reliability.
   675→
   676→### Test criteria
   677→
   678→- **Codebook determinism**: Two `CodebookGenerator` instances with the same domain and dim produce identical role vectors.
   679→- **Quantized encoding monotonicity**: For values v1 < v2, `hamming_similarity(encode(v1), encode(v2))` decreases as `|v2 - v1|` increases.
   680→- **Threshold calibration**: The null distribution mean is within 0.001 of 0.500 for dim = 10,240.
   681→- **Cross-domain routing**: A pattern stored by one oracle is retrievable by another oracle via `find_similar(domain: None)`.
   682→- **CBOR round-trip**: `deserialize(serialize(store))` produces an identical PatternStore.
   683→- **Pruning correctness**: After pruning, no pattern violates the configured thresholds.
   684→- **Dream reliability update**: After 10 agreeing dream cycles, reliability reaches >= 0.7. After 5 disagreeing cycles from reliability 0.7, reliability drops to <= 0.2.
   685→
   686→---
   687→
   688→## Academic foundations
   689→
   690→- Kanerva, P. (2009). "Hyperdimensional Computing: An Introduction." *Cognitive Computation*, 1(2), 139-159. — Binary Spatter Code (BSC) foundations.
   691→- Kleyko, D., et al. (2022). "A Survey on Hyperdimensional Computing." *ACM Computing Surveys*, 54(6), 1-51. — Comprehensive HDC survey including performance benchmarks.
   692→- Plate, T. A. (1995). "Holographic Reduced Representations." *IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks*, 6(3), 623-641. — Distributed representations for structured data.
   693→- Frady, E. P., Kleyko, D., & Sommer, F. T. (2018). "A Theory of Sequence Indexing and Working Memory in Recurrent Neural Networks." *Neural Computation*, 30(6), 1449-1513. — Temporal encoding via permutation.
   694→- Rachkovskij, D. A. (2001). "Representation and Processing of Structures with Binary Sparse Distributed Codes." *IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering*, 13(2), 261-276. — Sparse distributed memory for pattern matching.
   695→- Lacaux, C., et al. (2021). "Sleep onset is a creative sweet spot." *Science Advances*, 7(50). — Creativity during N1 sleep, motivating hypnagogia-based pattern generation.
   696→
   697→---
   698→
   699→## Cross-references
   700→
   701→- See [06-neuro](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for HDC fundamentals and the Neuro knowledge store
   702→- See [08-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md](./08-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md) for evolutionary dynamics of HDC-encoded signals
   703→- See [10-predictive-geometry-and-resonant-patterns.md](./10-predictive-geometry-and-resonant-patterns.md) for TDA + HDC pattern composition
   704→- See [12-somatic-ta-and-emergent-multiscale.md](./12-somatic-ta-and-emergent-multiscale.md) for somatic markers as HDC bindings
   705→

1→# Spectral Liquidity Manifolds
     2→
     3→> Riemannian geometry applied to DeFi execution costs. Liquidity pools form a curved manifold where geodesics are optimal execution paths and curvature indicates structural risk.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [02-chain-oracles](./02-chain-oracles.md) for chain TA primitives, [06-hyperdimensional-ta](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) for pattern encoding
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/02-spectral-liquidity-manifolds.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→DeFi execution is not a simple price lookup. Every trade traverses a **liquidity landscape** where costs depend on pool depth, gas fees, timing, and opportunity costs. These costs vary non-linearly with trade size, time, and market conditions. The spectral liquidity manifold framework models this landscape using Riemannian geometry — the mathematical framework for curved spaces.
    17→
    18→The core insight: a liquidity landscape is a curved space. The metric tensor encodes execution costs at each point. Geodesics (shortest paths on the manifold) are the optimal execution routes. Curvature measures structural stability — positive curvature (like a sphere) means the market self-corrects; negative curvature (like a saddle) means small perturbations amplify.
    19→
    20→While this framework is natively chain-specific (DeFi liquidity is the domain), the mathematical structure generalizes. Any domain with spatially varying costs — CI/CD pipeline routing, research strategy selection, resource allocation — can be modeled as a manifold with a cost metric.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## The state manifold
    25→
    26→The liquidity manifold is a smooth differentiable manifold M where each point represents a DeFi portfolio state:
    27→
    28→```rust
    29→/// A point on the liquidity manifold.
    30→///
    31→/// Coordinates represent the portfolio state:
    32→/// (asset_0_balance, asset_1_balance, ..., asset_n_balance, liquidity_position_params)
    33→///
    34→/// The manifold dimension equals the number of independent state variables.
    35→pub struct ManifoldPoint {
    36→    /// Portfolio state coordinates.
    37→    pub coordinates: Vec<f64>,
    38→
    39→    /// Which protocol/pool this point belongs to.
    40→    pub protocol: ProtocolId,
    41→
    42→    /// Timestamp of the state observation.
    43→    pub timestamp_ms: i64,
    44→}
    45→
    46→/// A tangent vector at a point on the manifold.
    47→///
    48→/// Tangent vectors represent infinitesimal trades — small changes
    49→/// in portfolio state. The metric tensor measures the "cost" of
    50→/// moving in each direction.
    51→pub struct TangentVector {
    52→    /// Components of the tangent vector in local coordinates.
    53→    pub components: Vec<f64>,
    54→
    55→    /// The point where this tangent vector is attached.
    56→    pub base_point: ManifoldPoint,
    57→}
    58→```
    59→
    60→### The metric tensor
    61→
    62→The metric tensor g_ij defines the cost of moving from one state to another. It encodes four types of execution cost:
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→/// The metric tensor at a point on the liquidity manifold.
    66→///
    67→/// g_ij = slippage_ij + gas_ij + time_ij + opportunity_ij
    68→///
    69→/// Each component captures a different execution cost:
    70→/// - slippage: price impact of the trade
    71→/// - gas: transaction fee on the blockchain
    72→/// - time: cost of waiting for confirmation
    73→/// - opportunity: cost of capital locked during execution
    74→pub struct MetricTensor {
    75→    /// The n×n matrix of metric components.
    76→    pub components: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
    77→
    78→    /// Dimension of the manifold.
    79→    pub dim: usize,
    80→}
    81→
    82→impl MetricTensor {
    83→    /// Compute the metric tensor at a given point.
    84→    ///
    85→    /// This requires querying the current liquidity state of the
    86→    /// underlying pools and computing the cost gradient in each direction.
    87→    pub fn compute(point: &ManifoldPoint, pools: &[PoolState]) -> Self {
    88→        let dim = point.coordinates.len();
    89→        let mut g = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
    90→
    91→        for i in 0..dim {
    92→            for j in 0..dim {
    93→                // Slippage component: d²(price_impact) / d(x_i)d(x_j)
    94→                g[i][j] += slippage_metric(point, i, j, pools);
    95→
    96→                // Gas component: constant per transaction, amortized
    97→                g[i][j] += gas_metric(point, i, j);
    98→
    99→                // Time component: confirmation time scaled by urgency
   100→                g[i][j] += time_metric(point, i, j);
   101→
   102→                // Opportunity component: capital lockup cost
   103→                g[i][j] += opportunity_metric(point, i, j);
   104→            }
   105→        }
   106→
   107→        MetricTensor { components: g, dim }
   108→    }
   109→
   110→    /// Inner product of two tangent vectors using this metric.
   111→    /// This gives the "cost squared" of moving in direction v.
   112→    pub fn inner_product(&self, v: &TangentVector, w: &TangentVector) -> f64 {
   113→        let mut result = 0.0;
   114→        for i in 0..self.dim {
   115→            for j in 0..self.dim {
   116→                result += self.components[i][j] * v.components[i] * w.components[j];
   117→            }
   118→        }
   119→        result
   120→    }
   121→
   122→    /// Length of a tangent vector: the "cost" of an infinitesimal trade.
   123→    pub fn norm(&self, v: &TangentVector) -> f64 {
   124→        self.inner_product(v, v).sqrt()
   125→    }
   126→}
   127→```
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## Christoffel symbols — How the manifold curves
   132→
   133→The Christoffel symbols Γ^k_ij describe how the coordinate system curves — they are the "gravitational field" of the liquidity manifold:
   134→
   135→```rust
   136→/// Christoffel symbols of the second kind: Γ^k_ij.
   137→///
   138→/// These describe how parallel transport along the manifold
   139→/// rotates tangent vectors. In financial terms: they describe
   140→/// how the cost of a trade changes as you move through the
   141→/// liquidity landscape.
   142→///
   143→/// Γ^k_ij = (1/2) g^{kl} (∂_i g_{jl} + ∂_j g_{il} - ∂_l g_{ij})
   144→pub struct ChristoffelSymbols {
   145→    /// Γ^k_ij stored as [k][i][j].
   146→    pub components: Vec<Vec<Vec<f64>>>,
   147→    pub dim: usize,
   148→}
   149→
   150→impl ChristoffelSymbols {
   151→    /// Compute Christoffel symbols from the metric tensor.
   152→    ///
   153→    /// Requires: metric tensor and its first derivatives at the point.
   154→    /// Uses finite differences for derivatives when analytical forms
   155→    /// are not available.
   156→    pub fn compute(
   157→        metric: &MetricTensor,
   158→        metric_derivatives: &[MetricTensor],  // ∂_l g_{ij} for each l
   159→    ) -> Self {
   160→        let dim = metric.dim;
   161→        let g_inv = metric.inverse();
   162→        let mut gamma = vec![vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim]; dim];
   163→
   164→        for k in 0..dim {
   165→            for i in 0..dim {
   166→                for j in 0..dim {
   167→                    for l in 0..dim {
   168→                        gamma[k][i][j] += 0.5 * g_inv.components[k][l] * (
   169→                            metric_derivatives[i].components[j][l] +
   170→                            metric_derivatives[j].components[i][l] -
   171→                            metric_derivatives[l].components[i][j]
   172→                        );
   173→                    }
   174→                }
   175→            }
   176→        }
   177→
   178→        ChristoffelSymbols { components: gamma, dim }
   179→    }
   180→}
   181→```
   182→
   183→---
   184→
   185→## Geodesics — Optimal execution paths
   186→
   187→A geodesic on the liquidity manifold is the path that minimizes total execution cost. Finding the optimal route for a DeFi trade is equivalent to solving the geodesic equation:
   188→
   189→```rust
   190→/// Compute the geodesic (optimal execution path) between two portfolio states.
   191→///
   192→/// The geodesic equation:
   193→///   d²x^k/dt² + Γ^k_ij (dx^i/dt)(dx^j/dt) = 0
   194→///
   195→/// Solved numerically using 4th-order Runge-Kutta integration.
   196→///
   197→/// The resulting path minimizes total execution cost (slippage + gas + time + opportunity).
   198→pub fn compute_geodesic(
   199→    start: &ManifoldPoint,
   200→    end: &ManifoldPoint,
   201→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   202→    n_steps: usize,
   203→) -> Vec<ManifoldPoint> {
   204→    let dt = 1.0 / n_steps as f64;
   205→    let mut path = vec![start.clone()];
   206→    let mut velocity = initial_velocity(start, end, manifold);
   207→
   208→    for _ in 0..n_steps {
   209→        let point = path.last().unwrap();
   210→        let christoffel = manifold.christoffel_at(point);
   211→
   212→        // Geodesic equation: acceleration = -Γ^k_ij v^i v^j
   213→        let mut acceleration = vec![0.0; manifold.dim];
   214→        for k in 0..manifold.dim {
   215→            for i in 0..manifold.dim {
   216→                for j in 0..manifold.dim {
   217→                    acceleration[k] -= christoffel.components[k][i][j]
   218→                        * velocity.components[i]
   219→                        * velocity.components[j];
   220→                }
   221→            }
   222→        }
   223→
   224→        // RK4 integration step
   225→        let (new_point, new_velocity) = rk4_step(point, &velocity, &acceleration, dt);
   226→        velocity = new_velocity;
   227→        path.push(new_point);
   228→    }
   229→
   230→    path
   231→}
   232→```
   233→
   234→### Geodesic interpretation
   235→
   236→| Geodesic property | Financial meaning |
   237→|---|---|
   238→| **Geodesic length** | Total execution cost of the optimal path |
   239→| **Geodesic curvature** | How far the optimal path deviates from a "straight" trade |
   240→| **Conjugate points** | Points where alternative optimal paths exist (arbitrage opportunities) |
   241→| **Geodesic incompleteness** | Regions where no optimal path exists (illiquid, fragmented markets) |
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→## Curvature — Structural risk
   246→
   247→The Riemann curvature tensor and its contractions reveal structural properties of the liquidity landscape:
   248→
   249→### Riemann curvature tensor
   250→
   251→```rust
   252→/// Riemann curvature tensor: R^l_{ijk}.
   253→///
   254→/// Measures the failure of parallel transport around an infinitesimal loop.
   255→/// In financial terms: how much does the cost structure change as you
   256→/// move around in the liquidity landscape?
   257→pub struct RiemannTensor {
   258→    pub components: Vec<Vec<Vec<Vec<f64>>>>,  // R^l_{ijk}
   259→    pub dim: usize,
   260→}
   261→```
   262→
   263→### Ricci scalar — Market stability indicator
   264→
   265→```rust
   266→/// Ricci scalar: R = g^{ij} R_{ij} where R_{ij} = R^k_{ikj}.
   267→///
   268→/// A single number that summarizes the overall curvature at a point.
   269→///
   270→/// R > 0 (positive curvature, sphere-like):
   271→///   Market self-corrects. Perturbations damp out.
   272→///   Liquidity is resilient. Safe to execute.
   273→///
   274→/// R = 0 (flat):
   275→///   Execution costs are uniform. No structural effects.
   276→///
   277→/// R < 0 (negative curvature, saddle-like):
   278→///   Perturbations amplify. Small trades can have outsized impact.
   279→///   Liquidity is fragile. Exercise caution.
   280→pub fn ricci_scalar(
   281→    riemann: &RiemannTensor,
   282→    metric: &MetricTensor,
   283→) -> f64 {
   284→    let dim = riemann.dim;
   285→    let g_inv = metric.inverse();
   286→    let mut scalar = 0.0;
   287→
   288→    // Contract R^k_{ikj} to get Ricci tensor R_{ij}
   289→    // Then contract with g^{ij} to get scalar
   290→    for i in 0..dim {
   291→        for j in 0..dim {
   292→            let mut ricci_ij = 0.0;
   293→            for k in 0..dim {
   294→                ricci_ij += riemann.components[k][i][k][j];
   295→            }
   296→            scalar += g_inv.components[i][j] * ricci_ij;
   297→        }
   298→    }
   299→
   300→    scalar
   301→}
   302→```
   303→
   304→The Ricci scalar acts as a chain oracle signal — when it turns negative, the chain oracle increases its prediction uncertainty and the Daimon raises arousal (urgency).
   305→
   306→---
   307→
   308→## Parallel transport — Cross-protocol pattern transfer
   309→
   310→Parallel transport moves a tangent vector (a trading strategy) along a path on the manifold without "rotating" it. This is how TA patterns transfer between protocols:
   311→
   312→```rust
   313→/// Parallel transport a vector from one point to another along a geodesic.
   314→///
   315→/// Financial interpretation: take a trading strategy that works on
   316→/// Protocol A and transport it to Protocol B, adjusting for the
   317→/// different cost structure.
   318→///
   319→/// d(v^k)/dt + Γ^k_ij v^i (dx^j/dt) = 0
   320→pub fn parallel_transport(
   321→    vector: &TangentVector,
   322→    along_path: &[ManifoldPoint],
   323→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   324→) -> TangentVector {
   325→    let mut transported = vector.clone();
   326→    let n = along_path.len();
   327→
   328→    for step in 0..n - 1 {
   329→        let point = &along_path[step];
   330→        let next = &along_path[step + 1];
   331→        let christoffel = manifold.christoffel_at(point);
   332→
   333→        let dx: Vec<f64> = next.coordinates.iter()
   334→            .zip(point.coordinates.iter())
   335→            .map(|(a, b)| a - b)
   336→            .collect();
   337→
   338→        // Update each component: dv^k = -Γ^k_ij v^i dx^j
   339→        let mut new_components = transported.components.clone();
   340→        for k in 0..manifold.dim {
   341→            let mut delta = 0.0;
   342→            for i in 0..manifold.dim {
   343→                for j in 0..manifold.dim {
   344→                    delta -= christoffel.components[k][i][j]
   345→                        * transported.components[i]
   346→                        * dx[j];
   347→                }
   348→            }
   349→            new_components[k] += delta;
   350→        }
   351→
   352→        transported.components = new_components;
   353→        transported.base_point = next.clone();
   354→    }
   355→
   356→    transported
   357→}
   358→```
   359→
   360→---
   361→
   362→## Exponential and logarithmic maps
   363→
   364→These maps connect the manifold to its tangent spaces, enabling local linear approximation:
   365→
   366→```rust
   367→/// Exponential map: project from tangent space to manifold.
   368→///
   369→/// Given a point p and a tangent vector v, exp_p(v) follows the
   370→/// geodesic starting at p in direction v for unit time.
   371→///
   372→/// Financial interpretation: "if I execute a trade of size v
   373→/// starting from portfolio state p, where do I end up?"
   374→pub fn exponential_map(
   375→    point: &ManifoldPoint,
   376→    vector: &TangentVector,
   377→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   378→) -> ManifoldPoint {
   379→    // Follow geodesic from point in direction vector for t=1
   380→    let path = compute_geodesic_from_velocity(point, vector, manifold, 100);
   381→    path.last().cloned().unwrap()
   382→}
   383→
   384→/// Logarithmic map: project from manifold to tangent space.
   385→///
   386→/// Given two points p and q, log_p(q) is the tangent vector at p
   387→/// that points toward q along the geodesic.
   388→///
   389→/// Financial interpretation: "what trade gets me from portfolio p to portfolio q
   390→/// via the optimal (geodesic) route?"
   391→pub fn logarithmic_map(
   392→    from: &ManifoldPoint,
   393→    to: &ManifoldPoint,
   394→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   395→) -> TangentVector {
   396→    // Solve the boundary value problem: find v such that exp_from(v) = to
   397→    // Uses shooting method with Newton iteration
   398→    shooting_method(from, to, manifold, max_iter: 20)
   399→}
   400→```
   401→
   402→### Fréchet mean — Consensus portfolio state
   403→
   404→```rust
   405→/// Fréchet mean: the point on the manifold that minimizes
   406→/// the sum of squared geodesic distances to a set of points.
   407→///
   408→/// Financial interpretation: the "average" portfolio state
   409→/// that is closest to all observed states. Used to compute
   410→/// consensus positions across a collective of agents.
   411→///
   412→/// Computed iteratively via the Karcher mean algorithm.
   413→pub fn frechet_mean(
   414→    points: &[ManifoldPoint],
   415→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   416→    max_iter: usize,
   417→) -> ManifoldPoint {
   418→    let mut mean = points[0].clone();
   419→
   420→    for _ in 0..max_iter {
   421→        // Compute mean tangent vector
   422→        let tangent_sum: Vec<f64> = points.iter()
   423→            .map(|p| logarithmic_map(&mean, p, manifold))
   424→            .fold(vec![0.0; manifold.dim], |acc, v| {
   425→                acc.iter().zip(v.components.iter())
   426→                    .map(|(a, b)| a + b)
   427→                    .collect()
   428→            });
   429→
   430→        let mean_tangent = TangentVector {
   431→            components: tangent_sum.iter().map(|v| v / points.len() as f64).collect(),
   432→            base_point: mean.clone(),
   433→        };
   434→
   435→        // Step toward mean tangent
   436→        let step_size = 0.5;  // damping for convergence
   437→        let scaled = TangentVector {
   438→            components: mean_tangent.components.iter().map(|v| v * step_size).collect(),
   439→            base_point: mean.clone(),
   440→        };
   441→
   442→        mean = exponential_map(&mean, &scaled, manifold);
   443→    }
   444→
   445→    mean
   446→}
   447→```

   450→
   451→## Spectral decomposition — Eigenvalue analysis
   452→
   453→The metric tensor's eigenvalues reveal the principal directions of cost and their magnitudes:
   454→
   455→```rust
   456→/// Spectral decomposition of the metric tensor.
   457→///
   458→/// Eigenvalues: the cost magnitude in each principal direction.
   459→///   Large eigenvalue → expensive to move in that direction.
   460→///   Small eigenvalue → cheap to move in that direction.
   461→///
   462→/// Eigenvectors: the principal directions.
   463→///   The cheapest direction is the eigenvector with smallest eigenvalue.
   464→///   The most expensive direction has the largest eigenvalue.
   465→///
   466→/// Condition number (λ_max / λ_min):
   467→///   High condition number → highly anisotropic cost structure.
   468→///   The market strongly favors some trades over others.
   469→pub struct SpectralDecomposition {
   470→    pub eigenvalues: Vec<f64>,
   471→    pub eigenvectors: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
   472→    pub condition_number: f64,
   473→}
   474→
   475→impl MetricTensor {
   476→    pub fn spectral_decomposition(&self) -> SpectralDecomposition {
   477→        let (eigenvalues, eigenvectors) = symmetric_eigendecomposition(&self.components);
   478→        let condition_number = eigenvalues.last().unwrap() / eigenvalues.first().unwrap();
   479→
   480→        SpectralDecomposition {
   481→            eigenvalues,
   482→            eigenvectors,
   483→            condition_number,
   484→        }
   485→    }
   486→}
   487→```
   488→
   489→---
   490→
   491→## Implementation details
   492→
   493→### Metric tensor computation: Hessian of price impact
   494→
   495→The metric tensor `g_ij` at a point is the Hessian of the total execution cost function with respect to portfolio state variables. Since analytical Hessians are unavailable for arbitrary pool types, the implementation uses central finite differences:
   496→
   497→```rust
   498→/// Compute the metric tensor via numerical differentiation.
   499→///
   500→/// Uses central finite differences on the execution cost function:
   501→///   g_ij = d²C / dx_i dx_j
   502→///        ≈ [C(x+ε_i+ε_j) - C(x+ε_i-ε_j) - C(x-ε_i+ε_j) + C(x-ε_i-ε_j)] / (4ε²)
   503→///
   504→/// The step size ε is adaptive: ε = max(|x_i| * relative_eps, absolute_eps).
   505→pub struct MetricTensorComputer {
   506→    /// Relative step size for finite differences.
   507→    pub relative_eps: f64,   // default: 1e-4
   508→    /// Absolute step size floor (prevents division by near-zero).
   509→    pub absolute_eps: f64,   // default: 1e-8
   510→    /// The execution cost function C(x) for a given pool configuration.
   511→    pub cost_fn: Box<dyn Fn(&[f64], &[PoolState]) -> f64 + Send + Sync>,
   512→}
   513→
   514→impl MetricTensorComputer {
   515→    /// Compute g_ij at a point using central finite differences.
   516→    pub fn compute(&self, point: &ManifoldPoint, pools: &[PoolState]) -> MetricTensor {
   517→        let dim = point.coordinates.len();
   518→        let mut g = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
   519→        let x = &point.coordinates;
   520→
   521→        for i in 0..dim {
   522→            let eps_i = (x[i].abs() * self.relative_eps).max(self.absolute_eps);
   523→            for j in i..dim {
   524→                let eps_j = (x[j].abs() * self.relative_eps).max(self.absolute_eps);
   525→
   526→                let mut x_pp = x.clone(); x_pp[i] += eps_i; x_pp[j] += eps_j;
   527→                let mut x_pm = x.clone(); x_pm[i] += eps_i; x_pm[j] -= eps_j;
   528→                let mut x_mp = x.clone(); x_mp[i] -= eps_i; x_mp[j] += eps_j;
   529→                let mut x_mm = x.clone(); x_mm[i] -= eps_i; x_mm[j] -= eps_j;
   530→
   531→                let c_pp = (self.cost_fn)(&x_pp, pools);
   532→                let c_pm = (self.cost_fn)(&x_pm, pools);
   533→                let c_mp = (self.cost_fn)(&x_mp, pools);
   534→                let c_mm = (self.cost_fn)(&x_mm, pools);
   535→
   536→                g[i][j] = (c_pp - c_pm - c_mp + c_mm) / (4.0 * eps_i * eps_j);
   537→                g[j][i] = g[i][j]; // symmetric
   538→            }
   539→        }
   540→
   541→        MetricTensor { components: g, dim }
   542→    }
   543→}
   544→```
   545→
   546→**Configuration parameters**:
   547→
   548→| Parameter | Default | Range | Notes |
   549→|---|---|---|---|
   550→| `relative_eps` | 1e-4 | 1e-6 - 1e-2 | Smaller = more accurate but noisier. 1e-4 balances accuracy and numerical noise for f64. |
   551→| `absolute_eps` | 1e-8 | 1e-12 - 1e-4 | Floor for coordinates near zero. |
   552→
   553→### Christoffel symbol finite difference parameters
   554→
   555→The Christoffel symbols require first derivatives of the metric tensor. These are also computed via central finite differences:
   556→
   557→```rust
   558→/// Compute ∂_l g_{ij} via central finite differences on the metric.
   559→///
   560→///   ∂_l g_{ij} ≈ [g_{ij}(x + ε_l) - g_{ij}(x - ε_l)] / (2ε_l)
   561→///
   562→/// This requires 2*dim metric tensor evaluations (each itself O(dim²) cost evaluations).
   563→/// Total cost: O(dim³) cost function evaluations per Christoffel computation.
   564→pub fn metric_derivatives(
   565→    computer: &MetricTensorComputer,
   566→    point: &ManifoldPoint,
   567→    pools: &[PoolState],
   568→) -> Vec<MetricTensor> {
   569→    let dim = point.coordinates.len();
   570→    let mut derivs = Vec::with_capacity(dim);
   571→
   572→    for l in 0..dim {
   573→        let eps_l = (point.coordinates[l].abs() * computer.relative_eps)
   574→            .max(computer.absolute_eps);
   575→
   576→        let mut x_plus = point.clone();
   577→        x_plus.coordinates[l] += eps_l;
   578→        let g_plus = computer.compute(&x_plus, pools);
   579→
   580→        let mut x_minus = point.clone();
   581→        x_minus.coordinates[l] -= eps_l;
   582→        let g_minus = computer.compute(&x_minus, pools);
   583→
   584→        let mut dg = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
   585→        for i in 0..dim {
   586→            for j in 0..dim {
   587→                dg[i][j] = (g_plus.components[i][j] - g_minus.components[i][j])
   588→                    / (2.0 * eps_l);
   589→            }
   590→        }
   591→
   592→        derivs.push(MetricTensor { components: dg, dim });
   593→    }
   594→
   595→    derivs
   596→}
   597→```
   598→
   599→The step size for Christoffel computation should match the metric tensor step size. Using a different scale introduces inconsistency between the metric and its derivatives.
   600→
   601→### Geodesic solver: dynamic step count and error tolerance
   602→
   603→The geodesic solver uses adaptive 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) with dynamic step count:
   604→
   605→```rust
   606→/// Adaptive geodesic solver with error-controlled step sizing.
   607→///
   608→/// Starts with `n_steps` uniform steps. After initial solve,
   609→/// estimates local truncation error by comparing RK4 with RK2.
   610→/// Doubles step count in regions where error exceeds tolerance.
   611→pub struct GeodesicSolverConfig {
   612→    /// Initial step count.
   613→    pub initial_n_steps: usize,     // default: 100
   614→    /// Maximum step count (prevents runaway refinement).
   615→    pub max_n_steps: usize,         // default: 10_000
   616→    /// Local truncation error tolerance per step.
   617→    pub error_tolerance: f64,       // default: 1e-6
   618→    /// Maximum geodesic parameter length (prevents infinite geodesics).
   619→    pub max_parameter: f64,         // default: 10.0
   620→    /// Singular point detection threshold (eigenvalue ratio).
   621→    pub singularity_threshold: f64, // default: 1e-10
   622→}
   623→
   624→impl GeodesicSolverConfig {
   625→    /// Detect singular points where the metric degenerates.
   626→    ///
   627→    /// A point is singular if the metric tensor's condition number
   628→    /// exceeds 1/singularity_threshold, or if any eigenvalue is
   629→    /// negative (the metric is no longer positive-definite).
   630→    pub fn is_singular(&self, metric: &MetricTensor) -> bool {
   631→        let spectral = metric.spectral_decomposition();
   632→        let min_eigenvalue = spectral.eigenvalues.first().copied().unwrap_or(0.0);
   633→        min_eigenvalue < self.singularity_threshold
   634→            || spectral.condition_number > 1.0 / self.singularity_threshold
   635→    }
   636→}
   637→```
   638→
   639→**Singular point handling**: When the solver encounters a singular point (degenerate metric), it:
   640→
   641→1. Halves the step size and retries.
   642→2. If still singular after 3 retries, records a `GeodesicIncomplete` result with the last valid point.
   643→3. Logs the singular location for manifold diagnostics.
   644→
   645→### Exponential and logarithmic map parameters
   646→
   647→```rust
   648→/// Exponential map configuration.
   649→pub struct ExpMapConfig {
   650→    /// Number of geodesic integration steps.
   651→    pub n_steps: usize,       // default: 100
   652→    /// Error tolerance for integration.
   653→    pub tolerance: f64,        // default: 1e-6
   654→}
   655→
   656→/// Logarithmic map configuration (shooting method).
   657→///
   658→/// The shooting method solves: find v such that exp_p(v) = q.
   659→/// It iterates by adjusting v based on the error exp_p(v) - q.
   660→pub struct LogMapConfig {
   661→    /// Maximum Newton iterations for the shooting method.
   662→    pub max_iterations: usize,     // default: 20
   663→    /// Convergence tolerance: ||exp_p(v) - q|| < tolerance.
   664→    pub convergence_tolerance: f64, // default: 1e-6
   665→    /// Line search parameters (backtracking Armijo).
   666→    pub armijo_c: f64,              // default: 1e-4
   667→    pub armijo_tau: f64,            // default: 0.5
   668→    /// Initial step size for Newton line search.
   669→    pub initial_step: f64,          // default: 1.0
   670→    /// Minimum step size before declaring failure.
   671→    pub min_step: f64,              // default: 1e-10
   672→}
   673→```
   674→
   675→The Newton line search in the logarithmic map uses backtracking Armijo conditions: accept a step if `f(x + alpha*d) <= f(x) + c*alpha*grad_f . d`, where `c = 1e-4` (sufficient decrease) and `alpha` is halved each backtrack attempt (`tau = 0.5`). Maximum backtracks: `ceil(log2(initial_step / min_step))`.
   676→
   677→### Ricci scalar thresholds for market fragility
   678→
   679→| Ricci scalar range | Interpretation | Agent response |
   680→|---|---|---|
   681→| R > 1.0 | Strongly self-correcting. Trades have predictable costs. | Execute normally. |
   682→| 0.0 < R <= 1.0 | Mildly stable. Some cost variation. | Execute with wider slippage tolerance. |
   683→| -0.5 <= R <= 0.0 | Neutral to mildly fragile. | Reduce position sizes by 50%. |
   684→| -2.0 <= R < -0.5 | Fragile. Small trades amplify. | Reduce position sizes by 80%. Alert Daimon (raise Arousal). |
   685→| R < -2.0 | Critically fragile. Market structure unstable. | Suppress all execution. Escalate to T2. |
   686→
   687→These thresholds are configurable per protocol. Concentrated liquidity AMMs (Uniswap V3) tend toward higher curvature magnitude than constant-product AMMs (Uniswap V2), so adjust accordingly.
   688→
   689→### Failure modes
   690→
   691→1. **Degenerate manifold**: The metric tensor has zero or negative eigenvalues. Cause: a liquidity pool is empty or nearly so. Mitigation: skip the degenerate dimension (project out the null eigenspace) or mark the pool as unavailable.
   692→
   693→2. **Disconnected components**: The manifold splits into disconnected regions (e.g., two isolated liquidity pools with no bridge). Geodesics between disconnected components do not exist. The solver returns `GeodesicIncomplete` with the reason `DisconnectedComponents`.
   694→
   695→3. **Numerical instability in Christoffel symbols**: When the metric changes rapidly (high curvature), finite differences amplify truncation error. Mitigation: reduce `eps` by 10x in high-curvature regions (detected when eigenvalue ratio > 100).
   696→
   697→4. **Ill-conditioned metric inverse**: Required for Christoffel computation. When condition number > 1e8, use pseudoinverse (SVD with eigenvalue floor at 1e-10).
   698→
   699→5. **Geodesic divergence**: RK4 integration can diverge near singular points. The adaptive solver detects divergence when `||velocity|| > 1e6` and terminates early.
   700→
   701→### Integration wiring
   702→
   703→The spectral liquidity manifold integrates into the chain oracle prediction pipeline:
   704→
   705→```
   706→ChainOracle::predict()
   707→  -> query on-chain pool states (via alloy provider)
   708→  -> construct ManifoldPoint from portfolio state
   709→  -> MetricTensorComputer::compute() at current point
   710→  -> SpectralDecomposition for eigenvalue analysis
   711→  -> ricci_scalar() for fragility assessment
   712→  -> if R > threshold: compute_geodesic() for optimal execution path
   713→  -> if R < threshold: suppress execution, raise Daimon arousal
   714→  -> encode manifold features as HDC vector (via DeFiCodebook)
   715→  -> emit as Engram to the witness pipeline
   716→```
   717→
   718→### Test criteria
   719→
   720→- **Metric symmetry**: `g[i][j] == g[j][i]` for all i, j (within f64 epsilon).
   721→- **Metric positive-definiteness**: All eigenvalues of a well-formed metric are positive.
   722→- **Geodesic consistency**: `exp_p(log_p(q)) == q` within convergence tolerance.
   723→- **Christoffel symmetry**: `Gamma[k][i][j] == Gamma[k][j][i]` (lower indices are symmetric).
   724→- **Ricci scalar sign**: For a known constant-product AMM with deep liquidity, R > 0. For a pool at 99% depletion, R < 0.
   725→- **Adaptive step refinement**: Halving error tolerance halves the integration error (4th-order convergence).
   726→- **Singular point detection**: A pool with zero liquidity triggers `is_singular() == true`.

1→# Adaptive Signal Metabolism
     2→
     3→> Signals are living organisms. They compete for attention, reproduce when useful, die when obsolete, and evolve through mutation and selection. The TA subsystem is an ecological system governed by Hebbian learning and replicator dynamics.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [06-hyperdimensional-ta](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) for HDC encoding, [05-witness-as-ta-generalized](./05-witness-as-ta-generalized.md) for the witness pipeline
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/03-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Signals as organisms
    15→
    16→In the adaptive signal metabolism framework, every TA signal is treated as a living organism that exists within an ecological system. Signals compete for the limited resource of agent attention. Useful signals grow stronger (reproduce). Obsolete signals weaken (die). Novel mutations emerge from HDC recombination. The system self-organizes into an optimal signal ensemble without manual curation.
    17→
    18→This is not metaphor — it is a direct implementation of replicator dynamics (Taylor & Jonker, 1978) and evolutionary game theory applied to signal selection.
    19→
    20→### The signal as a 5-tuple
    21→
    22→```rust
    23→/// A signal organism in the adaptive metabolism framework.
    24→///
    25→/// Each signal is a 5-tuple: (f, C, H, W, ctx)
    26→/// - f: the signal function (computation)
    27→/// - C: confidence (self-assessed reliability)
    28→/// - H: HDC vector (pattern identity)
    29→/// - W: weight (fitness in the attention economy)
    30→/// - ctx: context (domain-specific parameters)
    31→pub struct AdaptiveSignal {
    32→    /// Unique identifier.
    33→    pub id: SignalId,
    34→
    35→    /// The computation that produces this signal's value.
    36→    /// In Rust: a closure or function pointer.
    37→    pub function: Box<dyn Fn(&EngineState) -> f64 + Send + Sync>,
    38→
    39→    /// Self-assessed confidence [0.0, 1.0].
    40→    /// Updated via Hebbian learning after each prediction cycle.
    41→    pub confidence: f64,
    42→
    43→    /// HDC vector encoding this signal's identity.
    44→    /// Used for similarity search and cross-domain matching.
    45→    pub hdc_vector: HdcVector,
    46→
    47→    /// Weight (fitness) in the attention economy.
    48→    /// Signals with higher weight get more attention budget.
    49→    /// Updated via replicator dynamics.
    50→    pub weight: f64,
    51→
    52→    /// Domain-specific context for this signal.
    53→    pub context: SignalContext,
    54→
    55→    /// Lineage: parent signals this was derived from (for evolution tracking).
    56→    pub lineage: Vec<SignalId>,
    57→
    58→    /// Generation counter (how many mutation/selection cycles).
    59→    pub generation: u64,
    60→
    61→    /// Birth timestamp.
    62→    pub created_at_ms: i64,
    63→
    64→    /// Number of times this signal has been evaluated.
    65→    pub evaluation_count: u64,
    66→
    67→    /// Running accuracy statistics.
    68→    pub accuracy: ExponentialMovingAverage,
    69→}
    70→```
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→## Hebbian learning — "Neurons that fire together wire together"
    75→
    76→Signal confidence is updated via Oja's rule, a normalized variant of Hebbian learning that prevents runaway weight growth:
    77→
    78→```rust
    79→/// Hebbian update for signal confidence.
    80→///
    81→/// When a signal's prediction correlates with the actual outcome,
    82→/// confidence increases. When it anti-correlates, confidence decreases.
    83→///
    84→/// Uses Oja's rule (Oja, 1982) for stability:
    85→///   Δw = η × y × (x - y × w)
    86→///
    87→/// where:
    88→///   w = current confidence
    89→///   x = signal value (prediction)
    90→///   y = outcome (actual value)
    91→///   η = learning rate (typically 0.01-0.05)
    92→///
    93→/// The (- y × w) term prevents weights from growing without bound.
    94→pub fn hebbian_update(
    95→    signal: &mut AdaptiveSignal,
    96→    prediction: f64,
    97→    outcome: f64,
    98→    learning_rate: f64,
    99→) {
   100→    let delta = learning_rate * outcome * (prediction - outcome * signal.confidence);
   101→    signal.confidence = (signal.confidence + delta).clamp(0.0, 1.0);
   102→}
   103→
   104→/// Batch Hebbian update across all signals in the registry.
   105→///
   106→/// After each prediction resolution, all signals that contributed
   107→/// to the prediction have their confidence updated.
   108→pub fn batch_hebbian_update(
   109→    registry: &mut SignalRegistry,
   110→    predictions: &[(SignalId, f64)],
   111→    outcome: f64,
   112→    learning_rate: f64,
   113→) {
   114→    for (signal_id, prediction) in predictions {
   115→        if let Some(signal) = registry.get_mut(signal_id) {
   116→            hebbian_update(signal, *prediction, outcome, learning_rate);
   117→            signal.evaluation_count += 1;
   118→            signal.accuracy.update((prediction - outcome).abs());
   119→        }
   120→    }
   121→}
   122→```
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## Replicator dynamics — Fitness-proportionate selection
   127→
   128→Signal weights evolve according to replicator dynamics (Taylor & Jonker, 1978): signals with above-average fitness gain weight; below-average signals lose weight. This creates a self-organizing ensemble without manual threshold tuning:
   129→
   130→```rust
   131→/// Replicator dynamics update for signal weights.
   132→///
   133→/// The replicator equation:
   134→///   dw_i/dt = w_i × (f_i - f̄)
   135→///
   136→/// where:
   137→///   w_i = weight of signal i
   138→///   f_i = fitness of signal i
   139→///   f̄ = average fitness across all signals
   140→///
   141→/// Fitness is the accuracy of the signal's predictions.
   142→/// Signals that predict better than average grow.
   143→/// Signals that predict worse than average shrink.
   144→pub fn replicator_update(registry: &mut SignalRegistry, dt: f64) {
   145→    let signals: Vec<(SignalId, f64, f64)> = registry.iter()
   146→        .map(|s| (s.id, s.weight, s.fitness()))
   147→        .collect();
   148→
   149→    let total_weight: f64 = signals.iter().map(|(_, w, _)| w).sum();
   150→    let avg_fitness: f64 = signals.iter()
   151→        .map(|(_, w, f)| w * f / total_weight)
   152→        .sum();
   153→
   154→    for (id, weight, fitness) in &signals {
   155→        let delta = weight * (fitness - avg_fitness) * dt;
   156→        if let Some(signal) = registry.get_mut(id) {
   157→            signal.weight = (signal.weight + delta).max(0.001);  // floor to prevent extinction
   158→        }
   159→    }
   160→
   161→    // Normalize weights to sum to 1.0
   162→    registry.normalize_weights();
   163→}
   164→```
   165→
   166→### Fisher's fundamental theorem
   167→
   168→Fisher's fundamental theorem of natural selection (Fisher, 1930) applies: the rate of increase in mean fitness equals the genetic variance in fitness. In signal terms: **the rate at which the signal ensemble improves equals the diversity of signal quality**. This has a practical implication — if all signals have similar accuracy, improvement stalls. The system must maintain diversity to keep improving.
   169→
   170→```rust
   171→/// Compute Fisher's variance (rate of improvement potential).
   172→///
   173→/// V(fitness) = Σ w_i × (f_i - f̄)²
   174→///
   175→/// When V is high: the ensemble is rapidly improving.
   176→/// When V approaches 0: the ensemble has converged (may need mutation injection).
   177→pub fn fisher_variance(registry: &SignalRegistry) -> f64 {
   178→    let avg_fitness = registry.mean_fitness();
   179→    registry.iter()
   180→        .map(|s| s.weight * (s.fitness() - avg_fitness).powi(2))
   181→        .sum()
   182→}
   183→```
   184→
   185→---
   186→
   187→## Speciation — Signal evolution
   188→
   189→New signals emerge through mutation of successful parent signals:
   190→
   191→```rust
   192→/// Signal speciation: create a new signal by mutating a parent.
   193→///
   194→/// The mutation operator perturbs the parent's HDC vector
   195→/// (XOR with a random noise vector of controlled density)
   196→/// and adjusts the signal function parameters.
   197→///
   198→/// New signals start with low weight (0.01) and must prove
   199→/// themselves through replicator dynamics before gaining
   200→/// significant attention budget.
   201→pub fn speciate(
   202→    parent: &AdaptiveSignal,
   203→    mutation_rate: f64,
   204→    rng: &mut impl Rng,
   205→) -> AdaptiveSignal {
   206→    // Mutate HDC vector: flip bits with probability = mutation_rate
   207→    let noise = HdcVector::random_with_density(mutation_rate, rng);
   208→    let mutated_hv = parent.hdc_vector.xor(&noise);
   209→
   210→    // Mutate signal function parameters
   211→    let mutated_function = parent.function.mutate(mutation_rate, rng);
   212→
   213→    AdaptiveSignal {
   214→        id: SignalId::new(),
   215→        function: mutated_function,
   216→        confidence: 0.5,  // start neutral
   217→        hdc_vector: mutated_hv,
   218→        weight: 0.01,  // start with minimal weight
   219→        context: parent.context.clone(),
   220→        lineage: {
   221→            let mut l = parent.lineage.clone();
   222→            l.push(parent.id);
   223→            l
   224→        },
   225→        generation: parent.generation + 1,
   226→        created_at_ms: now_ms(),
   227→        evaluation_count: 0,
   228→        accuracy: ExponentialMovingAverage::new(0.1),
   229→    }
   230→}
   231→```
   232→
   233→### Fitness landscape (Sewall Wright, 1932)
   234→
   235→The ensemble of signals exists on a fitness landscape — a surface where each point is a signal configuration and the height is its fitness. The replicator dynamics push signals uphill (toward higher fitness), but speciation (mutation) allows escape from local optima:
   236→
   237→```rust
   238→/// The fitness landscape for a signal ensemble.
   239→///
   240→/// Each signal's position is its HDC vector (in 10,240-bit space).
   241→/// The height at each position is the signal's fitness (accuracy).
   242→///
   243→/// Properties:
   244→/// - Rugged: many local optima (similar signals with different accuracy)
   245→/// - Dynamic: the landscape shifts as market/code/research conditions change
   246→/// - High-dimensional: 10,240 dimensions, many escape routes from local optima
   247→///
   248→/// Navigation via: replicator dynamics (hill climbing) + speciation (exploration)
   249→pub struct FitnessLandscape {
   250→    /// Current signal positions and heights.
   251→    pub signals: Vec<(HdcVector, f64)>,
   252→
   253→    /// Landscape roughness (variance of fitness across neighbors).
   254→    pub roughness: f64,
   255→
   256→    /// Landscape shift rate (how fast the landscape changes).
   257→    pub shift_rate: f64,
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→### Red Queen dynamic
   262→
   263→Following Van Valen's Red Queen hypothesis (1973): in adversarial environments, signals must continuously evolve just to maintain their fitness, because the environment (adversary) co-evolves. In the chain domain, MEV searchers adapt to agent strategies. In the coding domain, codebase structure evolves. Signals that stop evolving become obsolete:
   264→
   265→```rust
   266→/// Red Queen pressure: signals must evolve to maintain fitness.
   267→///
   268→/// Implemented as a constant downward pressure on all signal weights:
   269→///   w_i(t+1) = w_i(t) × (1 - decay_rate) + replicator_delta
   270→///
   271→/// Without improvement, signals decay toward zero.
   272→/// Only signals that continuously outperform survive.
   273→pub fn apply_red_queen_pressure(registry: &mut SignalRegistry, decay_rate: f64) {
   274→    for signal in registry.iter_mut() {
   275→        signal.weight *= 1.0 - decay_rate;
   276→    }
   277→}
   278→```
   279→
   280→---
   281→
   282→## SignalRegistry — The ecosystem container
   283→
   284→```rust
   285→/// The signal registry: manages the full signal ecosystem.
   286→///
   287→/// Contains all living signals, tracks their fitness over time,
   288→/// manages speciation and extinction events.
   289→pub struct SignalRegistry {
   290→    /// All active signals.
   291→    signals: HashMap<SignalId, AdaptiveSignal>,
   292→
   293→    /// Maximum population (attention budget constraint).
   294→    max_population: usize,
   295→
   296→    /// Speciation rate (probability of mutation per generation).
   297→    speciation_rate: f64,
   298→
   299→    /// Extinction threshold (minimum weight before removal).
   300→    extinction_threshold: f64,
   301→
   302→    /// Generation counter.
   303→    generation: u64,
   304→}
   305→
   306→impl SignalRegistry {
   307→    /// Run one evolutionary step.
   308→    ///
   309→    /// 1. Evaluate all signals against recent data
   310→    /// 2. Hebbian update of confidence
   311→    /// 3. Replicator dynamics update of weights
   312→    /// 4. Speciate: create mutations of top performers
   313→    /// 5. Extinction: remove signals below threshold
   314→    /// 6. Red Queen: apply constant decay pressure
   315→    pub fn evolve_step(&mut self, data: &[Engram], outcomes: &[Engram]) {
   316→        // 1. Evaluate
   317→        let predictions = self.evaluate_all(data);
   318→
   319→        // 2. Hebbian update
   320→        for (pred, outcome) in predictions.iter().zip(outcomes) {
   321→            batch_hebbian_update(self, &pred.signal_contributions, outcome.numeric_value(), 0.02);
   322→        }
   323→
   324→        // 3. Replicator dynamics
   325→        replicator_update(self, dt: 1.0);
   326→
   327→        // 4. Speciation
   328→        let top_signals: Vec<_> = self.top_k(5);
   329→        for parent in &top_signals {
   330→            if rand::random::<f64>() < self.speciation_rate {
   331→                let child = speciate(parent, mutation_rate: 0.05, &mut rng);
   332→                self.insert(child);
   333→            }
   334→        }
   335→
   336→        // 5. Extinction
   337→        self.remove_below_threshold(self.extinction_threshold);
   338→
   339→        // 6. Red Queen
   340→        apply_red_queen_pressure(self, decay_rate: 0.001);
   341→
   342→        // 7. Enforce population cap
   343→        while self.signals.len() > self.max_population {
   344→            self.remove_weakest();
   345→        }
   346→
   347→        self.generation += 1;
   348→    }
   349→}
   350→```
   351→
   352→---
   353→
   354→## Heartbeat integration
   355→
   356→The signal metabolism operates at all three cognitive speeds:
   357→
   358→| Speed | Signal metabolism activity |
   359→|---|---|
   360→| **Gamma** (~5-15s) | Signals evaluate against current data. No learning. Cost: microseconds. |
   361→| **Theta** (~75s) | Hebbian update + replicator dynamics step. Predictions resolve. |
   362→| **Delta** (hours) | Full evolutionary step: speciation, extinction, Red Queen. Landscape analysis. |
   363→
   364→At Gamma frequency, the signal registry is read-only — probes read signal values but don't update weights. This ensures the T0 probe system (80% of ticks costing nothing) is not disrupted by evolutionary computation.
   365→
   366→At Theta frequency, learning happens — confidence updates and weight adjustments based on resolved predictions.
   367→
   368→At Delta frequency, the full evolutionary cycle runs — new signals are born, old ones die, and the fitness landscape is analyzed for stagnation.
   369→
   370→---
