1→# Spectral Liquidity Manifolds
     2→
     3→> Riemannian geometry applied to DeFi execution costs. Liquidity pools form a curved manifold where geodesics are optimal execution paths and curvature indicates structural risk.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [02-chain-oracles](./02-chain-oracles.md) for chain TA primitives, [06-hyperdimensional-ta](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) for pattern encoding
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/02-spectral-liquidity-manifolds.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→DeFi execution is not a simple price lookup. Every trade traverses a **liquidity landscape** where costs depend on pool depth, gas fees, timing, and opportunity costs. These costs vary non-linearly with trade size, time, and market conditions. The spectral liquidity manifold framework models this landscape using Riemannian geometry — the mathematical framework for curved spaces.
    17→
    18→The core insight: a liquidity landscape is a curved space. The metric tensor encodes execution costs at each point. Geodesics (shortest paths on the manifold) are the optimal execution routes. Curvature measures structural stability — positive curvature (like a sphere) means the market self-corrects; negative curvature (like a saddle) means small perturbations amplify.
    19→
    20→While this framework is natively chain-specific (DeFi liquidity is the domain), the mathematical structure generalizes. Any domain with spatially varying costs — CI/CD pipeline routing, research strategy selection, resource allocation — can be modeled as a manifold with a cost metric.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## The state manifold
    25→
    26→The liquidity manifold is a smooth differentiable manifold M where each point represents a DeFi portfolio state:
    27→
    28→```rust
    29→/// A point on the liquidity manifold.
    30→///
    31→/// Coordinates represent the portfolio state:
    32→/// (asset_0_balance, asset_1_balance, ..., asset_n_balance, liquidity_position_params)
    33→///
    34→/// The manifold dimension equals the number of independent state variables.
    35→pub struct ManifoldPoint {
    36→    /// Portfolio state coordinates.
    37→    pub coordinates: Vec<f64>,
    38→
    39→    /// Which protocol/pool this point belongs to.
    40→    pub protocol: ProtocolId,
    41→
    42→    /// Timestamp of the state observation.
    43→    pub timestamp_ms: i64,
    44→}
    45→
    46→/// A tangent vector at a point on the manifold.
    47→///
    48→/// Tangent vectors represent infinitesimal trades — small changes
    49→/// in portfolio state. The metric tensor measures the "cost" of
    50→/// moving in each direction.
    51→pub struct TangentVector {
    52→    /// Components of the tangent vector in local coordinates.
    53→    pub components: Vec<f64>,
    54→
    55→    /// The point where this tangent vector is attached.
    56→    pub base_point: ManifoldPoint,
    57→}
    58→```
    59→
    60→### The metric tensor
    61→
    62→The metric tensor g_ij defines the cost of moving from one state to another. It encodes four types of execution cost:
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→/// The metric tensor at a point on the liquidity manifold.
    66→///
    67→/// g_ij = slippage_ij + gas_ij + time_ij + opportunity_ij
    68→///
    69→/// Each component captures a different execution cost:
    70→/// - slippage: price impact of the trade
    71→/// - gas: transaction fee on the blockchain
    72→/// - time: cost of waiting for confirmation
    73→/// - opportunity: cost of capital locked during execution
    74→pub struct MetricTensor {
    75→    /// The n×n matrix of metric components.
    76→    pub components: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
    77→
    78→    /// Dimension of the manifold.
    79→    pub dim: usize,
    80→}
    81→
    82→impl MetricTensor {
    83→    /// Compute the metric tensor at a given point.
    84→    ///
    85→    /// This requires querying the current liquidity state of the
    86→    /// underlying pools and computing the cost gradient in each direction.
    87→    pub fn compute(point: &ManifoldPoint, pools: &[PoolState]) -> Self {
    88→        let dim = point.coordinates.len();
    89→        let mut g = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
    90→
    91→        for i in 0..dim {
    92→            for j in 0..dim {
    93→                // Slippage component: d²(price_impact) / d(x_i)d(x_j)
    94→                g[i][j] += slippage_metric(point, i, j, pools);
    95→
    96→                // Gas component: constant per transaction, amortized
    97→                g[i][j] += gas_metric(point, i, j);
    98→
    99→                // Time component: confirmation time scaled by urgency
   100→                g[i][j] += time_metric(point, i, j);
   101→
   102→                // Opportunity component: capital lockup cost
   103→                g[i][j] += opportunity_metric(point, i, j);
   104→            }
   105→        }
   106→
   107→        MetricTensor { components: g, dim }
   108→    }
   109→
   110→    /// Inner product of two tangent vectors using this metric.
   111→    /// This gives the "cost squared" of moving in direction v.
   112→    pub fn inner_product(&self, v: &TangentVector, w: &TangentVector) -> f64 {
   113→        let mut result = 0.0;
   114→        for i in 0..self.dim {
   115→            for j in 0..self.dim {
   116→                result += self.components[i][j] * v.components[i] * w.components[j];
   117→            }
   118→        }
   119→        result
   120→    }
   121→
   122→    /// Length of a tangent vector: the "cost" of an infinitesimal trade.
   123→    pub fn norm(&self, v: &TangentVector) -> f64 {
   124→        self.inner_product(v, v).sqrt()
   125→    }
   126→}
   127→```
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## Christoffel symbols — How the manifold curves
   132→
   133→The Christoffel symbols Γ^k_ij describe how the coordinate system curves — they are the "gravitational field" of the liquidity manifold:
   134→
   135→```rust
   136→/// Christoffel symbols of the second kind: Γ^k_ij.
   137→///
   138→/// These describe how parallel transport along the manifold
   139→/// rotates tangent vectors. In financial terms: they describe
   140→/// how the cost of a trade changes as you move through the
   141→/// liquidity landscape.
   142→///
   143→/// Γ^k_ij = (1/2) g^{kl} (∂_i g_{jl} + ∂_j g_{il} - ∂_l g_{ij})
   144→pub struct ChristoffelSymbols {
   145→    /// Γ^k_ij stored as [k][i][j].
   146→    pub components: Vec<Vec<Vec<f64>>>,
   147→    pub dim: usize,
   148→}
   149→
   150→impl ChristoffelSymbols {
   151→    /// Compute Christoffel symbols from the metric tensor.
   152→    ///
   153→    /// Requires: metric tensor and its first derivatives at the point.
   154→    /// Uses finite differences for derivatives when analytical forms
   155→    /// are not available.
   156→    pub fn compute(
   157→        metric: &MetricTensor,
   158→        metric_derivatives: &[MetricTensor],  // ∂_l g_{ij} for each l
   159→    ) -> Self {
   160→        let dim = metric.dim;
   161→        let g_inv = metric.inverse();
   162→        let mut gamma = vec![vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim]; dim];
   163→
   164→        for k in 0..dim {
   165→            for i in 0..dim {
   166→                for j in 0..dim {
   167→                    for l in 0..dim {
   168→                        gamma[k][i][j] += 0.5 * g_inv.components[k][l] * (
   169→                            metric_derivatives[i].components[j][l] +
   170→                            metric_derivatives[j].components[i][l] -
   171→                            metric_derivatives[l].components[i][j]
   172→                        );
   173→                    }
   174→                }
   175→            }
   176→        }
   177→
   178→        ChristoffelSymbols { components: gamma, dim }
   179→    }
   180→}
   181→```
   182→
   183→---
   184→
   185→## Geodesics — Optimal execution paths
   186→
   187→A geodesic on the liquidity manifold is the path that minimizes total execution cost. Finding the optimal route for a DeFi trade is equivalent to solving the geodesic equation:
   188→
   189→```rust
   190→/// Compute the geodesic (optimal execution path) between two portfolio states.
   191→///
   192→/// The geodesic equation:
   193→///   d²x^k/dt² + Γ^k_ij (dx^i/dt)(dx^j/dt) = 0
   194→///
   195→/// Solved numerically using 4th-order Runge-Kutta integration.
   196→///
   197→/// The resulting path minimizes total execution cost (slippage + gas + time + opportunity).
   198→pub fn compute_geodesic(
   199→    start: &ManifoldPoint,
   200→    end: &ManifoldPoint,
   201→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   202→    n_steps: usize,
   203→) -> Vec<ManifoldPoint> {
   204→    let dt = 1.0 / n_steps as f64;
   205→    let mut path = vec![start.clone()];
   206→    let mut velocity = initial_velocity(start, end, manifold);
   207→
   208→    for _ in 0..n_steps {
   209→        let point = path.last().unwrap();
   210→        let christoffel = manifold.christoffel_at(point);
   211→
   212→        // Geodesic equation: acceleration = -Γ^k_ij v^i v^j
   213→        let mut acceleration = vec![0.0; manifold.dim];
   214→        for k in 0..manifold.dim {
   215→            for i in 0..manifold.dim {
   216→                for j in 0..manifold.dim {
   217→                    acceleration[k] -= christoffel.components[k][i][j]
   218→                        * velocity.components[i]
   219→                        * velocity.components[j];
   220→                }
   221→            }
   222→        }
   223→
   224→        // RK4 integration step
   225→        let (new_point, new_velocity) = rk4_step(point, &velocity, &acceleration, dt);
   226→        velocity = new_velocity;
   227→        path.push(new_point);
   228→    }
   229→
   230→    path
   231→}
   232→```
   233→
   234→### Geodesic interpretation
   235→
   236→| Geodesic property | Financial meaning |
   237→|---|---|
   238→| **Geodesic length** | Total execution cost of the optimal path |
   239→| **Geodesic curvature** | How far the optimal path deviates from a "straight" trade |
   240→| **Conjugate points** | Points where alternative optimal paths exist (arbitrage opportunities) |
   241→| **Geodesic incompleteness** | Regions where no optimal path exists (illiquid, fragmented markets) |
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→## Curvature — Structural risk
   246→
   247→The Riemann curvature tensor and its contractions reveal structural properties of the liquidity landscape:
   248→
   249→### Riemann curvature tensor
   250→
   251→```rust
   252→/// Riemann curvature tensor: R^l_{ijk}.
   253→///
   254→/// Measures the failure of parallel transport around an infinitesimal loop.
   255→/// In financial terms: how much does the cost structure change as you
   256→/// move around in the liquidity landscape?
   257→pub struct RiemannTensor {
   258→    pub components: Vec<Vec<Vec<Vec<f64>>>>,  // R^l_{ijk}
   259→    pub dim: usize,
   260→}
   261→```
   262→
   263→### Ricci scalar — Market stability indicator
   264→
   265→```rust
   266→/// Ricci scalar: R = g^{ij} R_{ij} where R_{ij} = R^k_{ikj}.
   267→///
   268→/// A single number that summarizes the overall curvature at a point.
   269→///
   270→/// R > 0 (positive curvature, sphere-like):
   271→///   Market self-corrects. Perturbations damp out.
   272→///   Liquidity is resilient. Safe to execute.
   273→///
   274→/// R = 0 (flat):
   275→///   Execution costs are uniform. No structural effects.
   276→///
   277→/// R < 0 (negative curvature, saddle-like):
   278→///   Perturbations amplify. Small trades can have outsized impact.
   279→///   Liquidity is fragile. Exercise caution.
   280→pub fn ricci_scalar(
   281→    riemann: &RiemannTensor,
   282→    metric: &MetricTensor,
   283→) -> f64 {
   284→    let dim = riemann.dim;
   285→    let g_inv = metric.inverse();
   286→    let mut scalar = 0.0;
   287→
   288→    // Contract R^k_{ikj} to get Ricci tensor R_{ij}
   289→    // Then contract with g^{ij} to get scalar
   290→    for i in 0..dim {
   291→        for j in 0..dim {
   292→            let mut ricci_ij = 0.0;
   293→            for k in 0..dim {
   294→                ricci_ij += riemann.components[k][i][k][j];
   295→            }
   296→            scalar += g_inv.components[i][j] * ricci_ij;
   297→        }
   298→    }
   299→
   300→    scalar
   301→}
   302→```
   303→
   304→The Ricci scalar acts as a chain oracle signal — when it turns negative, the chain oracle increases its prediction uncertainty and the Daimon raises arousal (urgency).
   305→
   306→---
   307→
   308→## Parallel transport — Cross-protocol pattern transfer
   309→
   310→Parallel transport moves a tangent vector (a trading strategy) along a path on the manifold without "rotating" it. This is how TA patterns transfer between protocols:
   311→
   312→```rust
   313→/// Parallel transport a vector from one point to another along a geodesic.
   314→///
   315→/// Financial interpretation: take a trading strategy that works on
   316→/// Protocol A and transport it to Protocol B, adjusting for the
   317→/// different cost structure.
   318→///
   319→/// d(v^k)/dt + Γ^k_ij v^i (dx^j/dt) = 0
   320→pub fn parallel_transport(
   321→    vector: &TangentVector,
   322→    along_path: &[ManifoldPoint],
   323→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   324→) -> TangentVector {
   325→    let mut transported = vector.clone();
   326→    let n = along_path.len();
   327→
   328→    for step in 0..n - 1 {
   329→        let point = &along_path[step];
   330→        let next = &along_path[step + 1];
   331→        let christoffel = manifold.christoffel_at(point);
   332→
   333→        let dx: Vec<f64> = next.coordinates.iter()
   334→            .zip(point.coordinates.iter())
   335→            .map(|(a, b)| a - b)
   336→            .collect();
   337→
   338→        // Update each component: dv^k = -Γ^k_ij v^i dx^j
   339→        let mut new_components = transported.components.clone();
   340→        for k in 0..manifold.dim {
   341→            let mut delta = 0.0;
   342→            for i in 0..manifold.dim {
   343→                for j in 0..manifold.dim {
   344→                    delta -= christoffel.components[k][i][j]
   345→                        * transported.components[i]
   346→                        * dx[j];
   347→                }
   348→            }
   349→            new_components[k] += delta;
   350→        }
   351→
   352→        transported.components = new_components;
   353→        transported.base_point = next.clone();
   354→    }
   355→
   356→    transported
   357→}
   358→```
   359→
   360→---
   361→
   362→## Exponential and logarithmic maps
   363→
   364→These maps connect the manifold to its tangent spaces, enabling local linear approximation:
   365→
   366→```rust
   367→/// Exponential map: project from tangent space to manifold.
   368→///
   369→/// Given a point p and a tangent vector v, exp_p(v) follows the
   370→/// geodesic starting at p in direction v for unit time.
   371→///
   372→/// Financial interpretation: "if I execute a trade of size v
   373→/// starting from portfolio state p, where do I end up?"
   374→pub fn exponential_map(
   375→    point: &ManifoldPoint,
   376→    vector: &TangentVector,
   377→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   378→) -> ManifoldPoint {
   379→    // Follow geodesic from point in direction vector for t=1
   380→    let path = compute_geodesic_from_velocity(point, vector, manifold, 100);
   381→    path.last().cloned().unwrap()
   382→}
   383→
   384→/// Logarithmic map: project from manifold to tangent space.
   385→///
   386→/// Given two points p and q, log_p(q) is the tangent vector at p
   387→/// that points toward q along the geodesic.
   388→///
   389→/// Financial interpretation: "what trade gets me from portfolio p to portfolio q
   390→/// via the optimal (geodesic) route?"
   391→pub fn logarithmic_map(
   392→    from: &ManifoldPoint,
   393→    to: &ManifoldPoint,
   394→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   395→) -> TangentVector {
   396→    // Solve the boundary value problem: find v such that exp_from(v) = to
   397→    // Uses shooting method with Newton iteration
   398→    shooting_method(from, to, manifold, max_iter: 20)
   399→}
   400→```
   401→
   402→### Fréchet mean — Consensus portfolio state
   403→
   404→```rust
   405→/// Fréchet mean: the point on the manifold that minimizes
   406→/// the sum of squared geodesic distances to a set of points.
   407→///
   408→/// Financial interpretation: the "average" portfolio state
   409→/// that is closest to all observed states. Used to compute
   410→/// consensus positions across a collective of agents.
   411→///
   412→/// Computed iteratively via the Karcher mean algorithm.
   413→pub fn frechet_mean(
   414→    points: &[ManifoldPoint],
   415→    manifold: &LiquidityManifold,
   416→    max_iter: usize,
   417→) -> ManifoldPoint {
   418→    let mut mean = points[0].clone();
   419→
   420→    for _ in 0..max_iter {
   421→        // Compute mean tangent vector
   422→        let tangent_sum: Vec<f64> = points.iter()
   423→            .map(|p| logarithmic_map(&mean, p, manifold))
   424→            .fold(vec![0.0; manifold.dim], |acc, v| {
   425→                acc.iter().zip(v.components.iter())
   426→                    .map(|(a, b)| a + b)
   427→                    .collect()
   428→            });
   429→
   430→        let mean_tangent = TangentVector {
   431→            components: tangent_sum.iter().map(|v| v / points.len() as f64).collect(),
   432→            base_point: mean.clone(),
   433→        };
   434→
   435→        // Step toward mean tangent
   436→        let step_size = 0.5;  // damping for convergence
   437→        let scaled = TangentVector {
   438→            components: mean_tangent.components.iter().map(|v| v * step_size).collect(),
   439→            base_point: mean.clone(),
   440→        };
   441→
   442→        mean = exponential_map(&mean, &scaled, manifold);
   443→    }
   444→
   445→    mean
   446→}
   447→```
   448→
   449→---
   450→
   451→## Spectral decomposition — Eigenvalue analysis
   452→
   453→The metric tensor's eigenvalues reveal the principal directions of cost and their magnitudes:
   454→
   455→```rust
   456→/// Spectral decomposition of the metric tensor.
   457→///
   458→/// Eigenvalues: the cost magnitude in each principal direction.
   459→///   Large eigenvalue → expensive to move in that direction.
   460→///   Small eigenvalue → cheap to move in that direction.
   461→///
   462→/// Eigenvectors: the principal directions.
   463→///   The cheapest direction is the eigenvector with smallest eigenvalue.
   464→///   The most expensive direction has the largest eigenvalue.
   465→///
   466→/// Condition number (λ_max / λ_min):
   467→///   High condition number → highly anisotropic cost structure.
   468→///   The market strongly favors some trades over others.
   469→pub struct SpectralDecomposition {
   470→    pub eigenvalues: Vec<f64>,
   471→    pub eigenvectors: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
   472→    pub condition_number: f64,
   473→}
   474→
   475→impl MetricTensor {
   476→    pub fn spectral_decomposition(&self) -> SpectralDecomposition {
   477→        let (eigenvalues, eigenvectors) = symmetric_eigendecomposition(&self.components);
   478→        let condition_number = eigenvalues.last().unwrap() / eigenvalues.first().unwrap();
   479→
   480→        SpectralDecomposition {
   481→            eigenvalues,
   482→            eigenvectors,
   483→            condition_number,
   484→        }
   485→    }
   486→}
   487→```
   488→
   489→---
   490→
   491→## Implementation details
   492→
   493→### Metric tensor computation: Hessian of price impact
   494→
   495→The metric tensor `g_ij` at a point is the Hessian of the total execution cost function with respect to portfolio state variables. Since analytical Hessians are unavailable for arbitrary pool types, the implementation uses central finite differences:
   496→
   497→```rust
   498→/// Compute the metric tensor via numerical differentiation.
   499→///
   500→/// Uses central finite differences on the execution cost function:
   501→///   g_ij = d²C / dx_i dx_j
   502→///        ≈ [C(x+ε_i+ε_j) - C(x+ε_i-ε_j) - C(x-ε_i+ε_j) + C(x-ε_i-ε_j)] / (4ε²)
   503→///
   504→/// The step size ε is adaptive: ε = max(|x_i| * relative_eps, absolute_eps).
   505→pub struct MetricTensorComputer {
   506→    /// Relative step size for finite differences.
   507→    pub relative_eps: f64,   // default: 1e-4
   508→    /// Absolute step size floor (prevents division by near-zero).
   509→    pub absolute_eps: f64,   // default: 1e-8
   510→    /// The execution cost function C(x) for a given pool configuration.
   511→    pub cost_fn: Box<dyn Fn(&[f64], &[PoolState]) -> f64 + Send + Sync>,
   512→}
   513→
   514→impl MetricTensorComputer {
   515→    /// Compute g_ij at a point using central finite differences.
   516→    pub fn compute(&self, point: &ManifoldPoint, pools: &[PoolState]) -> MetricTensor {
   517→        let dim = point.coordinates.len();
   518→        let mut g = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
   519→        let x = &point.coordinates;
   520→
   521→        for i in 0..dim {
   522→            let eps_i = (x[i].abs() * self.relative_eps).max(self.absolute_eps);
   523→            for j in i..dim {
   524→                let eps_j = (x[j].abs() * self.relative_eps).max(self.absolute_eps);
   525→
   526→                let mut x_pp = x.clone(); x_pp[i] += eps_i; x_pp[j] += eps_j;
   527→                let mut x_pm = x.clone(); x_pm[i] += eps_i; x_pm[j] -= eps_j;
   528→                let mut x_mp = x.clone(); x_mp[i] -= eps_i; x_mp[j] += eps_j;
   529→                let mut x_mm = x.clone(); x_mm[i] -= eps_i; x_mm[j] -= eps_j;
   530→
   531→                let c_pp = (self.cost_fn)(&x_pp, pools);
   532→                let c_pm = (self.cost_fn)(&x_pm, pools);
   533→                let c_mp = (self.cost_fn)(&x_mp, pools);
   534→                let c_mm = (self.cost_fn)(&x_mm, pools);
   535→
   536→                g[i][j] = (c_pp - c_pm - c_mp + c_mm) / (4.0 * eps_i * eps_j);
   537→                g[j][i] = g[i][j]; // symmetric
   538→            }
   539→        }
   540→
   541→        MetricTensor { components: g, dim }
   542→    }
   543→}
   544→```
   545→
   546→**Configuration parameters**:
   547→
   548→| Parameter | Default | Range | Notes |
   549→|---|---|---|---|
   550→| `relative_eps` | 1e-4 | 1e-6 - 1e-2 | Smaller = more accurate but noisier. 1e-4 balances accuracy and numerical noise for f64. |
   551→| `absolute_eps` | 1e-8 | 1e-12 - 1e-4 | Floor for coordinates near zero. |
   552→
   553→### Christoffel symbol finite difference parameters
   554→
   555→The Christoffel symbols require first derivatives of the metric tensor. These are also computed via central finite differences:
   556→
   557→```rust
   558→/// Compute ∂_l g_{ij} via central finite differences on the metric.
   559→///
   560→///   ∂_l g_{ij} ≈ [g_{ij}(x + ε_l) - g_{ij}(x - ε_l)] / (2ε_l)
   561→///
   562→/// This requires 2*dim metric tensor evaluations (each itself O(dim²) cost evaluations).
   563→/// Total cost: O(dim³) cost function evaluations per Christoffel computation.
   564→pub fn metric_derivatives(
   565→    computer: &MetricTensorComputer,
   566→    point: &ManifoldPoint,
   567→    pools: &[PoolState],
   568→) -> Vec<MetricTensor> {
   569→    let dim = point.coordinates.len();
   570→    let mut derivs = Vec::with_capacity(dim);
   571→
   572→    for l in 0..dim {
   573→        let eps_l = (point.coordinates[l].abs() * computer.relative_eps)
   574→            .max(computer.absolute_eps);
   575→
   576→        let mut x_plus = point.clone();
   577→        x_plus.coordinates[l] += eps_l;
   578→        let g_plus = computer.compute(&x_plus, pools);
   579→
   580→        let mut x_minus = point.clone();
   581→        x_minus.coordinates[l] -= eps_l;
   582→        let g_minus = computer.compute(&x_minus, pools);
   583→
   584→        let mut dg = vec![vec![0.0; dim]; dim];
   585→        for i in 0..dim {
   586→            for j in 0..dim {
   587→                dg[i][j] = (g_plus.components[i][j] - g_minus.components[i][j])
   588→                    / (2.0 * eps_l);
   589→            }
   590→        }
   591→
   592→        derivs.push(MetricTensor { components: dg, dim });
   593→    }
   594→
   595→    derivs
   596→}
   597→```
   598→
   599→The step size for Christoffel computation should match the metric tensor step size. Using a different scale introduces inconsistency between the metric and its derivatives.
   600→
   601→### Geodesic solver: dynamic step count and error tolerance
   602→
   603→The geodesic solver uses adaptive 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) with dynamic step count:
   604→
   605→```rust
   606→/// Adaptive geodesic solver with error-controlled step sizing.
   607→///
   608→/// Starts with `n_steps` uniform steps. After initial solve,
   609→/// estimates local truncation error by comparing RK4 with RK2.
   610→/// Doubles step count in regions where error exceeds tolerance.
   611→pub struct GeodesicSolverConfig {
   612→    /// Initial step count.
   613→    pub initial_n_steps: usize,     // default: 100
   614→    /// Maximum step count (prevents runaway refinement).
   615→    pub max_n_steps: usize,         // default: 10_000
   616→    /// Local truncation error tolerance per step.
   617→    pub error_tolerance: f64,       // default: 1e-6
   618→    /// Maximum geodesic parameter length (prevents infinite geodesics).
   619→    pub max_parameter: f64,         // default: 10.0
   620→    /// Singular point detection threshold (eigenvalue ratio).
   621→    pub singularity_threshold: f64, // default: 1e-10
   622→}
   623→
   624→impl GeodesicSolverConfig {
   625→    /// Detect singular points where the metric degenerates.
   626→    ///
   627→    /// A point is singular if the metric tensor's condition number
   628→    /// exceeds 1/singularity_threshold, or if any eigenvalue is
   629→    /// negative (the metric is no longer positive-definite).
   630→    pub fn is_singular(&self, metric: &MetricTensor) -> bool {
   631→        let spectral = metric.spectral_decomposition();
   632→        let min_eigenvalue = spectral.eigenvalues.first().copied().unwrap_or(0.0);
   633→        min_eigenvalue < self.singularity_threshold
   634→            || spectral.condition_number > 1.0 / self.singularity_threshold
   635→    }
   636→}
   637→```
   638→
   639→**Singular point handling**: When the solver encounters a singular point (degenerate metric), it:
   640→
   641→1. Halves the step size and retries.
   642→2. If still singular after 3 retries, records a `GeodesicIncomplete` result with the last valid point.
   643→3. Logs the singular location for manifold diagnostics.
   644→
   645→### Exponential and logarithmic map parameters
   646→
   647→```rust
   648→/// Exponential map configuration.
   649→pub struct ExpMapConfig {
   650→    /// Number of geodesic integration steps.
   651→    pub n_steps: usize,       // default: 100
   652→    /// Error tolerance for integration.
   653→    pub tolerance: f64,        // default: 1e-6
   654→}
   655→
   656→/// Logarithmic map configuration (shooting method).
   657→///
   658→/// The shooting method solves: find v such that exp_p(v) = q.
   659→/// It iterates by adjusting v based on the error exp_p(v) - q.
   660→pub struct LogMapConfig {
   661→    /// Maximum Newton iterations for the shooting method.
   662→    pub max_iterations: usize,     // default: 20
   663→    /// Convergence tolerance: ||exp_p(v) - q|| < tolerance.
   664→    pub convergence_tolerance: f64, // default: 1e-6
   665→    /// Line search parameters (backtracking Armijo).
   666→    pub armijo_c: f64,              // default: 1e-4
   667→    pub armijo_tau: f64,            // default: 0.5
   668→    /// Initial step size for Newton line search.
   669→    pub initial_step: f64,          // default: 1.0
   670→    /// Minimum step size before declaring failure.
   671→    pub min_step: f64,              // default: 1e-10
   672→}
   673→```
   674→
   675→The Newton line search in the logarithmic map uses backtracking Armijo conditions: accept a step if `f(x + alpha*d) <= f(x) + c*alpha*grad_f . d`, where `c = 1e-4` (sufficient decrease) and `alpha` is halved each backtrack attempt (`tau = 0.5`). Maximum backtracks: `ceil(log2(initial_step / min_step))`.
   676→
   677→### Ricci scalar thresholds for market fragility
   678→
   679→| Ricci scalar range | Interpretation | Agent response |
   680→|---|---|---|
   681→| R > 1.0 | Strongly self-correcting. Trades have predictable costs. | Execute normally. |
   682→| 0.0 < R <= 1.0 | Mildly stable. Some cost variation. | Execute with wider slippage tolerance. |
   683→| -0.5 <= R <= 0.0 | Neutral to mildly fragile. | Reduce position sizes by 50%. |
   684→| -2.0 <= R < -0.5 | Fragile. Small trades amplify. | Reduce position sizes by 80%. Alert Daimon (raise Arousal). |
   685→| R < -2.0 | Critically fragile. Market structure unstable. | Suppress all execution. Escalate to T2. |
   686→
   687→These thresholds are configurable per protocol. Concentrated liquidity AMMs (Uniswap V3) tend toward higher curvature magnitude than constant-product AMMs (Uniswap V2), so adjust accordingly.
   688→
   689→### Failure modes
   690→
   691→1. **Degenerate manifold**: The metric tensor has zero or negative eigenvalues. Cause: a liquidity pool is empty or nearly so. Mitigation: skip the degenerate dimension (project out the null eigenspace) or mark the pool as unavailable.
   692→
   693→2. **Disconnected components**: The manifold splits into disconnected regions (e.g., two isolated liquidity pools with no bridge). Geodesics between disconnected components do not exist. The solver returns `GeodesicIncomplete` with the reason `DisconnectedComponents`.
   694→
   695→3. **Numerical instability in Christoffel symbols**: When the metric changes rapidly (high curvature), finite differences amplify truncation error. Mitigation: reduce `eps` by 10x in high-curvature regions (detected when eigenvalue ratio > 100).
   696→
   697→4. **Ill-conditioned metric inverse**: Required for Christoffel computation. When condition number > 1e8, use pseudoinverse (SVD with eigenvalue floor at 1e-10).
   698→
   699→5. **Geodesic divergence**: RK4 integration can diverge near singular points. The adaptive solver detects divergence when `||velocity|| > 1e6` and terminates early.
   700→
   701→### Integration wiring
   702→
   703→The spectral liquidity manifold integrates into the chain oracle prediction pipeline:
   704→
   705→```
   706→ChainOracle::predict()
   707→  -> query on-chain pool states (via alloy provider)
   708→  -> construct ManifoldPoint from portfolio state
   709→  -> MetricTensorComputer::compute() at current point
   710→  -> SpectralDecomposition for eigenvalue analysis
   711→  -> ricci_scalar() for fragility assessment
   712→  -> if R > threshold: compute_geodesic() for optimal execution path
   713→  -> if R < threshold: suppress execution, raise Daimon arousal
   714→  -> encode manifold features as HDC vector (via DeFiCodebook)
   715→  -> emit as Engram to the witness pipeline
   716→```
   717→
   718→### Test criteria
   719→
   720→- **Metric symmetry**: `g[i][j] == g[j][i]` for all i, j (within f64 epsilon).
   721→- **Metric positive-definiteness**: All eigenvalues of a well-formed metric are positive.
   722→- **Geodesic consistency**: `exp_p(log_p(q)) == q` within convergence tolerance.
   723→- **Christoffel symmetry**: `Gamma[k][i][j] == Gamma[k][j][i]` (lower indices are symmetric).
   724→- **Ricci scalar sign**: For a known constant-product AMM with deep liquidity, R > 0. For a pool at 99% depletion, R < 0.
   725→- **Adaptive step refinement**: Halving error tolerance halves the integration error (4th-order convergence).
   726→- **Singular point detection**: A pool with zero liquidity triggers `is_singular() == true`.
   727→
   728→---
   729→
   730→## Academic foundations
   731→
   732→- Amari, S., & Nagaoka, H. (2000). *Methods of Information Geometry*. AMS/Oxford. — Riemannian geometry for statistical manifolds.
   733→- do Carmo, M. P. (1992). *Riemannian Geometry*. Birkhäuser. — Standard reference for geodesics, curvature, parallel transport.
   734→- Pennec, X. (2006). "Intrinsic Statistics on Riemannian Manifolds." *Journal of Mathematical Imaging and Vision*, 25(1), 127-154. — Fréchet mean and Karcher iteration.
   735→- Adams, R. P., & Stegle, O. (2012). "Gaussian Process Product Models." *ICML 2012*. — GP-based metric tensor estimation.
   736→- Bronstein, M. M., et al. (2017). "Geometric Deep Learning." *IEEE Signal Processing Magazine*, 34(6), 18-42. — Geometric methods for learning on manifolds.
   737→
   738→---
   739→
   740→## Cross-references
   741→
   742→- See [02-chain-oracles.md](./02-chain-oracles.md) for chain oracle integration

1→# Causal Microstructure Discovery
     2→
     3→> Correlation is not causation. The causal discovery subsystem uses Pearl's structural causal models, Granger causality, and interventional experiments (via mirage-rs simulation) to discover genuine causal relationships in structured domains.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [01-oracle-trait](./01-oracle-trait.md) for prediction integration, [06-hyperdimensional-ta](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) for pattern encoding
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/04-causal-microstructure-discovery.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Pearl's causal hierarchy in Roko
    15→
    16→Judea Pearl's causal hierarchy (Pearl, 2009, *Causality*) defines three levels of causal reasoning. Roko implements all three:
    17→
    18→| Level | Question | Roko implementation |
    19→|---|---|---|
    20→| **L1: Association** (seeing) | "What is the probability of Y given X?" | Standard TA indicators (correlation, regression) |
    21→| **L2: Intervention** (doing) | "What happens to Y if I do X?" | Mirage-rs simulation (do-operator) |
    22→| **L3: Counterfactual** (imagining) | "Would Y have occurred if X hadn't happened?" | Dreams counterfactual engine (REM phase) |
    23→
    24→Most TA systems operate exclusively at Level 1. They detect correlations — "when RSI drops below 30, price tends to rise." But correlation is not causation. The RSI drop and the price rise might both be caused by a third variable (e.g., a whale liquidation). Acting on correlation without understanding causation leads to fragile strategies that fail when the causal structure changes.
    25→
    26→Roko's causal discovery subsystem moves the agent up Pearl's hierarchy, enabling genuinely causal predictions that survive regime changes.
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Structural Causal Model (SCM)
    31→
    32→```rust
    33→/// A structural causal model (SCM) following Pearl's formalism.
    34→///
    35→/// An SCM is a tuple (U, V, F) where:
    36→/// - U: exogenous (external, unobserved) variables
    37→/// - V: endogenous (internal, observed) variables
    38→/// - F: structural equations V_i = f_i(Pa(V_i), U_i)
    39→///   where Pa(V_i) are the parents of V_i in the causal graph
    40→pub struct StructuralCausalModel {
    41→    /// Exogenous variables (external factors).
    42→    pub exogenous: Vec<Variable>,
    43→
    44→    /// Endogenous variables (observed state).
    45→    pub endogenous: Vec<Variable>,
    46→
    47→    /// Structural equations: each variable is a function of its parents.
    48→    pub equations: HashMap<VariableId, StructuralEquation>,
    49→
    50→    /// The causal DAG (directed acyclic graph).
    51→    pub graph: CausalGraph,
    52→}
    53→
    54→pub struct Variable {
    55→    pub id: VariableId,
    56→    pub name: String,
    57→    pub domain: VariableDomain,
    58→}
    59→
    60→pub enum VariableDomain {
    61→    Continuous { min: f64, max: f64 },
    62→    Discrete(Vec<String>),
    63→    Binary,
    64→}
    65→
    66→/// A structural equation: V_i = f(Pa(V_i), U_i).
    67→pub struct StructuralEquation {
    68→    /// The variable this equation defines.
    69→    pub target: VariableId,
    70→
    71→    /// Parent variables (causal inputs).
    72→    pub parents: Vec<VariableId>,
    73→
    74→    /// The functional form.
    75→    pub function: Box<dyn Fn(&HashMap<VariableId, f64>) -> f64 + Send + Sync>,
    76→
    77→    /// Exogenous noise distribution.
    78→    pub noise: NoiseDistribution,
    79→}
    80→```
    81→
    82→### The do-operator
    83→
    84→Pearl's do-operator `do(X = x)` intervenes on the model by setting variable X to value x and removing all incoming edges to X. This breaks the causal mechanism that normally determines X, allowing us to measure the pure causal effect of X on downstream variables:
    85→
    86→```rust
    87→/// Apply the do-operator to an SCM.
    88→///
    89→/// do(X = x):
    90→///   1. Set X = x (override its structural equation)
    91→///   2. Remove all incoming edges to X in the causal graph
    92→///   3. Propagate through remaining structural equations
    93→///
    94→/// Returns the distribution of downstream variables under intervention.
    95→pub fn do_intervention(
    96→    scm: &StructuralCausalModel,
    97→    variable: VariableId,
    98→    value: f64,
    99→) -> InterventionalDistribution {
   100→    // Create a modified SCM with X fixed
   101→    let mut modified = scm.clone();
   102→
   103→    // Replace X's equation with a constant
   104→    modified.equations.insert(variable, StructuralEquation {
   105→        target: variable,
   106→        parents: vec![],  // no parents — intervention severs incoming arrows
   107→        function: Box::new(move |_| value),
   108→        noise: NoiseDistribution::Constant(0.0),
   109→    });
   110→
   111→    // Remove incoming edges to X in the graph
   112→    modified.graph.remove_incoming_edges(variable);
   113→
   114→    // Propagate through the modified model
   115→    modified.propagate()
   116→}
   117→```
   118→
   119→---
   120→
   121→## Causal discovery algorithms
   122→
   123→### PC Algorithm (Spirtes, Glymour, Scheines, 2000)
   124→
   125→The PC algorithm discovers the causal graph structure from observational data:
   126→
   127→```rust
   128→/// The PC (Peter-Clark) algorithm for causal graph discovery.
   129→///
   130→/// Spirtes, Glymour, & Scheines (2000), "Causation, Prediction, and Search"
   131→///
   132→/// 1. Start with a complete undirected graph
   133→/// 2. Remove edges based on conditional independence tests
   134→/// 3. Orient edges based on v-structures (colliders)
   135→/// 4. Apply Meek's orientation rules
   136→///
   137→/// Output: a Partially Directed Acyclic Graph (PDAG)
   138→pub fn pc_algorithm(
   139→    data: &DataFrame,
   140→    alpha: f64,  // significance level for independence tests (typically 0.05)
   141→    max_conditioning_set: usize,
   142→) -> CausalGraph {
   143→    let variables: Vec<VariableId> = data.columns().collect();
   144→    let mut graph = CausalGraph::complete_undirected(&variables);
   145→
   146→    // Phase I: Edge removal via conditional independence
   147→    for conditioning_size in 0..=max_conditioning_set {
   148→        for (x, y) in graph.edges() {
   149→            let neighbors = graph.neighbors(x);
   150→            for conditioning_set in neighbors.combinations(conditioning_size) {
   151→                if conditional_independence_test(data, x, y, &conditioning_set, alpha) {
   152→                    graph.remove_edge(x, y);
   153→                    graph.add_separation_set(x, y, conditioning_set);
   154→                    break;
   155→                }
   156→            }
   157→        }
   158→    }
   159→
   160→    // Phase II: Orient v-structures
   161→    for (x, z) in graph.undirected_edges() {
   162→        for y in graph.common_neighbors(x, z) {
   163→            if !graph.separation_set(x, z).contains(&y) {
   164→                // x → y ← z is a v-structure (collider)
   165→                graph.orient(x, y);
   166→                graph.orient(z, y);
   167→            }
   168→        }
   169→    }
   170→
   171→    // Phase III: Meek's orientation rules
   172→    graph.apply_meek_rules();
   173→
   174→    graph
   175→}
   176→```
   177→
   178→### Granger causality with DeFi extensions
   179→
   180→Granger causality (Granger, 1969) tests whether past values of X help predict Y beyond Y's own past values. Four extensions adapt it to DeFi:
   181→
   182→```rust
   183→/// Granger causality test with DeFi-specific extensions.
   184→///
   185→/// Base test: does X_{t-k} Granger-cause Y_t?
   186→/// H0: past values of X add no predictive power for Y
   187→/// H1: past values of X improve Y prediction
   188→pub struct GrangerCausalityTest {
   189→    /// Maximum lag order to test.
   190→    pub max_lag: usize,
   191→    /// Significance level.
   192→    pub alpha: f64,
   193→}
   194→
   195→impl GrangerCausalityTest {
   196→    /// Extension 1: Block-aware Granger causality.
   197→    ///
   198→    /// Standard Granger assumes uniform time steps.
   199→    /// Blockchain data has variable block times and MEV-induced
   200→    /// ordering effects. This extension uses block number as the
   201→    /// time index and accounts for intra-block ordering.
   202→    pub fn block_aware(&self, x: &TimeSeries, y: &TimeSeries, blocks: &[Block]) -> GrangerResult;
   203→
   204→    /// Extension 2: Cross-protocol Granger causality.
   205→    ///
   206→    /// Tests whether events on Protocol A Granger-cause events on
   207→    /// Protocol B. Accounts for different time granularities across
   208→    /// protocols (Uniswap has per-swap data, Aave has per-block updates).
   209→    pub fn cross_protocol(&self, x: &ProtocolSeries, y: &ProtocolSeries) -> GrangerResult;
   210→
   211→    /// Extension 3: Multi-chain Granger causality.
   212→    ///
   213→    /// Tests whether events on Chain A Granger-cause events on Chain B.
   214→    /// Accounts for bridge latency and cross-chain message propagation.
   215→    pub fn multi_chain(&self, x: &ChainSeries, y: &ChainSeries) -> GrangerResult;
   216→
   217→    /// Extension 4: MEV-adjusted Granger causality.
   218→    ///
   219→    /// Removes MEV-induced spurious correlations (sandwich attacks
   220→    /// create artificial dependencies between transactions that are
   221→    /// not genuinely causal).
   222→    pub fn mev_adjusted(&self, x: &TimeSeries, y: &TimeSeries, mev_labels: &[bool]) -> GrangerResult;
   223→}
   224→```
   225→
   226→---
   227→
   228→## Interventional discovery via mirage-rs
   229→
   230→The deepest causal reasoning requires interventions — actively changing variables and observing effects. In the chain domain, mirage-rs enables this without risking real assets:
   231→
   232→```rust
   233→/// Interventional causal discovery using mirage-rs simulation.
   234→///
   235→/// The agent constructs causal hypotheses from observational data,
   236→/// then tests them by simulating interventions:
   237→///
   238→/// 1. Observe: "When pool TVL drops, gas spikes."
   239→/// 2. Hypothesize: "TVL drop → liquidation cascade → gas spike" (causal)
   240→///    vs. "Both caused by external event (whale movement)" (confounded)
   241→/// 3. Intervene: In mirage-rs, force TVL to drop while holding
   242→///    external factors constant.
   243→/// 4. Observe: If gas spikes in the simulation, the causal hypothesis
   244→///    is supported. If not, it's confounded.
   245→pub struct InterventionalDiscovery {
   246→    /// The simulation environment.
   247→    mirage: Arc<MirageSimulator>,
   248→
   249→    /// The current causal model.
   250→    scm: StructuralCausalModel,
   251→
   252→    /// Hypotheses to test.
   253→    hypotheses: Vec<CausalHypothesis>,
   254→}
   255→
   256→pub struct CausalHypothesis {
   257→    /// Hypothesized cause.
   258→    pub cause: VariableId,
   259→
   260→    /// Hypothesized effect.
   261→    pub effect: VariableId,
   262→
   263→    /// Hypothesized mechanism (intermediate variables).
   264→    pub mechanism: Vec<VariableId>,
   265→
   266→    /// Confidence in this hypothesis.
   267→    pub confidence: f64,
   268→
   269→    /// Test results (from interventional experiments).
   270→    pub test_results: Vec<InterventionResult>,
   271→}
   272→
   273→pub struct InterventionResult {
   274→    /// The intervention applied.
   275→    pub intervention: (VariableId, f64),
   276→
   277→    /// The observed effect.
   278→    pub observed_effect: f64,
   279→
   280→    /// The predicted effect (from the causal model).
   281→    pub predicted_effect: f64,
   282→
   283→    /// Whether the hypothesis was supported.
   284→    pub supported: bool,
   285→}
   286→
   287→impl InterventionalDiscovery {
   288→    /// Run an interventional experiment.
   289→    pub async fn test_hypothesis(
   290→        &self,
   291→        hypothesis: &CausalHypothesis,
   292→    ) -> InterventionResult {
   293→        // Fork the current chain state in mirage-rs
   294→        let fork = self.mirage.fork_current_state().await;
   295→
   296→        // Apply the intervention (set cause variable to a specific value)
   297→        fork.set_variable(hypothesis.cause, 0.5).await;  // e.g., reduce TVL by 50%
   298→
   299→        // Advance the simulation for the hypothesized propagation time
   300→        fork.advance_blocks(10).await;
   301→
   302→        // Observe the effect variable
   303→        let observed = fork.get_variable(hypothesis.effect).await;
   304→        let predicted = do_intervention(&self.scm, hypothesis.cause, 0.5)
   305→            .mean(hypothesis.effect);
   306→
   307→        InterventionResult {
   308→            intervention: (hypothesis.cause, 0.5),
   309→            observed_effect: observed,
   310→            predicted_effect: predicted,
   311→            supported: (observed - predicted).abs() < 0.1,
   312→        }
   313→    }
   314→}
   315→```
   316→
   317→### Coding domain causal discovery
   318→
   319→In the coding domain, interventional experiments use the workspace itself as the simulation environment:
   320→
   321→```rust
   322→/// Coding causal discovery: test whether code change X causes test failure Y.
   323→///
   324→/// Example hypothesis: "Modifying auth.rs causes security_tests to fail"
   325→///
   326→/// Interventional test:
   327→///   1. Create a workspace snapshot (git stash or worktree)
   328→///   2. Apply the change to auth.rs
   329→///   3. Run security_tests
   330→///   4. If they fail: hypothesis supported
   331→///   5. If they pass: hypothesis not supported (the failure was confounded)
   332→pub async fn test_coding_hypothesis(
   333→    hypothesis: &CodingCausalHypothesis,
   334→    workspace: &Workspace,
   335→) -> InterventionResult {
   336→    let snapshot = workspace.create_snapshot().await?;
   337→
   338→    // Apply the change (intervention)
   339→    workspace.apply_change(&hypothesis.change).await?;
   340→
   341→    // Run the tests (observe effect)
   342→    let test_result = workspace.run_tests(&hypothesis.affected_tests).await?;
   343→
   344→    // Restore snapshot
   345→    workspace.restore_snapshot(&snapshot).await?;
   346→
   347→    InterventionResult {
   348→        intervention: hypothesis.change.clone(),
   349→        observed_effect: test_result.pass_rate,
   350→        predicted_effect: hypothesis.predicted_pass_rate,
   351→        supported: (test_result.pass_rate - hypothesis.predicted_pass_rate).abs() < 0.1,
   352→    }
   353→}
   354→```
   355→
   356→---
   357→
   358→## Backdoor criterion — Controlling for confounders
   359→
   360→```rust
   361→/// The backdoor criterion (Pearl, 2009).
   362→///
   363→/// A set Z satisfies the backdoor criterion relative to (X, Y) if:
   364→/// 1. No node in Z is a descendant of X
   365→/// 2. Z blocks every path between X and Y that contains an arrow INTO X
   366→///
   367→/// If Z satisfies the backdoor criterion, the causal effect of X on Y
   368→/// can be computed from observational data:
   369→///
   370→/// P(Y | do(X)) = Σ_z P(Y | X, Z=z) × P(Z=z)
   371→///
   372→/// This is the "adjustment formula" — it converts observational
   373→/// probabilities into interventional ones without running experiments.
   374→pub fn backdoor_adjustment(
   375→    graph: &CausalGraph,
   376→    x: VariableId,
   377→    y: VariableId,
   378→    z: &[VariableId],
   379→    data: &DataFrame,
   380→) -> Option<f64> {
   381→    // Verify backdoor criterion
   382→    if !graph.satisfies_backdoor(x, y, z) {
   383→        return None;
   384→    }
   385→
   386→    // Compute adjustment formula
   387→    let mut causal_effect = 0.0;
   388→    for z_values in data.unique_values(z) {
   389→        let p_y_given_xz = data.conditional_probability(y, &[(x, 1.0)], z, &z_values);
   390→        let p_z = data.marginal_probability(z, &z_values);
   391→        causal_effect += p_y_given_xz * p_z;
   392→    }
   393→
   394→    Some(causal_effect)
   395→}
   396→```
   397→
   398→---
   399→
   400→## Dream-based counterfactual discovery
   401→
   402→During REM Dreams, the agent generates counterfactual scenarios using the causal model:
   403→
   404→```rust
   405→/// Counterfactual generation during REM phase.
   406→///
   407→/// "What would have happened if X had been different?"
   408→///
   409→/// Pearl's three-step counterfactual:
   410→/// 1. Abduction: Use evidence to determine exogenous variables U
   411→/// 2. Action: Modify the SCM with do(X = x')
   412→/// 3. Prediction: Propagate through modified model
   413→pub fn generate_counterfactual(
   414→    scm: &StructuralCausalModel,
   415→    evidence: &HashMap<VariableId, f64>,
   416→    intervention: (VariableId, f64),
   417→) -> HashMap<VariableId, f64> {
   418→    // Step 1: Abduction — infer exogenous variables from evidence
   419→    let exogenous = scm.abduct(evidence);
   420→
   421→    // Step 2: Action — apply intervention to modified model
   422→    let modified = scm.intervene(intervention.0, intervention.1);
   423→
   424→    // Step 3: Prediction — propagate with inferred exogenous values
   425→    modified.propagate_with_exogenous(&exogenous)
   426→}
   427→```
   428→
   429→Counterfactual discovery is unique to Level 3 of Pearl's hierarchy. No competitor agent framework operates at this level. Combined with HDC encoding, discovered causal relationships are stored as `CausalLink` knowledge entries in the Neuro subsystem:
   430→
   431→```rust
   432→// Store discovered causal link
   433→neuro.store(KnowledgeEntry {
   434→    kind: KnowledgeType::CausalLink,
   435→    content: format!(
   436→        "Causal link discovered: {} → {} (effect size: {:.3}, confidence: {:.2})",
   437→        cause_name, effect_name, effect_size, confidence
   438→    ),
   439→    hdc_vector: HdcVector::bind(&cause_hv, &effect_hv),  // HDC encoding
   440→    confidence,
   441→    tier: KnowledgeTier::Working,
   442→    ..Default::default()
   443→}).await?;
   444→```
   445→
   446→---
   447→
   448→## Implementation details
   449→
   450→### PC algorithm: conditional independence test
   451→
   452→The PC algorithm uses the **partial correlation test** as its conditional independence test. For continuous variables X, Y conditioned on set Z:
   453→
   454→```rust
   455→/// Conditional independence test via partial correlation.
   456→///
   457→/// Tests H0: X ⊥ Y | Z (X is independent of Y given Z).
   458→///
   459→/// Method: Compute partial correlation r_{XY|Z} from the
   460→/// correlation matrix using recursive formula (Baba et al., 2004).
   461→/// Convert to a test statistic via Fisher's z-transform:
   462→///   z = 0.5 * ln((1+r)/(1-r)) * sqrt(n - |Z| - 3)
   463→///
   464→/// Under H0, z ~ N(0,1). Reject H0 if |z| > z_{alpha/2}.
   465→pub fn conditional_independence_test(
   466→    data: &DataFrame,
   467→    x: VariableId,
   468→    y: VariableId,
   469→    z: &[VariableId],
   470→    alpha: f64,
   471→) -> bool {
   472→    let n = data.n_rows();
   473→    let r_xy_z = partial_correlation(data, x, y, z);
   474→    let z_stat = 0.5 * ((1.0 + r_xy_z) / (1.0 - r_xy_z)).ln()
   475→        * ((n as f64 - z.len() as f64 - 3.0).max(1.0)).sqrt();
   476→    let critical = normal_quantile(1.0 - alpha / 2.0); // two-sided
   477→    z_stat.abs() < critical // true = independent
   478→}
   479→```
   480→
   481→### Significance level adaptation
   482→
   483→The significance level `alpha` adapts based on the number of variables and available data:
   484→
   485→| n_variables | n_observations | Recommended alpha |
   486→|---|---|---|
   487→| < 10 | > 1000 | 0.05 (standard) |
   488→| 10 - 50 | > 1000 | 0.01 (Bonferroni-style correction) |
   489→| > 50 | > 1000 | 0.001 |
   490→| any | < 100 | 0.10 (relaxed, low power) |
   491→
   492→```rust
   493→/// Adapt alpha based on problem scale.
   494→///
   495→/// Uses Bonferroni-like correction: alpha_adj = base_alpha / n_tests_estimate.
   496→/// The n_tests estimate is O(p^2) where p = number of variables.
   497→pub fn adaptive_alpha(n_variables: usize, n_observations: usize, base_alpha: f64) -> f64 {
   498→    let n_tests = n_variables * (n_variables - 1) / 2; // upper bound on pairwise tests
   499→    let bonferroni = base_alpha / n_tests.max(1) as f64;
   500→    // Floor at 1e-6 to prevent test from never rejecting
   501→    let alpha = bonferroni.max(1e-6);
   502→    // Relax if sample size is too small for the correction
   503→    if n_observations < 10 * n_variables {
   504→        (alpha * 10.0).min(0.1)
   505→    } else {
   506→        alpha
   507→    }
   508→}
   509→```
   510→
   511→### Maximum conditioning set formula
   512→
   513→The PC algorithm tests conditional independence with conditioning sets of increasing size. The maximum conditioning set size controls the computational cost:
   514→
   515→```
   516→max_conditioning_set = min(
   517→    max_neighbors - 1,         // can't condition on more than the neighbor count
   518→    floor(log2(n_observations)) - 1,  // statistical power limit
   519→    user_max                   // user override (default: 5)
   520→)
   521→```
   522→
   523→**Rationale**: Conditioning on k variables requires estimating a (k+2)-dimensional distribution. With n observations, you need roughly `2^(k+2)` samples for reliable estimation. So `k < log2(n) - 2` is the practical limit.
   524→
   525→### Meek's orientation rules
   526→
   527→After v-structure orientation, Meek's four rules orient remaining undirected edges:
   528→
   529→```
   530→Rule 1 (Acyclicity): If X → Y — Z and X and Z are not adjacent, orient Y → Z.
   531→Rule 2 (Directed path): If X → Y → Z and X — Z, orient X → Z.
   532→Rule 3 (Two directed paths): If X — Y, X → Z, X → W, Y — Z, Y — W,
   533→        and Z and W are not adjacent, orient X → Y.
   534→Rule 4 (Transitive closure): If X — Y, Y → Z, X — Z, orient X → Z.
   535→```
   536→
   537→Apply rules repeatedly until no new orientations are produced. This converges in at most O(p^2) iterations where p is the number of variables.
   538→
   539→### Granger causality extensions
   540→
   541→#### Time alignment for cross-protocol tests
   542→
   543→DeFi protocols produce observations at different cadences. Before running Granger tests, time-align the series:
   544→
   545→```rust
   546→/// Align two time series to a common time grid.
   547→///
   548→/// Method: snap each observation to the nearest grid point.
   549→/// Grid spacing = max(cadence_x, cadence_y).
   550→/// Missing values are forward-filled (last observation carried forward).
   551→pub fn time_align(
   552→    x: &TimeSeries,
   553→    y: &TimeSeries,
   554→    grid_spacing: Duration,
   555→) -> (Vec<f64>, Vec<f64>) {
   556→    let start = x.start().min(y.start());
   557→    let end = x.end().max(y.end());
   558→    let n_points = ((end - start).as_secs_f64() / grid_spacing.as_secs_f64()).ceil() as usize;
   559→
   560→    let mut aligned_x = Vec::with_capacity(n_points);
   561→    let mut aligned_y = Vec::with_capacity(n_points);
   562→
   563→    for i in 0..n_points {
   564→        let t = start + grid_spacing * i as u32;
   565→        aligned_x.push(x.value_at_or_before(t).unwrap_or(f64::NAN));
   566→        aligned_y.push(y.value_at_or_before(t).unwrap_or(f64::NAN));
   567→    }
   568→
   569→    (aligned_x, aligned_y)
   570→}
   571→```
   572→
   573→#### MEV label generation
   574→
   575→The MEV-adjusted Granger test requires labels identifying which transactions are MEV-related:
   576→
   577→```rust
   578→/// Heuristic MEV labeling for transaction sequences.
   579→///
   580→/// Labels a transaction as MEV if any of:
   581→/// 1. It is part of a sandwich bundle (buy-victim-sell in same block).
   582→/// 2. It is a backrun (immediately follows a large swap in the same block).
   583→/// 3. It interacts with a known MEV relay (Flashbots, MEV-Boost builder).
   584→/// 4. Its gas price is >3x the block median (priority fee bidding).
   585→pub fn label_mev_transactions(txs: &[Transaction], block: &Block) -> Vec<bool> {
   586→    let median_gas = median_gas_price(txs);
   587→    txs.iter().map(|tx| {
   588→        is_sandwich_component(tx, txs)
   589→            || is_backrun(tx, txs)
   590→            || is_known_mev_relay(&tx.from)
   591→            || tx.gas_price > median_gas * 3.0
   592→    }).collect()
   593→}
   594→```
   595→
   596→#### Bridge latency model for multi-chain tests
   597→
   598→Cross-chain Granger tests must account for message propagation delay:
   599→
   600→| Bridge type | Typical latency | Model |
   601→|---|---|---|
   602→| Native bridge (L1 -> L2) | 1 - 15 minutes | Fixed lag = 10 minutes |
   603→| Third-party bridge (LayerZero, Wormhole) | 2 - 30 minutes | Fixed lag = 15 minutes |
   604→| Optimistic rollup -> L1 | 7 days (dispute period) | Fixed lag = 7 days |
   605→| ZK rollup -> L1 | 1 - 4 hours (proof generation) | Fixed lag = 2 hours |
   606→
   607→The Granger test lag order `max_lag` is set to `ceil(bridge_latency / grid_spacing) + 2` to account for the bridge latency plus a buffer.
   608→
   609→### Do-operator on code: supported change formats
   610→
   611→The coding-domain do-operator supports these intervention types:
   612→

   614→/// Supported code intervention formats for causal experiments.
   615→pub enum CodeIntervention {
   616→    /// Modify a function body.
   617→    FunctionBody {
   618→        file: PathBuf,
   619→        function_name: String,
   620→        new_body: String,
   621→    },
   622→    /// Add/remove a dependency.
   623→    DependencyChange {
   624→        crate_name: String,
   625→        action: DepAction, // Add, Remove, ChangeVersion
   626→    },
   627→    /// Modify a configuration value.
   628→    ConfigChange {
   629→        key: String,
   630→        old_value: String,
   631→        new_value: String,
   632→    },
   633→    /// Apply a diff patch.
   634→    Patch {
   635→        diff: String, // unified diff format
   636→    },
   637→}
   638→
   639→/// Observable timing model for coding experiments.
   640→///
   641→/// After applying a code change, measure these observables:
   642→pub struct CodingObservables {
   643→    pub compile_time_ms: u64,
   644→    pub test_pass_rate: f64,
   645→    pub test_duration_ms: u64,
   646→    pub clippy_warning_count: usize,
   647→    pub binary_size_bytes: u64,

   649→```
   650→
   651→**Snapshot/restore**: Uses `git stash` for lightweight snapshots. For heavier experiments (dependency changes), creates a temporary git worktree. Restore is `git stash pop` or worktree deletion.
   652→
   653→### Backdoor adjustment: handling high-cardinality Z
   654→
   655→When the conditioning set Z contains high-cardinality variables (many unique values), direct enumeration of Z values is infeasible. Two mitigation strategies:
   656→
   657→1. **Binning**: For continuous Z variables, bin into quantiles (default: 10 bins). This trades precision for tractability.
   658→
   659→2. **Propensity score**: Replace Z with a 1-dimensional propensity score `e(Z) = P(X=1|Z)`. The backdoor adjustment becomes `sum over e(Z) bins of P(Y|X, e(Z)) * P(e(Z))`.
   660→
   661→```rust
   662→/// Backdoor adjustment with propensity score dimensionality reduction.
   663→///
   664→/// When |Z| > max_cardinality, collapses Z into a propensity score.
   665→pub fn backdoor_adjustment_propensity(
   666→    graph: &CausalGraph,
   667→    x: VariableId,
   668→    y: VariableId,
   669→    z: &[VariableId],
   670→    data: &DataFrame,
   671→    max_cardinality: usize,  // default: 100
   672→    n_bins: usize,           // default: 10
   673→) -> Option<f64> {
   674→    if !graph.satisfies_backdoor(x, y, z) {
   675→        return None;
   676→    }
   677→
   678→    let z_cardinality: usize = z.iter()
   679→        .map(|v| data.n_unique(v))
   680→        .product();
   681→
   682→    if z_cardinality <= max_cardinality {
   683→        // Direct adjustment
   684→        return backdoor_adjustment(graph, x, y, z, data);
   685→    }
   686→
   687→    // Propensity score collapse
   688→    let propensity = logistic_regression(data, x, z);
   689→    let binned_propensity = quantile_bin(&propensity, n_bins);
   690→    backdoor_adjustment(graph, x, y, &[binned_propensity], data)
   691→}
   692→```
   693→
   694→### Adequacy detection for backdoor sets
   695→
   696→Automatically find a valid backdoor set (if one exists):
   697→
   698→```rust
   699→/// Find a minimal valid backdoor adjustment set.
   700→///
   701→/// Algorithm: start with all non-descendants of X. Test the backdoor
   702→/// criterion. If valid, greedily remove variables while maintaining
   703→/// validity. Returns None if no valid backdoor set exists.
   704→pub fn find_backdoor_set(
   705→    graph: &CausalGraph,
   706→    x: VariableId,
   707→    y: VariableId,
   708→) -> Option<Vec<VariableId>> {
   709→    let descendants_x = graph.descendants(x);
   710→    let candidates: Vec<VariableId> = graph.all_variables()
   711→        .filter(|v| *v != x && *v != y && !descendants_x.contains(v))
   712→        .collect();
   713→
   714→    // Start with full candidate set and prune
   715→    let mut z = candidates.clone();
   716→    if !graph.satisfies_backdoor(x, y, &z) {
   717→        return None; // no valid backdoor set exists
   718→    }
   719→
   720→    // Greedy minimization
   721→    for candidate in &candidates {
   722→        let reduced: Vec<_> = z.iter().filter(|v| *v != candidate).cloned().collect();
   723→        if graph.satisfies_backdoor(x, y, &reduced) {
   724→            z = reduced;
   725→        }
   726→    }
   727→
   728→    Some(z)
   729→}
   730→```
   731→
   732→### Intervention hypothesis confidence threshold
   733→
   734→Hypotheses are accepted when the observed effect matches the predicted effect within a tolerance:
   735→
   736→```
   737→|observed_effect - predicted_effect| < tolerance
   738→
   739→tolerance = max(0.1, 0.2 * |predicted_effect|)
   740→```
   741→
   742→The 0.1 absolute floor prevents rejection of hypotheses with small predicted effects due to noise. The 0.2 relative component accounts for model imprecision scaling with effect size.
   743→
   744→Confidence updates after each test:
   745→
   746→```
   747→if supported:
   748→    confidence = confidence + 0.1 * (1.0 - confidence)   // diminishing increase
   749→if not supported:
   750→    confidence = confidence * 0.7                         // 30% penalty
   751→```
   752→
   753→A hypothesis is promoted to "confirmed" at confidence >= 0.8 (requires ~5 supporting tests from a neutral prior of 0.5). It is demoted to "rejected" at confidence < 0.1 (requires ~3 consecutive failures from 0.5).
   754→
   755→### Test criteria
   756→
   757→- **PC algorithm on known DAG**: Given data generated from X -> Y -> Z, the PC algorithm recovers the correct structure.
   758→- **Conditional independence calibration**: On independent data, the test rejects at rate <= alpha.
   759→- **Granger test on known causal series**: When X(t) = X(t-1) + noise, Y(t) = 0.5*X(t-1) + Y(t-1) + noise, the test detects X -> Y.
   760→- **Do-operator correctness**: In a confounded model (X <- Z -> Y, X -> Y), `do(X)` gives a different result than conditioning on X.
   761→- **Backdoor adjustment**: On synthetic data with known causal effect, the adjusted estimate is within 10% of the true effect.
   762→- **Coding intervention round-trip**: After applying and restoring a CodeIntervention, the workspace is in its original state.
   763→- **MEV label accuracy**: On a set of labeled Flashbots bundles, the heuristic achieves >90% recall.

1→# Predictive Geometry and Resonant Pattern Ecosystems
     2→
     3→> Topological Data Analysis (TDA) extracts shape from time series. Persistence landscapes provide a Banach space for pattern comparison. Resonant patterns are living organisms with HDC genomes that compete for attention via VCG auction.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Specified
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Technical Analysis](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [06-hyperdimensional-ta](./06-hyperdimensional-ta.md) for HDC encoding, [08-adaptive-signal-metabolism](./08-adaptive-signal-metabolism.md) for evolutionary dynamics
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/05-predictive-geometry.md`, `bardo-backup/prd/23-ta/06-resonant-pattern-ecosystem.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Part I: Predictive Geometry via TDA
    15→
    16→### Why topology for time series
    17→
    18→Standard TA reduces time series to statistics (means, variances, correlations). These statistics capture numerical properties but miss **shape** — the topological structure of the data. A time series with two peaks and a valley has different topology than one with a gradual rise, even if both have the same mean and variance.
    19→
    20→Topological Data Analysis (TDA) extracts shape features that are:
    21→
    22→- **Coordinate-free**: invariant to scaling, translation, and monotone transformations
    23→- **Multi-scale**: captures structure at every resolution simultaneously
    24→- **Robust**: small perturbations in data produce small changes in topology
    25→- **Composable**: topological features from different domains can be compared
    26→
    27→### Persistence diagrams
    28→
    29→A persistence diagram tracks the birth and death of topological features (connected components, loops, voids) across a filtration of the data:
    30→
    31→```rust
    32→/// A persistence diagram: the set of (birth, death) pairs for
    33→/// topological features at a given dimension.
    34→///
    35→/// Each point (b, d) represents a feature that appears at scale b
    36→/// and disappears at scale d. Long-lived features (d - b is large)
    37→/// represent genuine structure. Short-lived features are noise.
    38→pub struct PersistenceDiagram {
    39→    /// The topological dimension (0 = components, 1 = loops, 2 = voids).
    40→    pub dimension: usize,
    41→
    42→    /// The (birth, death) pairs.
    43→    pub points: Vec<(f64, f64)>,
    44→}
    45→
    46→impl PersistenceDiagram {
    47→    /// Compute persistence from a time series using Rips filtration.
    48→    pub fn from_time_series(series: &[f64], dimension: usize) -> Self {
    49→        // Embed time series as point cloud using delay embedding
    50→        // (Takens' theorem guarantees topological equivalence)
    51→        let point_cloud = delay_embedding(series, embedding_dim: 3, delay: 1);
    52→
    53→        // Build Rips complex filtration
    54→        let filtration = rips_filtration(&point_cloud, max_scale: f64::MAX);
    55→
    56→        // Compute persistent homology
    57→        let diagram = compute_persistence(&filtration, dimension);
    58→
    59→        diagram
    60→    }
    61→
    62→    /// Lifetime of a feature: death - birth.
    63→    pub fn lifetimes(&self) -> Vec<f64> {
    64→        self.points.iter().map(|(b, d)| d - b).collect()
    65→    }
    66→
    67→    /// The persistence of the longest-lived feature.
    68→    pub fn max_persistence(&self) -> f64 {
    69→        self.lifetimes().iter().cloned().fold(0.0, f64::max)
    70→    }
    71→}
    72→```
    73→
    74→### Persistence landscapes (Bubenik, 2015)
    75→
    76→Persistence landscapes transform persistence diagrams into functions that live in a Banach space — enabling arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, scaling) on topological features:
    77→
    78→```rust
    79→/// A persistence landscape: a sequence of piecewise-linear functions
    80→/// derived from a persistence diagram.
    81→///
    82→/// Bubenik (2015): persistence landscapes form a Banach space,
    83→/// enabling statistical operations (mean, variance, hypothesis testing)
    84→/// on topological features.
    85→///
    86→/// Key property: the landscape is a FUNCTION, not a set of points.
    87→/// Functions can be added, subtracted, scaled, and integrated —
    88→/// operations that are not well-defined on persistence diagrams directly.
    89→pub struct PersistenceLandscape {
    90→    /// The landscape functions λ_k(t) for k = 1, 2, 3, ...
    91→    /// λ_1 is the outermost envelope, λ_2 the next, etc.
    92→    pub layers: Vec<PiecewiseLinearFunction>,
    93→
    94→    /// The dimension of the underlying persistence diagram.
    95→    pub dimension: usize,
    96→}
    97→
    98→pub struct PiecewiseLinearFunction {
    99→    /// Breakpoints (t_i, f(t_i)).
   100→    pub points: Vec<(f64, f64)>,
   101→}
   102→
   103→impl PersistenceLandscape {
   104→    /// Convert a persistence diagram to a landscape.
   105→    pub fn from_diagram(diagram: &PersistenceDiagram) -> Self {
   106→        let mut tent_functions: Vec<PiecewiseLinearFunction> = diagram.points.iter()
   107→            .map(|(b, d)| {
   108→                let mid = (b + d) / 2.0;
   109→                let height = (d - b) / 2.0;
   110→                PiecewiseLinearFunction {
   111→                    points: vec![(*b, 0.0), (mid, height), (*d, 0.0)],
   112→                }
   113→            })
   114→            .collect();
   115→
   116→        // Sort by peak height (descending) to get layers
   117→        tent_functions.sort_by(|a, b| {
   118→            b.max_value().partial_cmp(&a.max_value()).unwrap()
   119→        });
   120→
   121→        // Build layers by taking the k-th largest value at each t
   122→        let layers = build_layers(&tent_functions);
   123→
   124→        PersistenceLandscape { layers, dimension: diagram.dimension }
   125→    }
   126→
   127→    /// Add two landscapes (point-wise).
   128→    pub fn add(&self, other: &PersistenceLandscape) -> PersistenceLandscape {
   129→        // Point-wise addition of corresponding layers
   130→        let layers = self.layers.iter()
   131→            .zip_longest(other.layers.iter())
   132→            .map(|pair| match pair {
   133→                Both(a, b) => a.pointwise_add(b),
   134→                Left(a) => a.clone(),
   135→                Right(b) => b.clone(),
   136→            })
   137→            .collect();
   138→
   139→        PersistenceLandscape { layers, dimension: self.dimension }
   140→    }
   141→
   142→    /// Scale a landscape by a constant.
   143→    pub fn scale(&self, factor: f64) -> PersistenceLandscape {
   144→        let layers = self.layers.iter()
   145→            .map(|l| l.pointwise_scale(factor))
   146→            .collect();
   147→
   148→        PersistenceLandscape { layers, dimension: self.dimension }
   149→    }
   150→
   151→    /// L^p norm of the landscape (measure of total topological complexity).
   152→    pub fn lp_norm(&self, p: f64) -> f64 {
   153→        self.layers.iter()
   154→            .map(|l| l.lp_integral(p))
   155→            .sum::<f64>()
   156→            .powf(1.0 / p)
   157→    }
   158→}
   159→```
   160→
   161→### Topology-to-trajectory mapping
   162→
   163→The key application: use topological features to constrain trajectory predictions:
   164→
   165→```rust
   166→/// Map topological features to trajectory forecasts.
   167→///
   168→/// The persistence landscape provides topological constraints
   169→/// on future price/metric trajectories. For example:
   170→///
   171→/// β_0 (component count):
   172→///   - If the current time series has 2 connected components,
   173→///     any predicted trajectory must eventually reduce to 1
   174→///     (convergence) or increase to 3+ (divergence).
   175→///   - This constrains the set of possible futures.
   176→///
   177→/// β_1 (loop count):
   178→///   - If the time series has a persistent 1-cycle (loop),
   179→///     the predicted trajectory should account for periodic behavior.
   180→///
   181→/// The mapping uses kernel regression: given a topological feature,
   182→/// predict the trajectory parameters.
   183→pub struct TopologyToTrajectory {
   184→    /// Trained kernel regression model.
   185→    kernel: KernelRegression,
   186→
   187→    /// Historical (topology, trajectory) pairs for training.
   188→    training_data: Vec<(PersistenceLandscape, Vec<f64>)>,
   189→}
   190→
   191→impl TopologyToTrajectory {
   192→    /// Predict future trajectory from current topology.
   193→    pub fn predict(&self, current_topology: &PersistenceLandscape) -> TrajectoryPrediction {
   194→        let weights = self.kernel.compute_weights(current_topology);
   195→        let predicted = self.training_data.iter()
   196→            .zip(weights.iter())
   197→            .map(|((_, traj), w)| traj.iter().map(|v| v * w).collect::<Vec<_>>())
   198→            .fold(vec![0.0; self.training_data[0].1.len()], |acc, t| {
   199→                acc.iter().zip(t.iter()).map(|(a, b)| a + b).collect()
   200→            });
   201→
   202→        TrajectoryPrediction {
   203→            values: predicted,
   204→            topological_constraints: self.extract_constraints(current_topology),
   205→        }
   206→    }
   207→}
   208→
   209→pub struct TrajectoryPrediction {
   210→    /// Predicted future values.
   211→    pub values: Vec<f64>,
   212→
   213→    /// Topological constraints on the prediction.
   214→    pub topological_constraints: Vec<TopologicalConstraint>,
   215→}
   216→
   217→pub enum TopologicalConstraint {
   218→    /// β_0 constraint: the trajectory must have this many components.
   219→    ComponentCount { expected: usize, tolerance: usize },
   220→
   221→    /// β_1 constraint: the trajectory exhibits periodic behavior.
   222→    PeriodicBehavior { period_estimate: f64, confidence: f64 },
   223→
   224→    /// Persistence constraint: features with lifetime > threshold
   225→    /// will likely persist in the future.
   226→    FeaturePersistence { min_lifetime: f64, count: usize },
   227→}
   228→```
